     7|## 1. Company Model [DRAFT]
     8|
     9|A Company is a first-order object. One Paperclip instance runs multiple Companies. A Company does not have a standalone "goal" field — its direction is defined by its set of Initiatives (see Task Hierarchy Mapping).
    10|
    11|### Fields (Draft)
    12|
    13|| Field       | Type          | Notes                             |
    14|| ----------- | ------------- | --------------------------------- |
    15|| `id`        | uuid          | Primary key                       |
    16|| `name`      | string        | Company name                      |
    17|| `createdAt` | timestamp     |                                   |
    18|| `updatedAt` | timestamp     |                                   |
    19|
    20|### Board Governance [DRAFT]
    21|
    22|Every Company has a **Board** that governs high-impact decisions. The Board is the human oversight layer.
    23|
    24|**V1: Single human Board.** One human operator.
    25|
    26|#### Board Approval Gates (V1)
    27|
    28|- New Agent hires (creating new Agents)
    29|- CEO's initial strategic breakdown (CEO proposes, Board approves before execution begins)
    30|- [TBD: other governance-gated actions — goal changes, firing Agents?]
    31|
    32|#### Board Powers (Always Available)
    33|
    34|The Board has **unrestricted access** to the entire system at all times:
    35|
    36|- **Set and modify Company budgets** — the Board sets top-level token/LLM cost budgets
    37|- **Pause/resume any Agent** — stop an Agent's heartbeat immediately
    38|- **Pause/resume any work item** — pause a task, project, subtask tree, milestone. Paused items are not picked up by Agents.
    39|- **Full project management access** — create, edit, comment on, modify, delete, reassign any task/project/milestone through the UI
    40|- **Override any Agent decision** — reassign tasks, change priorities, modify descriptions
    41|- **Manually change any budget** at any level
    42|
    43|The Board is not just an approval gate — it's a live control surface. The human can intervene at any level at any time.
    44|
    45|#### Budget Delegation
    46|
    47|The Board sets Company-level budgets. The CEO can set budgets for Agents below them, and every manager Agent can do the same for their reports. How this cascading budget delegation works in practice is TBD, but the permission structure supports it. The Board can manually override any budget at any level.
    48|
    49|**Future governance models** (not V1):
    50|
    51|- Hiring budgets (auto-approve hires within $X/month)
    52|- Multi-member boards
    53|- Delegated authority (CEO can hire within limits)
    54|
    55|### Open Questions
    56|
    57|- External revenue/expense tracking — future plugin. Token/LLM cost budgeting is core.
    58|- Company-level settings and configuration?
    59|- Company lifecycle (pause, archive, delete)?
    60|- What governance-gated actions exist beyond hiring and CEO strategy approval?
    61|
    62|---
    63|
    64|## 2. Agent Model [DRAFT]
    65|
    66|Every employee is an agent. Agents are the workforce.
    67|
    68|### Agent Identity (Adapter-Level)
    69|
    70|Concepts like SOUL.md (identity/mission) and HEARTBEAT.md (loop definition) are **not part of the Paperclip protocol**. They are adapter-specific configurations. For example, an OpenClaw adapter might use SOUL.md and HEARTBEAT.md files. A Claude Code adapter might use CLAUDE.md. A bare Python script might use command-line args.
    71|
    72|Paperclip doesn't prescribe how an agent defines its identity or behavior. It provides the control plane; the adapter defines the agent's inner workings.
    73|
    74|### Agent Configuration [DRAFT]
    75|
    76|Each agent has an **adapter type** and an **adapter-specific configuration blob**. The adapter defines what config fields exist.
    77|
    78|#### Paperclip Protocol (What Paperclip Knows)
    79|
    80|At the protocol level, Paperclip tracks:
    81|
    82|- Agent identity (id, name, role, title)
    83|- Org position (who they report to, who reports to them)
    84|- Adapter type + adapter config
    85|- Status (active, paused, terminated)
    86|- Cost tracking data (if the agent reports it)
    87|
    88|#### Adapter Configuration (Agent-Specific)
    89|
    90|Each adapter type defines its own config schema. Examples:
    91|
    92|- **OpenClaw adapter**: SOUL.md content, HEARTBEAT.md content, OpenClaw-specific settings
    93|- **Process adapter**: command to run, environment variables, working directory
    94|- **HTTP adapter**: endpoint URL, auth headers, payload template
    95|
    96|#### Exportable Org Configs
    97|
    98|A key goal: **the entire org's agent configurations are exportable.** You can export a company's complete agent setup — every agent, their adapter configs, org structure — as a portable artifact. This enables:
    99|
   100|- Sharing company templates ("here's a pre-built marketing agency org")
   101|- Version controlling your company configuration
   102|- Duplicating/forking companies
   103|
   104|#### Context Delivery
   105|
   106|Configurable per agent. Two ends of the spectrum:
   107|
   108|- **Fat payload** — Paperclip bundles relevant context (current tasks, messages, company state, metrics) into the heartbeat invocation. Suited for simple/stateless agents that can't call back to Paperclip.
   109|- **Thin ping** — Heartbeat is just a wake-up signal. Agent calls Paperclip's API to fetch whatever context it needs. Suited for sophisticated agents that manage their own state.
   110|
   111|#### Minimum Contract
   112|
   113|The minimum requirement to be a Paperclip agent: **be callable.** That's it. Paperclip can invoke you via command or webhook. No requirement to report back — Paperclip infers basic status from process liveness when it can.
   114|
   115|#### Integration Levels
   116|
   117|Beyond the minimum, Paperclip provides progressively richer integration:
   118|
   119|1. **Callable** (minimum) — Paperclip can start you. That's the only contract.
   120|2. **Status reporting** — Agent reports back success/failure/in-progress after execution.
   121|3. **Fully instrumented** — Agent reports status, cost/token usage, task updates, and logs. Bidirectional integration with the control plane.
   122|
   123|Paperclip ships **default agents** that demonstrate full integration: progress tracking, cost instrumentation, and a **Paperclip skill** (a Claude Code skill for interacting with the Paperclip API) for task management. These serve as both useful defaults and reference implementations for adapter authors.
   124|
   125|#### Export Formats
   126|
   127|Two export modes:
   128|
   129|1. **Template export** (default) — structure only: agent definitions, org chart, adapter configs, role descriptions. Optionally includes a few seed tasks to help get started. This is the blueprint for spinning up a new company.
   130|2. **Snapshot export** — full state: structure + current tasks, progress, agent status. A complete picture you could restore or fork.
   131|
   132|The usual workflow: export a template, create a new company from it, add a couple initial tasks, go.
   133|
   134|---
   135|
   136|## 3. Org Structure [DRAFT]
   137|
   138|Hierarchical reporting structure. CEO at top, reports cascade down.
   139|
   140|### Agent Visibility
   141|
   142|**Full visibility across the org.** Every agent can see the entire org chart, all tasks, all agents. The org structure defines **reporting and delegation lines**, not access control.
   143|
   144|Each agent publishes a short description of their responsibilities and capabilities — almost like skills ("when I'm relevant"). This lets other agents discover who can help with what.
   145|
   146|### Cross-Team Work
   147|
   148|Agents can create tasks and assign them to agents outside their reporting line. This is the mechanism for cross-team collaboration. These rules are primarily encoded in the Paperclip SKILL.md which is recommended for all agents. Paperclip the app enforces the tooling and some light governance, but the cross-team rules below are mainly implemented by agent decisions.
   149|
   150|#### Task Acceptance Rules
   151|
   152|When an agent receives a task from outside their team:
   153|
   154|1. **Agrees it's appropriate + can do it** → complete it directly
   155|2. **Agrees it's appropriate + can't do it** → mark as blocked
   156|3. **Questions whether it's worth doing** → **cannot cancel it themselves.** Must reassign to their own manager, explain the situation. Manager decides whether to accept, reassign, or escalate.
   157|
   158|#### Manager Escalation Protocol
   159|
   160|It's any manager's responsibility to understand why their subordinates are blocked and resolve it:
   161|
   162|0. **Decide** — as a manager, is this work worth doing?
   163|1. **Delegate down** — ask someone under them to help unblock
   164|2. **Escalate up** — ask the manager above them for help
   165|
   166|#### Request Depth Tracking
   167|
   168|When a task originates from a cross-team request, track the **depth** as an integer — how many delegation hops from the original requester. This provides visibility into how far work cascades through the org.
   169|
   170|#### Billing Codes
   171|
   172|Tasks carry a **billing code** so that token spend during execution can be attributed upstream to the requesting task/agent. When Agent A asks Agent B to do work, the cost of B's work is tracked against A's request. This enables cost attribution across the org.
   173|
   174|### Open Questions
   175|
   176|- Is this a strict tree or can agents report to multiple managers?
   177|- Can org structure change at runtime? (agents reassigned, teams restructured)
   178|- Do agents inherit any configuration from their manager?
   179|- Billing code format — simple string? Hierarchical?
   180|
   181|---
   182|
   183|## 4. Heartbeat System [DRAFT]
   184|
   185|The heartbeat is a protocol, not a runtime. Paperclip defines how to initiate an agent's cycle. What the agent does with that cycle — how long it runs, whether it's task-scoped or continuous — is entirely up to the agent.
   186|
   187|### Execution Adapters
   188|
   189|Agent configuration includes an **adapter** that defines how Paperclip invokes the agent. Built-in adapters include:
   190|
   191|| Adapter | Mechanism | Example |
   192|| ---------------- | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
   193|| `process` | Execute a child process | `python run_agent.py --agent-id {id}` |
   194|| `http` | Send an HTTP request | `POST https://openclaw.example.com/hook/{id}` |
   195|| `claude_local` | Local Claude Code process | Claude Code heartbeat worker |
   196|| `codex_local` | Local Codex process | Codex CLI heartbeat worker |
   197|| `opencode_local` | Local OpenCode process | OpenCode heartbeat worker |
   198|| `pi_local` | Local Pi process | Pi CLI heartbeat worker |
   199|| `cursor` | Cursor API/CLI bridge | Cursor-integrated heartbeat worker |
   200|| `openclaw_gateway` | OpenClaw gateway API | Managed OpenClaw agent via gateway |
   201|| `hermes_local` | Local Hermes process | Hermes agent heartbeat worker |
   202|
   203|The `process` and `http` adapters ship as generic defaults. Additional built-in adapters cover common local coding runtimes (see list above), and new adapter types can be registered via the plugin system (see Plugin / Extension Architecture).
   204|
   205|### Adapter Interface
   206|
   207|Every adapter implements three methods:
   208|
   209|```
   210|invoke(agentConfig, context?) → void     // Start the agent's cycle
   211|status(agentConfig) → AgentStatus        // Is it running? finished? errored?
   212|cancel(agentConfig) → void               // Graceful stop signal (for pause/resume)
   213|```
   214|
   215|This is the full adapter contract. `invoke` starts the agent, `status` lets Paperclip check on it, `cancel` enables the board's pause functionality. Everything else (cost reporting, task updates) is optional and flows through the Paperclip REST API.
   216|
   217|### What Paperclip Controls
   218|
   219|- **When** to fire the heartbeat (schedule/frequency, per-agent)
   220|- **How** to fire it (adapter selection + config)
   221|- **What context** to include (thin ping vs. fat payload, per-agent)
   222|
   223|### What Paperclip Does NOT Control
   224|
   225|- How long the agent runs
   226|- What the agent does during its cycle
   227|- Whether the agent is task-scoped, time-windowed, or continuous
   228|
   229|### Pause Behavior
   230|
   231|When the board (or system) pauses an agent:
   232|
   233|1. **Signal the current execution** — send a graceful termination signal to the running process/session
   234|2. **Grace period** — give the agent time to wrap up, save state, report final status
   235|3. **Force-kill after timeout** — if the agent doesn't stop within the grace period, terminate
   236|4. **Stop future heartbeats** — no new heartbeat cycles will fire until the agent is resumed
   237|
   238|This is "graceful signal + stop future heartbeats." The current run gets a chance to land cleanly.
   239|
   240|### Open Questions
   241|
   242|- Heartbeat frequency — who controls it? Fixed? Per-agent? Cron-like?
   243|- What happens when a heartbeat invocation fails? (process crashes, HTTP 500)
   244|- Health monitoring — how does Paperclip distinguish "stuck" from "working on a long task"?
   245|- Can agents self-trigger their next heartbeat? ("I'm done, wake me again in 5 min")
   246|- Grace period duration — fixed? configurable per agent?
   247|
   248|---
   249|
   250|## 5. Inter-Agent Communication [DRAFT]
   251|
   252|All agent communication flows through the **task system**.
   253|
   254|### Model: Tasks + Comments
   255|
   256|- **Delegation** = creating a task and assigning it to another agent
   257|- **Coordination** = commenting on tasks
   258|- **Status updates** = updating task status and fields
   259|
   260|There is no separate messaging or chat system. Tasks are the communication channel. This keeps all context attached to the work it relates to and creates a natural audit trail.
   261|
   262|### Implications
   263|
   264|- An agent's "inbox" is: tasks assigned to them + comments on tasks they're involved in
   265|- The CEO delegates by creating tasks assigned to the CTO
   266|- The CTO breaks those down into sub-tasks assigned to engineers
   267|- Discussion happens in task comments, not a side channel
   268|- If an agent needs to escalate, they comment on the parent task or reassign
   269|
   270|### Task Hierarchy Mapping
   271|
   272|Full hierarchy: **Initiative** (company goal) → Projects → Milestones → Issues → Sub-issues. Everything traces back to an initiative, and the "company goal" is just the first/primary initiative.
   273|
   274|---
   275|
   276|## 6. Cost Tracking [DRAFT]
   277|
   278|Token/LLM cost budgeting is a core part of Paperclip. External revenue and expense tracking is a future plugin.
   279|
   280|### Cost Reporting
   281|
   282|Fully-instrumented Agents report token/API usage back to Paperclip. Costs are tracked at every level:
   283|
   284|- **Per Agent** — how much is this employee costing?
   285|- **Per task** — how much did this unit of work cost?
   286|- **Per project** — how much is this deliverable costing?
   287|- **Per Company** — total burn rate
   288|
   289|Costs should be denominated in both **tokens and dollars**.
   290|
   291|Billing codes on tasks (see Org Structure) enable cost attribution across teams — when Agent A requests work from Agent B, B's costs roll up to A's request.
   292|
   293|### Budget Controls
   294|
   295|Three tiers:
   296|
   297|1. **Visibility** — dashboards showing spend at every level (Agent, task, project, Company)
   298|2. **Soft alerts** — configurable thresholds (e.g. warn at 80% of budget)
   299|3. **Hard ceiling** — auto-pause the Agent when budget is hit. Board notified. Board can override/raise the limit.
   300|
   301|Budgets can be set to **unlimited** (no ceiling).
   302|
   303|### Open Questions
   304|
   305|- Cost reporting API — what's the schema for an agent to report costs?
   306|- Dashboard design — what metrics matter most at each level?
   307|- Budget period — per-day? per-week? per-month? rolling?
   308|
   309|---
   310|
   311|## 7. Default Agents & Bootstrap Flow [DRAFT]
   312|
   313|### Bootstrap Sequence
   314|
   315|How a Company goes from "created" to "running":
   316|
   317|1. Human creates a Company and its initial Initiatives
   318|2. Human defines initial top-level tasks
   319|3. Human creates the CEO Agent (using the default CEO template or custom)
   320|4. CEO's first heartbeat: reviews the Initiatives and tasks, proposes a strategic breakdown (org structure, sub-tasks, hiring plan)
   321|5. **Board approves** the CEO's strategic plan
   322|6. CEO begins execution — creating tasks, proposing hires (Board-approved), delegating
   323|
   324|### Default Agents
   325|
   326|Paperclip ships default Agent templates:
   327|
   328|- **Default Agent** — a basic Claude Code or Codex loop. Knows the **Paperclip Skill** (SKILL.md) so it can interact with the task system, read Company context, report status.
   329|- **Default CEO** — extends the Default Agent with CEO-specific behavior: strategic planning, delegation to reports, progress review, Board communication.
   330|
   331|These are starting points. Users can customize or replace them entirely.
   332|
   333|### Default Agent Behavior
   334|
   335|The default agent's loop is **config-driven**. The adapter config contains the instructions that define what the agent does on each heartbeat cycle. There is no hardcoded standard loop — each agent's config determines its behavior.
   336|
   337|This means the default CEO config tells the CEO to review strategy, check on reports, etc. The default engineer config tells the engineer to check assigned tasks, pick the highest priority, and work it. But these are config choices, not protocol requirements.
   338|
   339|### Paperclip Skill (SKILL.md)
   340|
   341|A skill definition that teaches agents how to interact with Paperclip. Provides:
   342|
   343|- Task CRUD (create, read, update, complete tasks)
   344|- Status reporting (check in, report progress)
   345|- Company context (read goal, org chart, current state)
   346|- Cost reporting (log token/API usage)
   347|- Inter-agent communication rules
   348|
   349|This skill is adapter-agnostic — it can be loaded into Claude Code, injected into prompts, or used as API documentation for custom agents.
   350|
   351|---
   352|
   353|## 8. Architecture & Deployment [DRAFT]
   354|
   355|### Deployment Model
   356|
   357|**Single-tenant, self-hostable.** Not a SaaS. One instance = one operator's companies.
   358|
   359|#### Development Path (Progressive Deployment)
   360|
   361|1. **Local dev** — One command to install and run. Embedded Postgres. Everything on your machine. Agents run locally.
   362|2. **Hosted** — Deploy to Vercel/Supabase/AWS/anywhere. Remote agents connect to your server with a shared database. The UI is accessible via the web.
   363|3. **Open company** — Optionally make parts public (e.g. a job board visible to the public for open companies).
   364|
   365|The key constraint: it must be trivial to go from "I'm trying this on my machine" to "my agents are running on remote servers talking to my Paperclip instance."
   366|
   367|#### Agent Authentication
   368|
   369|When a user creates an Agent, Paperclip generates a **connection string** containing: the server URL, an API key, and instructions for how to authenticate. The Agent is assumed to be capable of figuring out how to call the API with its token/key from there.
   370|
   371|Flow:
   372|
   373|1. Human creates an Agent in the UI
   374|2. Paperclip generates a connection string (URL + key + instructions)
   375|3. Human provides this string to the Agent (e.g. in its adapter config, environment, etc.)
   376|4. Agent uses the key to authenticate API calls to the control plane
   377|
   378|### Tech Stack
   379|
   380|| Layer    | Technology                                                   |
   381|| -------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
   382|| Frontend | React + Vite                                                 |
   383|| Backend  | TypeScript + Express (REST API, not tRPC — need non-TS clients) |
   384|| Database | PostgreSQL (see [doc/DATABASE.md](./doc/DATABASE.md) for details — PGlite embedded for dev, Docker or hosted Supabase for production) |
   385|| Auth     | [Better Auth](https://www.better-auth.com/)                  |
   386|
   387|### Concurrency Model: Atomic Task Checkout
   388|
   389|Tasks use **single assignment** (one agent per task) with **atomic checkout**:
   390|
   391|1. Agent attempts to set a task to `in_progress` (claiming it)
   392|2. The API/database enforces this atomically — if another agent already claimed it, the request fails with an error identifying which agent has it
   393|3. If the task is already assigned to the requesting agent from a previous session, they can resume
   394|
   395|No optimistic locking or CRDTs needed. The single-assignment model + atomic checkout prevents conflicts at the design level.
   396|
   397|### Human in the Loop
   398|
   399|Agents can create tasks assigned to humans. The board member (or any human with access) can complete these tasks through the UI.
   400|
   401|When a human completes a task, if the requesting agent's adapter supports **pingbacks** (e.g. OpenClaw hooks), Paperclip sends a notification to wake that agent. This keeps humans rare but possible participants in the workflow.
   402|
   403|The agents are discouraged from assigning tasks to humans in the Paperclip SKILL, but sometimes it's unavoidable.
   404|
   405|### API Design
   406|
   407|**Single unified REST API.** The same API serves both the frontend UI and agents. Authentication determines permissions — board auth has full access, agent API keys have scoped access (their own tasks, cost reporting, company context).
   408|
   409|No separate "agent API" vs. "board API." Same endpoints, different authorization levels.
   410|
   411|### Work Artifacts
   412|
   413|Paperclip manages task-linked work artifacts: issue documents (rich-text plans, specs, notes attached to issues) and file attachments. Agents read and write these through the API as part of normal task execution. Full delivery infrastructure (code repos, deployments, production runtime) remains the agent's domain — Paperclip orchestrates the work, not the build pipeline.
   414|
   415|### Open Questions
   416|
   417|- Real-time updates to the UI — WebSocket? SSE? Polling?
   418|- Agent API key scoping — what exactly can an Agent access? Only their own tasks? Their team's? The whole Company?
   419|
   420|### Crash Recovery: Manual, Not Automatic
   421|
   422|When an agent crashes or disappears mid-task, Paperclip does **not** auto-reassign or auto-release the task. Instead:
   423|
   424|- Paperclip surfaces stale tasks (tasks in `in_progress` with no recent activity) through dashboards and reporting
   425|- Paperclip does not fail silently — the auditing and visibility tools make problems obvious
   426|- Recovery is handled by humans or by emergent processes (e.g. a project manager agent whose job is to monitor for stale work and surface it)
   427|
   428|**Principle: Paperclip reports problems, it doesn't silently fix them.** Automatic recovery hides failures. Good visibility lets the right entity (human or agent) decide what to do.
   429|
   430|### Plugin / Extension Architecture
   431|
   432|The core Paperclip system must be extensible. Features like knowledge bases, external revenue tracking, and new Agent Adapters should be addable as **plugins** without modifying core. This means:
   433|
   434|- Well-defined API boundaries that plugins can hook into
   435|- Event system or hooks for reacting to task/Agent lifecycle events
   436|- **Agent Adapter plugins** — new Adapter types can be registered via the plugin system
   437|- Plugin-registrable UI components (future)
   438|
   439|The plugin framework has shipped. Plugins can register new adapter types, hook into lifecycle events, and contribute UI components (e.g. global toolbar buttons). A plugin SDK and CLI commands (`paperclipai plugin`) are available for authoring and installing plugins.
   440|
   441|---
   442|
   443|## 9. Frontend / UI [DRAFT]
   444|
   445|### Primary Views
   446|
   447|Each is a distinct page/route:
   448|
   449|1. **Org Chart** — the org tree with live status indicators (running/idle/paused/error) per agent. Real-time activity feed of what agents are doing.
   450|2. **Task Board** — Task management. Kanban and list views. Filter by team, agent, project, status.
   451|3. **Dashboard** — high-level metrics: agent count, active tasks, costs, goal progress, burn rate. The "glance" view from GOAL.md.
   452|4. **Agent Detail** — deep dive on a single agent: their tasks, activity, costs, configuration, status history.
   453|5. **Project/Initiative Views** — progress tracking against milestones and goals.
   454|6. **Cost Dashboard** — spend visualization at every level (agent, task, project, company).
   455|
   456|### Board Controls (Available Everywhere)
   457|
   458|- Pause/resume agents (any view)
   459|- Pause/resume tasks/projects (any view)
   460|- Approve/reject pending actions (hiring, strategy proposals)
   461|- Direct task creation, editing, commenting
   462|
   463|---
   464|
   465|## 10. V1 Scope (MVP) [DRAFT]
   466|
   467|**Full loop with one adapter.** V1 must demonstrate the complete Paperclip cycle end-to-end, even if narrow.
   468|
   469|### Must Have (V1)
   470|
   471|- [ ] **Company CRUD** — create a Company with Initiatives
   472|- [ ] **Agent CRUD** — create/edit/pause/resume Agents with Adapter config
   473|- [ ] **Org chart** — define reporting structure, visualize it
   474|- [ ] **Process adapter** — invoke(), status(), cancel() for local child processes
   475|- [ ] **Task management** — full lifecycle with hierarchy (tasks trace to company goal)
   476|- [ ] **Atomic task checkout** — single assignment, in_progress locking
   477|- [ ] **Board governance** — human approves hires, pauses Agents, sets budgets, full PM access
   478|- [ ] **Cost tracking** — Agents report token usage, per-Agent/task/Company visibility
   479|- [ ] **Budget controls** — soft alerts + hard ceiling with auto-pause
   480|- [ ] **Default agent** — basic Claude Code/Codex loop with Paperclip skill
   481|- [ ] **Default CEO** — strategic planning, delegation, board communication
   482|- [ ] **Paperclip skill (SKILL.md)** — teaches agents to interact with the API
   483|- [ ] **REST API** — full API for agent interaction (Express)
   484|- [ ] **Web UI** — React/Vite: org chart, task board, dashboard, cost views
   485|- [ ] **Agent auth** — connection string generation with URL + key + instructions
   486|- [ ] **One-command dev setup** — embedded PGlite, everything local
   487|- [ ] **Multiple Adapter types** (HTTP, OpenClaw gateway, and local coding adapters)
   488|
   489|### Not V1
   490|
   491|- Knowledge base - a future plugin
   492|- Advanced governance models (hiring budgets, multi-member boards)
   493|- Revenue/expense tracking beyond token costs - a future plugin
   494|- Public job board / open company features
   495|
   496|---
   497|
   498|## 11. Knowledge Base
   499|
   500|**Anti-goal for core.** The knowledge base is not part of the Paperclip core — it will be a plugin. The task system + comments + agent descriptions provide sufficient shared context.
   501|
   502|The architecture must support adding a knowledge base plugin later (clean API boundaries, hookable lifecycle events) but the core system explicitly does not include one.
   503|
   504|---
   505|
   506|## 12. Anti-Requirements
   507|
   508|Things Paperclip explicitly does **not** do:
   509|
   510|- **Not an Agent runtime** — Paperclip orchestrates, Agents run elsewhere
   511|- **Not a knowledge base** — core has no wiki/docs/vector-DB (plugin territory)
   512|- **Not a SaaS** — single-tenant, self-hosted
   513|- **Not opinionated about Agent implementation** — any language, any framework, any runtime
   514|- **Not automatically self-healing** — surfaces problems, doesn't silently fix them
   515|- **Does not manage delivery infrastructure** — no repo management, no deployment, no file systems (but does manage task-linked documents and attachments)
   516|- **Does not auto-reassign work** — stale tasks are surfaced, not silently redistributed
   517|- **Does not track external revenue/expenses** — that's a future plugin. Token/LLM cost budgeting is core.
   518|
   519|---
   520|
   521|## 13. Principles (Consolidated)
   522|
   523|1. **Unopinionated about how you run your Agents.** Any language, any framework, any runtime. Paperclip is the control plane, not the execution plane.
   524|2. **Company is the unit of organization.** Everything lives under a Company.
   525|3. **Tasks are the communication channel.** All Agent communication flows through tasks + comments. No side channels.
   526|4. **All work traces to the goal.** Hierarchical task management — nothing exists in isolation.
   527|5. **Board governs.** Humans retain control through the Board. Conservative defaults (human approval required).
   528|6. **Surface problems, don't hide them.** Good auditing and visibility. No silent auto-recovery.
   529|7. **Atomic ownership.** Single assignee per task. Atomic checkout prevents conflicts.
   530|8. **Progressive deployment.** Trivial to start local, straightforward to scale to hosted.
   531|9. **Extensible core.** Clean boundaries so plugins can add capabilities (Adapters, knowledge base, revenue tracking) without modifying core.

{"content": "     1|# Docker Quickstart
     2|
     3|Run Paperclip in Docker without installing Node or pnpm locally.
     4|
     5|All commands below assume you are in the **project root** (the directory containing `package.json`), not inside `docker/`.
     6|
     7|## Building the image
     8|
     9|```sh
    10|docker build -t paperclip-local .
    11|```
    12|
    13|The Dockerfile installs common agent tools (`git`, `gh`, `curl`, `wget`, `ripgrep`, `python3`) and the Claude, Codex, and OpenCode CLIs.
    14|
    15|Build arguments:
    16|
    17|| Arg | Default | Purpose |
    18||-----|---------|---------|
    19|| `USER_UID` | `1000` | UID for the container `node` user (match your host UID to avoid permission issues on bind mounts) |
    20|| `USER_GID` | `1000` | GID for the container `node` group |
    21|
    22|```sh
    23|docker build -t paperclip-local \
    24|  --build-arg USER_UID=$(id -u) --build-arg USER_GID=$(id -g) .
    25|```
    26|
    27|## One-liner (build + run)
    28|
    29|```sh
    30|docker build -t paperclip-local . && \
    31|docker run --name paperclip \
    32|  -p 3100:3100 \
    33|  -e HOST=0.0.0.0 \
    34|  -e PAPERCLIP_HOME=/paperclip \
    35|  -e BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=*** rand -hex 32) \
    36|  -v "$(pwd)/data/docker-paperclip:/paperclip" \
    37|  paperclip-local
    38|```
    39|
    40|Open: `http://localhost:3100`
    41|
    42|Data persistence:
    43|
    44|- Embedded PostgreSQL data
    45|- uploaded assets
    46|- local secrets key
    47|- local agent workspace data
    48|
    49|All persisted under your bind mount (`./data/docker-paperclip` in the example above).
    50|
    51|## Docker Compose
    52|
    53|### Quickstart (embedded SQLite)
    54|
    55|Single container, no external database. Data persists via a bind mount.
    56|
    57|```sh
    58|BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=*** rand -hex 32) \
    59|  docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.quickstart.yml up --build
    60|```
    61|
    62|Defaults:
    63|
    64|- host port: `3100`
    65|- persistent data dir: `./data/docker-paperclip`
    66|
    67|Optional overrides:
    68|
    69|```sh
    70|PAPERCLIP_PORT=3200 PAPERCLIP_DATA_DIR=../data/pc \
    71|  docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.quickstart.yml up --build
    72|```
    73|
    74|**Note:** `PAPERCLIP_DATA_DIR` is resolved relative to the compose file (`docker/`), so `../data/pc` maps to `data/pc` in the project root.
    75|
    76|If you change host port or use a non-local domain, set `PAPERCLIP_PUBLIC_URL` to the external URL you will use in browser/auth flows.
    77|
    78|Pass `OPENAI_API_KEY` and/or `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` to enable local adapter runs.
    79|
    80|### Full stack (with PostgreSQL)
    81|
    82|Paperclip server + PostgreSQL 17. The database is health-checked before the server starts.
    83|
    84|```sh
    85|BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=*** rand -hex 32) \
    86|  docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml up --build
    87|```
    88|
    89|PostgreSQL data persists in a named Docker volume (`pgdata`). Paperclip data persists in `paperclip-data`.
    90|
    91|### Untrusted PR review
    92|
    93|Isolated container for reviewing untrusted pull requests with Codex or Claude, without exposing your host machine. See `doc/UNTRUSTED-PR-REVIEW.md` for the full workflow.
    94|
    95|```sh
    96|docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.untrusted-review.yml build
    97|docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.untrusted-review.yml run --rm --service-ports review
    98|```
    99|
   100|## Authenticated Compose (Single Public URL)
   101|
   102|For authenticated deployments, set one canonical public URL and let Paperclip derive auth/callback defaults:
   103|
   104|```yaml
   105|services:
   106|  paperclip:
   107|    environment:
   108|      PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_MODE: authenticated
   109|      PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_EXPOSURE: private
   110|      PAPERCLIP_PUBLIC_URL: https://desk.koker.net
   111|```
   112|
   113|`PAPERCLIP_PUBLIC_URL` is used as the primary source for:
   114|
   115|- auth public base URL
   116|- Better Auth base URL defaults
   117|- bootstrap invite URL defaults
   118|- hostname allowlist defaults (hostname extracted from URL)
   119|
   120|Granular overrides remain available if needed (`PAPERCLIP_AUTH_PUBLIC_BASE_URL`, `BETTER_AUTH_URL`, `BETTER_AUTH_TRUSTED_ORIGINS`, `PAPERCLIP_ALLOWED_HOSTNAMES`).
   121|
   122|Set `PAPERCLIP_ALLOWED_HOSTNAMES` explicitly only when you need additional hostnames beyond the public URL host (for example Tailscale/LAN aliases or multiple private hostnames).
   123|
   124|## Claude + Codex Local Adapters in Docker
   125|
   126|The image pre-installs:
   127|
   128|- `claude` (Anthropic Claude Code CLI)
   129|- `codex` (OpenAI Codex CLI)
   130|
   131|If you want local adapter runs inside the container, pass API keys when starting the container:
   132|
   133|```sh
   134|docker run --name paperclip \
   135|  -p 3100:3100 \
   136|  -e HOST=0.0.0.0 \
   137|  -e PAPERCLIP_HOME=/paperclip \
   138|  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=*** \
   139|  -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=*** \
   140|  -v "$(pwd)/data/docker-paperclip:/paperclip" \
   141|  paperclip-local
   142|```
   143|
   144|Notes:
   145|
   146|- Without API keys, the app still runs normally.
   147|- Adapter environment checks in Paperclip will surface missing auth/CLI prerequisites.
   148|
   149|## Podman Quadlet (systemd)
   150|
   151|The `docker/quadlet/` directory contains unit files to run Paperclip + PostgreSQL as systemd services via Podman Quadlet.
   152|
   153|| File | Purpose |
   154||------|---------|
   155|| `docker/quadlet/paperclip.pod` | Pod definition — groups containers into a shared network namespace |
   156|| `docker/quadlet/paperclip.container` | Paperclip server — joins the pod, connects to Postgres at `127.0.0.1` |
   157|| `docker/quadlet/paperclip-db.container` | PostgreSQL 17 — joins the pod, health-checked |
   158|
   159|### Setup
   160|
   161|1. Build the image (see above).
   162|
   163|2. Copy quadlet files to your systemd directory:
   164|
   165|   ```sh
   166|   # Rootless (recommended)
   167|   cp docker/quadlet/*.pod docker/quadlet/*.container \
   168|     ~/.config/containers/systemd/
   169|
   170|   # Or rootful
   171|   sudo cp docker/quadlet/*.pod docker/quadlet/*.container \
   172|     /etc/containers/systemd/
   173|   ```
   174|
   175|3. Create a secrets env file (keep out of version control):
   176|
   177|   ```sh
   178|   cat > ~/.config/containers/systemd/paperclip.env <<EOL
   179|   BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=*** rand -hex 32)
   180|   POSTGRES_USER=paperclip
   181|   POSTGRES_PASSWORD=***
   182|   POSTGRES_DB=paperclip
   183|   DATABASE_URL=postgres://paperclip:***@127.0.0.1:5432/paperclip
   184|   # OPENAI_API_KEY=***
   185|   # ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=***
   186|   EOL
   187|   ```
   188|
   189|4. Create the data directory and start:
   190|
   191|   ```sh
   192|   mkdir -p ~/.local/share/paperclip
   193|   systemctl --user daemon-reload
   194|   systemctl --user start paperclip-pod
   195|   ```
   196|
   197|### Quadlet management
   198|
   199|```sh
   200|journalctl --user -u paperclip -f        # App logs
   201|journalctl --user -u paperclip-db -f     # DB logs
   202|systemctl --user status paperclip-pod    # Pod status
   203|systemctl --user restart paperclip-pod   # Restart all
   204|systemctl --user stop paperclip-pod      # Stop all
   205|```
   206|
   207|### Quadlet notes
   208|
   209|- **First boot**: Unlike Docker Compose's `condition: service_healthy`, Quadlet's `After=` only waits for the DB unit to *start*, not for PostgreSQL to be ready. On a cold first boot you may see one or two restart attempts in `journalctl --user -u paperclip` while PostgreSQL initialises — this is expected and resolves automatically via `Restart=on-failure`.
   210|- Containers in a pod share `localhost`, so Paperclip reaches Postgres at `127.0.0.1:5432`.
   211|- PostgreSQL data persists in the `paperclip-pgdata` named volume.
   212|- Paperclip data persists at `~/.local/share/paperclip`.
   213|- For rootful quadlet deployment, remove `%h` prefixes and use absolute paths.
   214|
   215|## Onboard Smoke Test (Ubuntu + npm only)
   216|
   217|Use this when you want to mimic a fresh machine that only has Ubuntu + npm and verify:
   218|
   219|- `npx paperclipai onboard --yes` completes
   220|- the server binds to `0.0.0.0:3100` so host access works
   221|- onboard/run banners and startup logs are visible in your terminal
   222|
   223|Build + run:
   224|
   225|```sh
   226|./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   227|```
   228|
   229|Open: `http://localhost:3131` (default smoke host port)
   230|
   231|Useful overrides:
   232|
   233|```sh
   234|HOST_PORT=3200 PAPERCLIPAI_VERSION=latest ./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   235|PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_MODE=authenticated PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_EXPOSURE=private ./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   236|SMOKE_DETACH=true SMOKE_METADATA_FILE=/tmp/paperclip-smoke.env PAPERCLIPAI_VERSION=latest ./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   237|```
   238|
   239|Notes:
   240|
   241|- Persistent data is mounted at `./data/docker-onboard-smoke` by default.
   242|- Container runtime user id defaults to your local `id -u` so the mounted data dir stays writable while avoiding root runtime.
   243|- Smoke script defaults to `authenticated/private` mode so `HOST=0.0.0.0` can be exposed to the host.
   244|- Smoke script defaults host port to `3131` to avoid conflicts with local Paperclip on `3100`.
   245|- Smoke script also defaults `PAPERCLIP_PUBLIC_URL` to `http://localhost:<HOST_PORT>` so bootstrap invite URLs and auth callbacks use the reachable host port instead of the container's internal `3100`.
   246|- In authenticated mode, the smoke script defaults `SMOKE_AUTO_BOOTSTRAP=true` and drives the real bootstrap path automatically: it signs up a real user, runs `paperclipai auth bootstrap-ceo` inside the container to mint a real bootstrap invite, accepts that invite over HTTP, and verifies board session access.
   247|- Run the script in the foreground to watch the onboarding flow; stop with `Ctrl+C` after validation.
   248|- Set `SMOKE_DETACH=true` to leave the container running for automation and optionally write shell-ready metadata to `SMOKE_METADATA_FILE`.
   249|- The image definition is in `docker/Dockerfile.onboard-smoke`.
   250|
   251|## General Notes
   252|
   253|- The `docker-entrypoint.sh` adjusts the container `node` user UID/GID at startup to match the values passed via `USER_UID`/`USER_GID`, avoiding permission issues on bind-mounted volumes.
   254|- Paperclip data persists via Docker volumes/bind mounts (compose) or at `~/.local/share/paperclip` (quadlet).
   255|", "total_lines": 254, "file_size": 9077, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Deployment Modes
     2|
     3|Status: Canonical deployment and auth mode model  
     4|Date: 2026-02-23
     5|
     6|## 1. Purpose
     7|
     8|Paperclip supports two runtime modes:
     9|
    10|1. `local_trusted`
    11|2. `authenticated`
    12|
    13|`authenticated` supports two exposure policies:
    14|
    15|1. `private`
    16|2. `public`
    17|
    18|This keeps one authenticated auth stack while still separating low-friction private-network defaults from internet-facing hardening requirements.
    19|
    20|Paperclip now treats **bind** as a separate concern from auth:
    21|
    22|- auth model: `local_trusted` vs `authenticated`, plus `private/public`
    23|- reachability model: `server.bind = loopback | lan | tailnet | custom`
    24|
    25|## 2. Canonical Model
    26|
    27|| Runtime Mode | Exposure | Human auth | Primary use |
    28||---|---|---|---|
    29|| `local_trusted` | n/a | No login required | Single-operator local machine workflow |
    30|| `authenticated` | `private` | Login required | Private-network access (for example Tailscale/VPN/LAN) |
    31|| `authenticated` | `public` | Login required | Internet-facing/cloud deployment |
    32|
    33|## Reachability Model
    34|
    35|| Bind | Meaning | Typical use |
    36||---|---|---|
    37|| `loopback` | Listen on localhost only | default local usage, reverse-proxy deployments |
    38|| `lan` | Listen on all interfaces (`0.0.0.0`) | LAN/VPN/private-network access |
    39|| `tailnet` | Listen on a detected Tailscale IP | Tailscale-only access |
    40|| `custom` | Listen on an explicit host/IP | advanced interface-specific setups |
    41|
    42|## 3. Security Policy
    43|
    44|## `local_trusted`
    45|
    46|- loopback-only host binding
    47|- no human login flow
    48|- optimized for fastest local startup
    49|
    50|## `authenticated + private`
    51|
    52|- login required
    53|- low-friction URL handling (`auto` base URL mode)
    54|- private-host trust policy required
    55|- bind can be `loopback`, `lan`, `tailnet`, or `custom`
    56|
    57|## `authenticated + public`
    58|
    59|- login required
    60|- explicit public URL required
    61|- stricter deployment checks and failures in doctor
    62|- recommended bind is `loopback` behind a reverse proxy; direct `lan/custom` is advanced
    63|
    64|## 4. Onboarding UX Contract
    65|
    66|Default onboarding remains interactive and flagless:
    67|
    68|```sh
    69|pnpm paperclipai onboard
    70|```
    71|
    72|Server prompt behavior:
    73|
    74|1. quickstart `--yes` defaults to `server.bind=loopback` and therefore `local_trusted/private`
    75|2. advanced server setup asks reachability first:
    76|- `Trusted local` → `bind=loopback`, `local_trusted/private`
    77|- `Private network` → `bind=lan`, `authenticated/private`
    78|- `Tailnet` → `bind=tailnet`, `authenticated/private`
    79|- `Custom` → manual mode/exposure/host entry
    80|3. raw host entry is only required for the `Custom` path
    81|4. explicit public URL is only required for `authenticated + public`
    82|
    83|Examples:
    84|
    85|```sh
    86|pnpm paperclipai onboard --yes
    87|pnpm paperclipai onboard --yes --bind lan
    88|pnpm paperclipai run --bind tailnet
    89|```
    90|
    91|`configure --section server` follows the same interactive behavior.
    92|
    93|## 5. Doctor UX Contract
    94|
    95|Default doctor remains flagless:
    96|
    97|```sh
    98|pnpm paperclipai doctor
    99|```
   100|
   101|Doctor reads configured mode/exposure and applies mode-aware checks. Optional override flags are secondary.
   102|
   103|## 6. Board/User Integration Contract
   104|
   105|Board identity must be represented by a real DB user principal for user-based features to work consistently.
   106|
   107|Required integration points:
   108|
   109|- real user row in `authUsers` for Board identity
   110|- `instance_user_roles` entry for Board admin authority
   111|- `company_memberships` integration for user-level task assignment and access
   112|
   113|This is required because user assignment paths validate active membership for `assigneeUserId`.
   114|
   115|## 7. Local Trusted -> Authenticated Claim Flow
   116|
   117|When running `authenticated` mode, if the only instance admin is `local-board`, Paperclip emits a startup warning with a one-time high-entropy claim URL.
   118|
   119|- URL format: `/board-claim/<token>?code=<code>`
   120|- intended use: signed-in human claims board ownership
   121|- claim action:
   122|  - promotes current signed-in user to `instance_admin`
   123|  - demotes `local-board` admin role
   124|  - ensures active owner membership for the claiming user across existing companies
   125|
   126|This prevents lockout when a user migrates from long-running local trusted usage to authenticated mode.
   127|
   128|## 8. Current Code Reality (As Of 2026-02-23)
   129|
   130|- runtime values are `local_trusted | authenticated`
   131|- `authenticated` uses Better Auth sessions and bootstrap invite flow
   132|- `local_trusted` ensures a real local Board user principal in `authUsers` with `instance_user_roles` admin access
   133|- company creation ensures creator membership in `company_memberships` so user assignment/access flows remain consistent
   134|
   135|## 9. Naming and Compatibility Policy
   136|
   137|- canonical naming is `local_trusted` and `authenticated` with `private/public` exposure
   138|- no long-term compatibility alias layer for discarded naming variants
   139|
   140|## 10. Relationship to Other Docs
   141|
   142|- implementation plan: `doc/plans/deployment-auth-mode-consolidation.md`
   143|- V1 contract: `doc/SPEC-implementation.md`
   144|- operator workflows: `doc/DEVELOPING.md` and `doc/CLI.md`
   145|", "total_lines": 144, "file_size": 4863, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# CLI Reference
     2|
     3|Paperclip CLI now supports both:
     4|
     5|- instance setup/diagnostics (`onboard`, `doctor`, `configure`, `env`, `allowed-hostname`)
     6|- control-plane client operations (issues, approvals, agents, activity, dashboard)
     7|
     8|## Base Usage
     9|
    10|Use repo script in development:
    11|
    12|```sh
    13|pnpm paperclipai --help
    14|```
    15|
    16|First-time local bootstrap + run:
    17|
    18|```sh
    19|pnpm paperclipai run
    20|```
    21|
    22|Choose local instance:
    23|
    24|```sh
    25|pnpm paperclipai run --instance dev
    26|```
    27|
    28|## Deployment Modes
    29|
    30|Mode taxonomy and design intent are documented in `doc/DEPLOYMENT-MODES.md`.
    31|
    32|Current CLI behavior:
    33|
    34|- `paperclipai onboard` and `paperclipai configure --section server` set deployment mode in config
    35|- server onboarding/configure ask for reachability intent and write `server.bind`
    36|- `paperclipai run --bind <loopback|lan|tailnet>` passes a quickstart bind preset into first-run onboarding when config is missing
    37|- runtime can override mode with `PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_MODE`
    38|- `paperclipai run` and `paperclipai doctor` still do not expose a direct low-level `--mode` flag
    39|
    40|Canonical behavior is documented in `doc/DEPLOYMENT-MODES.md`.
    41|
    42|Allow an authenticated/private hostname (for example custom Tailscale DNS):
    43|
    44|```sh
    45|pnpm paperclipai allowed-hostname dotta-macbook-pro
    46|```
    47|
    48|All client commands support:
    49|
    50|- `--data-dir <path>`
    51|- `--api-base <url>`
    52|- `--api-key <token>`
    53|- `--context <path>`
    54|- `--profile <name>`
    55|- `--json`
    56|
    57|Company-scoped commands also support `--company-id <id>`.
    58|
    59|Use `--data-dir` on any CLI command to isolate all default local state (config/context/db/logs/storage/secrets) away from `~/.paperclip`:
    60|
    61|```sh
    62|pnpm paperclipai run --data-dir ./tmp/paperclip-dev
    63|pnpm paperclipai issue list --data-dir ./tmp/paperclip-dev
    64|```
    65|
    66|## Context Profiles
    67|
    68|Store local defaults in `~/.paperclip/context.json`:
    69|
    70|```sh
    71|pnpm paperclipai context set --api-base http://localhost:3100 --company-id <company-id>
    72|pnpm paperclipai context show
    73|pnpm paperclipai context list
    74|pnpm paperclipai context use default
    75|```
    76|
    77|To avoid storing secrets in context, set `apiKeyEnvVarName` and keep the key in env:
    78|
    79|```sh
    80|pnpm paperclipai context set --api-key-env-var-name PAPERCLIP_API_KEY
    81|export PAPERCLIP_API_KEY=***
    82|```
    83|
    84|## Company Commands
    85|
    86|```sh
    87|pnpm paperclipai company list
    88|pnpm paperclipai company get <company-id>
    89|pnpm paperclipai company delete <company-id-or-prefix> --yes --confirm <same-id-or-prefix>
    90|```
    91|
    92|Examples:
    93|
    94|```sh
    95|pnpm paperclipai company delete PAP --yes --confirm PAP
    96|pnpm paperclipai company delete 5cbe79ee-acb3-4597-896e-7662742593cd --yes --confirm 5cbe79ee-acb3-4597-896e-7662742593cd
    97|```
    98|
    99|Notes:
   100|
   101|- Deletion is server-gated by `PAPERCLIP_ENABLE_COMPANY_DELETION`.
   102|- With agent authentication, company deletion is company-scoped. Use the current company ID/prefix (for example via `--company-id` or `PAPERCLIP_COMPANY_ID`), not another company.
   103|
   104|## Issue Commands
   105|
   106|```sh
   107|pnpm paperclipai issue list --company-id <company-id> [--status todo,in_progress] [--assignee-agent-id <agent-id>] [--match text]
   108|pnpm paperclipai issue get <issue-id-or-identifier>
   109|pnpm paperclipai issue create --company-id <company-id> --title "..." [--description "..."] [--status todo] [--priority high]
   110|pnpm paperclipai issue update <issue-id> [--status in_progress] [--comment "..."]
   111|pnpm paperclipai issue comment <issue-id> --body "..." [--reopen]
   112|pnpm paperclipai issue checkout <issue-id> --agent-id <agent-id> [--expected-statuses todo,backlog,blocked]
   113|pnpm paperclipai issue release <issue-id>
   114|```
   115|
   116|## Agent Commands
   117|
   118|```sh
   119|pnpm paperclipai agent list --company-id <company-id>
   120|pnpm paperclipai agent get <agent-id>
   121|pnpm paperclipai agent local-cli <agent-id-or-shortname> --company-id <company-id>
   122|```
   123|
   124|`agent local-cli` is the quickest way to run local Claude/Codex manually as a Paperclip agent:
   125|
   126|- creates a new long-lived agent API key
   127|- installs missing Paperclip skills into `~/.codex/skills` and `~/.claude/skills`
   128|- prints `export ...` lines for `PAPERCLIP_API_URL`, `PAPERCLIP_COMPANY_ID`, `PAPERCLIP_AGENT_ID`, and `PAPERCLIP_API_KEY`
   129|
   130|Example for shortname-based local setup:
   131|
   132|```sh
   133|pnpm paperclipai agent local-cli codexcoder --company-id <company-id>
   134|pnpm paperclipai agent local-cli claudecoder --company-id <company-id>
   135|```
   136|
   137|## Approval Commands
   138|
   139|```sh
   140|pnpm paperclipai approval list --company-id <company-id> [--status pending]
   141|pnpm paperclipai approval get <approval-id>
   142|pnpm paperclipai approval create --company-id <company-id> --type hire_agent --payload '{"name":"..."}' [--issue-ids <id1,id2>]
   143|pnpm paperclipai approval approve <approval-id> [--decision-note "..."]
   144|pnpm paperclipai approval reject <approval-id> [--decision-note "..."]
   145|pnpm paperclipai approval request-revision <approval-id> [--decision-note "..."]
   146|pnpm paperclipai approval resubmit <approval-id> [--payload '{"...":"..."}']
   147|pnpm paperclipai approval comment <approval-id> --body "..."
   148|```
   149|
   150|## Activity Commands
   151|
   152|```sh
   153|pnpm paperclipai activity list --company-id <company-id> [--agent-id <agent-id>] [--entity-type issue] [--entity-id <id>]
   154|```
   155|
   156|## Dashboard Commands
   157|
   158|```sh
   159|pnpm paperclipai dashboard get --company-id <company-id>
   160|```
   161|
   162|## Heartbeat Command
   163|
   164|`heartbeat run` now also supports context/api-key options and uses the shared client stack:
   165|
   166|```sh
   167|pnpm paperclipai heartbeat run --agent-id <agent-id> [--api-base http://localhost:3100] [--api-key <token>]
   168|```
   169|
   170|## Local Storage Defaults
   171|
   172|Default local instance root is `~/.paperclip/instances/default`:
   173|
   174|- config: `~/.paperclip/instances/default/config.json`
   175|- embedded db: `~/.paperclip/instances/default/db`
   176|- logs: `~/.paperclip/instances/default/logs`
   177|- storage: `~/.paperclip/instances/default/data/storage`
   178|- secrets key: `~/.paperclip/instances/default/secrets/master.key`
   179|
   180|Override base home or instance with env vars:
   181|
   182|```sh
   183|PAPERCLIP_HOME=/custom/home PAPERCLIP_INSTANCE_ID=dev pnpm paperclipai run
   184|```
   185|
   186|## Storage Configuration
   187|
   188|Configure storage provider and settings:
   189|
   190|```sh
   191|pnpm paperclipai configure --section storage
   192|```
   193|
   194|Supported providers:
   195|
   196|- `local_disk` (default; local single-user installs)
   197|- `s3` (S3-compatible object storage)
   198|", "total_lines": 197, "file_size": 6073, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     2|  db:
     3|    image: postgres:17-alpine

{"content": "     1|# @paperclipai/ui
     2|
     3|Published static assets for the Paperclip board UI.
     4|
     5|## What gets published
     6|
     7|The npm package contains the production build under `dist/`. It does not ship the UI source tree or workspace-only dependencies.
     8|
     9|## Typical use
    10|
    11|Install the package, then serve or copy the built files from `node_modules/@paperclipai/ui/dist`.
    12|", "total_lines": 11, "file_size": 340, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: What is Paperclip?
     3|summary: The control plane for autonomous AI companies
     4|---
     5|
     6|Paperclip is the control plane for autonomous AI companies. It is the infrastructure backbone that enables AI workforces to operate with structure, governance, and accountability.
     7|
     8|One instance of Paperclip can run multiple companies. Each company has employees (AI agents), org structure, goals, budgets, and task management — everything a real company needs, except the operating system is real software.
     9|
    10|## The Problem
    11|
    12|Task management software doesn't go far enough. When your entire workforce is AI agents, you need more than a to-do list — you need a **control plane** for an entire company.
    13|
    14|## What Paperclip Does
    15|
    16|Paperclip is the command, communication, and control plane for a company of AI agents. It is the single place where you:
    17|
    18|- **Manage agents as employees** — hire, organize, and track who does what
    19|- **Define org structure** — org charts that agents themselves operate within
    20|- **Track work in real time** — see at any moment what every agent is working on
    21|- **Control costs** — token salary budgets per agent, spend tracking, burn rate
    22|- **Align to goals** — agents see how their work serves the bigger mission
    23|- **Govern autonomy** — board approval gates, activity audit trails, budget enforcement
    24|
    25|## Two Layers
    26|
    27|### 1. Control Plane (Paperclip)
    28|
    29|The central nervous system. Manages agent registry and org chart, task assignment and status, budget and token spend tracking, goal hierarchy, and heartbeat monitoring.
    30|
    31|### 2. Execution Services (Adapters)
    32|
    33|Agents run externally and report into the control plane. Adapters connect different execution environments — Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, shell processes, HTTP webhooks, or any runtime that can call an API.
    34|
    35|The control plane doesn't run agents. It orchestrates them. Agents run wherever they run and phone home.
    36|
    37|## Core Principle
    38|
    39|You should be able to look at Paperclip and understand your entire company at a glance — who's doing what, how much it costs, and whether it's working.
    40|", "total_lines": 39, "file_size": 2074, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Quickstart
     3|summary: Get Paperclip running in minutes
     4|---
     5|
     6|Get Paperclip running locally in under 5 minutes.
     7|
     8|## Quick Start (Recommended)
     9|
    10|```sh
    11|npx paperclipai onboard --yes
    12|```
    13|
    14|This walks you through setup, configures your environment, and gets Paperclip running.
    15|
    16|If you already have a Paperclip install, rerunning `onboard` keeps your current config and data paths intact. Use `paperclipai configure` if you want to edit settings.
    17|
    18|To start Paperclip again later:
    19|
    20|```sh
    21|npx paperclipai run
    22|```
    23|
    24|> **Note:** If you used `npx` for setup, always use `npx paperclipai` to run commands. The `pnpm paperclipai` form only works inside a cloned copy of the Paperclip repository (see Local Development below).
    25|
    26|## Local Development
    27|
    28|For contributors working on Paperclip itself. Prerequisites: Node.js 20+ and pnpm 9+.
    29|
    30|Clone the repository, then:
    31|
    32|```sh
    33|pnpm install
    34|pnpm dev
    35|```
    36|
    37|This starts the API server and UI at [http://localhost:3100](http://localhost:3100).
    38|
    39|No external database required — Paperclip uses an embedded PostgreSQL instance by default.
    40|
    41|When working from the cloned repo, you can also use:
    42|
    43|```sh
    44|pnpm paperclipai run
    45|```
    46|
    47|This auto-onboards if config is missing, runs health checks with auto-repair, and starts the server.
    48|
    49|## What's Next
    50|
    51|Once Paperclip is running:
    52|
    53|1. Create your first company in the web UI
    54|2. Define a company goal
    55|3. Create a CEO agent and configure its adapter
    56|4. Build out the org chart with more agents
    57|5. Set budgets and assign initial tasks
    58|6. Hit go — agents start their heartbeats and the company runs
