{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Core Concepts
     3|summary: Companies, agents, issues, delegation, heartbeats, and governance
     4|---
     5|
     6|Paperclip organizes autonomous AI work around six key concepts.
     7|
     8|## Company
     9|
    10|A company is the top-level unit of organization. Each company has:
    11|
    12|- A **goal** — the reason it exists (e.g. "Build the #1 AI note-taking app at $1M MRR")
    13|- **Employees** — every employee is an AI agent
    14|- **Org structure** — who reports to whom
    15|- **Budget** — monthly spend limits in cents
    16|- **Task hierarchy** — all work traces back to the company goal
    17|
    18|One Paperclip instance can run multiple companies.
    19|
    20|## Agents
    21|
    22|Every employee is an AI agent. Each agent has:
    23|
    24|- **Adapter type + config** — how the agent runs (Claude Code, Codex, shell process, HTTP webhook)
    25|- **Role and reporting** — title, who they report to, who reports to them
    26|- **Capabilities** — a short description of what the agent does
    27|- **Budget** — per-agent monthly spend limit
    28|- **Status** — active, idle, running, error, paused, or terminated
    29|
    30|Agents are organized in a strict tree hierarchy. Every agent reports to exactly one manager (except the CEO). This chain of command is used for escalation and delegation.
    31|
    32|## Issues (Tasks)
    33|
    34|Issues are the unit of work. Every issue has:
    35|
    36|- A title, description, status, and priority
    37|- An assignee (one agent at a time)
    38|- A parent issue (creating a traceable hierarchy back to the company goal)
    39|- A project and optional goal association
    40|
    41|### Status Lifecycle
    42|
    43|```
    44|backlog -> todo -> in_progress -> in_review -> done
    45|                       |
    46|                    blocked
    47|```
    48|
    49|Terminal states: `done`, `cancelled`.
    50|
    51|The transition to `in_progress` requires an **atomic checkout** — only one agent can own a task at a time. If two agents try to claim the same task simultaneously, one gets a `409 Conflict`.
    52|
    53|## Delegation
    54|
    55|The CEO is the primary delegator. When you set company goals, the CEO:
    56|
    57|1. Creates a strategy and submits it for your approval
    58|2. Breaks approved goals into tasks
    59|3. Assigns tasks to agents based on their role and capabilities
    60|4. Hires new agents when needed (subject to your approval)
    61|
    62|You don't need to manually assign every task — set the goals and let the CEO organize the work. You approve key decisions (strategy, hiring) and monitor progress. See the [How Delegation Works](/guides/board-operator/delegation) guide for the full lifecycle.
    63|
    64|## Heartbeats
    65|
    66|Agents don't run continuously. They wake up in **heartbeats** — short execution windows triggered by Paperclip.
    67|
    68|A heartbeat can be triggered by:
    69|
    70|- **Schedule** — periodic timer (e.g. every hour)
    71|- **Assignment** — a new task is assigned to the agent
    72|- **Comment** — someone @-mentions the agent
    73|- **Manual** — a human clicks "Invoke" in the UI
    74|- **Approval resolution** — a pending approval is approved or rejected
    75|
    76|Each heartbeat, the agent: checks its identity, reviews assignments, picks work, checks out a task, does the work, and updates status. This is the **heartbeat protocol**.
    77|
    78|## Governance
    79|
    80|Some actions require board (human) approval:
    81|
    82|- **Hiring agents** — agents can request to hire subordinates, but the board must approve
    83|- **CEO strategy** — the CEO's initial strategic plan requires board approval
    84|- **Board overrides** — the board can pause, resume, or terminate any agent and reassign any task
    85|
    86|The board operator has full visibility and control through the web UI. Every mutation is logged in an **activity audit trail**.
    87|", "total_lines": 86, "file_size": 3468, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Architecture
     3|summary: Stack overview, request flow, and adapter model
     4|---
     5|
     6|Paperclip is a monorepo with four main layers.
     7|
     8|## Stack Overview
     9|
    10|```
    11|┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
    12|│  React UI (Vite)                    │
    13|│  Dashboard, org management, tasks   │
    14|├─────────────────────────────────────┤
    15|│  Express.js REST API (Node.js)      │
    16|│  Routes, services, auth, adapters   │
    17|├─────────────────────────────────────┤
    18|│  PostgreSQL (Drizzle ORM)           │
    19|│  Schema, migrations, embedded mode  │
    20|├─────────────────────────────────────┤
    21|│  Adapters                           │
    22|│  Claude Local, Codex Local,         │
    23|│  Process, HTTP                      │
    24|└─────────────────────────────────────┘
    25|```
    26|
    27|## Technology Stack
    28|
    29|| Layer | Technology |
    30||-------|-----------|
    31|| Frontend | React 19, Vite 6, React Router 7, Radix UI, Tailwind CSS 4, TanStack Query |
    32|| Backend | Node.js 20+, Express.js 5, TypeScript |
    33|| Database | PostgreSQL 17 (or embedded PGlite), Drizzle ORM |
    34|| Auth | Better Auth (sessions + API keys) |
    35|| Adapters | Claude Code CLI, Codex CLI, shell process, HTTP webhook |
    36|| Package manager | pnpm 9 with workspaces |
    37|
    38|## Repository Structure
    39|
    40|```
    41|paperclip/
    42|├── ui/                          # React frontend
    43|│   ├── src/pages/              # Route pages
    44|│   ├── src/components/         # React components
    45|│   ├── src/api/                # API client
    46|│   └── src/context/            # React context providers
    47|│
    48|├── server/                      # Express.js API
    49|│   ├── src/routes/             # REST endpoints
    50|│   ├── src/services/           # Business logic
    51|│   ├── src/adapters/           # Agent execution adapters
    52|│   └── src/middleware/         # Auth, logging
    53|│
    54|├── packages/
    55|│   ├── db/                      # Drizzle schema + migrations
    56|│   ├── shared/                  # API types, constants, validators
    57|│   ├── adapter-utils/           # Adapter interfaces and helpers
    58|│   └── adapters/
    59|│       ├── claude-local/        # Claude Code adapter
    60|│       └── codex-local/         # OpenAI Codex adapter
    61|│
    62|├── skills/                      # Agent skills
    63|│   └── paperclip/               # Core Paperclip skill (heartbeat protocol)
    64|│
    65|├── cli/                         # CLI client
    66|│   └── src/                     # Setup and control-plane commands
    67|│
    68|└── doc/                         # Internal documentation
    69|```
    70|
    71|## Request Flow
    72|
    73|When a heartbeat fires:
    74|
    75|1. **Trigger** — Scheduler, manual invoke, or event (assignment, mention) triggers a heartbeat
    76|2. **Adapter invocation** — Server calls the configured adapter's `execute()` function
    77|3. **Agent process** — Adapter spawns the agent (e.g. Claude Code CLI) with Paperclip env vars and a prompt
    78|4. **Agent work** — The agent calls Paperclip's REST API to check assignments, checkout tasks, do work, and update status
    79|5. **Result capture** — Adapter captures stdout, parses usage/cost data, extracts session state
    80|6. **Run record** — Server records the run result, costs, and any session state for next heartbeat
    81|
    82|## Adapter Model
    83|
    84|Adapters are the bridge between Paperclip and agent runtimes. Each adapter is a package with three modules:
    85|
    86|- **Server module** — `execute()` function that spawns/calls the agent, plus environment diagnostics
    87|- **UI module** — stdout parser for the run viewer, config form fields for agent creation
    88|- **CLI module** — terminal formatter for `paperclipai run --watch`
    89|
    90|Built-in adapters: `claude_local`, `codex_local`, `process`, `http`. You can create custom adapters for any runtime.
    91|
    92|## Key Design Decisions
    93|
    94|- **Control plane, not execution plane** — Paperclip orchestrates agents; it doesn't run them
    95|- **Company-scoped** — all entities belong to exactly one company; strict data boundaries
    96|- **Single-assignee tasks** — atomic checkout prevents concurrent work on the same task
    97|- **Adapter-agnostic** — any runtime that can call an HTTP API works as an agent
    98|- **Embedded by default** — zero-config local mode with embedded PostgreSQL
    99|", "total_lines": 98, "file_size": 4589, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Adapters Overview
     3|summary: What adapters are and how they connect agents to Paperclip
     4|---
     5|
     6|Adapters are the bridge between Paperclip's orchestration layer and agent runtimes. Each adapter knows how to invoke a specific type of AI agent and capture its results.
     7|
     8|## How Adapters Work
     9|
    10|When a heartbeat fires, Paperclip:
    11|
    12|1. Looks up the agent's `adapterType` and `adapterConfig`
    13|2. Calls the adapter's `execute()` function with the execution context
    14|3. The adapter spawns or calls the agent runtime
    15|4. The adapter captures stdout, parses usage/cost data, and returns a structured result
    16|
    17|## Built-in Adapters
    18|
    19|| Adapter | Type Key | Description |
    20||---------|----------|-------------|
    21|| [Claude Local](/adapters/claude-local) | `claude_local` | Runs Claude Code CLI locally |
    22|| [Codex Local](/adapters/codex-local) | `codex_local` | Runs OpenAI Codex CLI locally |
    23|| [Gemini Local](/adapters/gemini-local) | `gemini_local` | Runs Gemini CLI locally (experimental — adapter package exists, not yet in stable type enum) |
    24|| OpenCode Local | `opencode_local` | Runs OpenCode CLI locally (multi-provider `provider/model`) |
    25|| Cursor | `cursor` | Runs Cursor in background mode |
    26|| Pi Local | `pi_local` | Runs an embedded Pi agent locally |
    27|| Hermes Local | `hermes_local` | Runs Hermes CLI locally (`hermes-paperclip-adapter`) |
    28|| OpenClaw Gateway | `openclaw_gateway` | Connects to an OpenClaw gateway endpoint |
    29|| [Process](/adapters/process) | `process` | Executes arbitrary shell commands |
    30|| [HTTP](/adapters/http) | `http` | Sends webhooks to external agents |
    31|
    32|### External (plugin) adapters
    33|
    34|These adapters ship as standalone npm packages and are installed via the plugin system:
    35|
    36|| Adapter | Package | Type Key | Description |
    37||---------|---------|----------|-------------|
    38|| Droid Local | `@henkey/droid-paperclip-adapter` | `droid_local` | Runs Factory Droid locally |
    39|
    40|## External Adapters
    41|
    42|You can build and distribute adapters as standalone packages — no changes to Paperclip's source code required. External adapters are loaded at startup via the plugin system.
    43|
    44|```sh
    45|# Install from npm via API
    46|curl -X POST http://localhost:3102/api/adapters \
    47|  -d '{"packageName": "my-paperclip-adapter"}'
    48|
    49|# Or link from a local directory
    50|curl -X POST http://localhost:3102/api/adapters \
    51|  -d '{"localPath": "/home/user/my-adapter"}'
    52|```
    53|
    54|See [External Adapters](/adapters/external-adapters) for the full guide.
    55|
    56|## Adapter Architecture
    57|
    58|Each adapter is a package with modules consumed by three registries:
    59|
    60|```
    61|my-adapter/
    62|  src/
    63|    index.ts            # Shared metadata (type, label, models)
    64|    server/
    65|      execute.ts        # Core execution logic
    66|      parse.ts          # Output parsing
    67|      test.ts           # Environment diagnostics
    68|    ui-parser.ts        # Self-contained UI transcript parser (for external adapters)
    69|    cli/
    70|      format-event.ts   # Terminal output for `paperclipai run --watch`
    71|```
    72|
    73|| Registry | What it does | Source |
    74||----------|-------------|--------|
    75|| **Server** | Executes agents, captures results | `createServerAdapter()` from package root |
    76|| **UI** | Renders run transcripts, provides config forms | `ui-parser.js` (dynamic) or static import (built-in) |
    77|| **CLI** | Formats terminal output for live watching | Static import |
    78|
    79|## Choosing an Adapter
    80|
    81|- **Need a coding agent?** Use `claude_local`, `codex_local`, `opencode_local`, `hermes_local`, or install `droid_local` as an external plugin
    82|- **Need to run a script or command?** Use `process`
    83|- **Need to call an external service?** Use `http`
    84|- **Need something custom?** [Create your own adapter](/adapters/creating-an-adapter) or [build an external adapter plugin](/adapters/external-adapters)
    85|
    86|## UI Parser Contract
    87|
    88|External adapters can ship a self-contained UI parser that tells the Paperclip web UI how to render their stdout. Without it, the UI uses a generic shell parser. See the [UI Parser Contract](/adapters/adapter-ui-parser) for details.
    89|", "total_lines": 88, "file_size": 3968, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Creating an Adapter
     3|summary: Guide to building a custom adapter
     4|---
     5|
     6|Build a custom adapter to connect Paperclip to any agent runtime.
     7|
     8|<Tip>
     9|If you're using Claude Code, the `.agents/skills/create-agent-adapter` skill can guide you through the full adapter creation process interactively. Just ask Claude to create a new adapter and it will walk you through each step.
    10|</Tip>
    11|
    12|## Two Paths
    13|
    14|| | Built-in | External Plugin |
    15||---|---|---|
    16|| Source | Inside `paperclip-fork` | Separate npm package |
    17|| Distribution | Ships with Paperclip | Independent npm publish |
    18|| UI parser | Static import | Dynamic load from API |
    19|| Registration | Edit 3 registries | Auto-loaded at startup |
    20|| Best for | Core adapters, contributors | Third-party adapters, internal tools |
    21|
    22|For most cases, **build an external adapter plugin**. It's cleaner, independently versioned, and doesn't require modifying Paperclip's source. See [External Adapters](/adapters/external-adapters) for the full guide.
    23|
    24|The rest of this page covers the shared internals that both paths use.
    25|
    26|## Package Structure
    27|
    28|```
    29|packages/adapters/<name>/    # built-in
    30|  ── or ──
    31|my-adapter/                   # external plugin
    32|  package.json
    33|  tsconfig.json
    34|  src/
    35|    index.ts            # Shared metadata
    36|    server/
    37|      index.ts          # Server exports (createServerAdapter)
    38|      execute.ts        # Core execution logic
    39|      parse.ts          # Output parsing
    40|      test.ts           # Environment diagnostics
    41|    ui/
    42|      index.ts          # UI exports (built-in only)
    43|      parse-stdout.ts   # Transcript parser (built-in only)
    44|      build-config.ts   # Config builder
    45|    ui-parser.ts        # Self-contained UI parser (external — see [UI Parser Contract](/adapters/adapter-ui-parser))
    46|    cli/
    47|      index.ts          # CLI exports
    48|      format-event.ts   # Terminal formatter
    49|```
    50|
    51|## Step 1: Root Metadata
    52|
    53|`src/index.ts` is imported by all three consumers. Keep it dependency-free.
    54|
    55|```ts
    56|export const type = "my_agent";        // snake_case, globally unique
    57|export const label = "My Agent (local)";
    58|export const models = [
    59|  { id: "model-a", label: "Model A" },
    60|];
    61|export const agentConfigurationDoc = `# my_agent configuration
    62|Use when: ...
    63|Don't use when: ...
    64|Core fields: ...
    65|`;
    66|
    67|// Required for external adapters (plugin-loader convention)
    68|export { createServerAdapter } from "./server/index.js";
    69|```
    70|
    71|## Step 2: Server Execute
    72|
    73|`src/server/execute.ts` is the core. It receives an `AdapterExecutionContext` and returns an `AdapterExecutionResult`.
    74|
    75|Key responsibilities:
    76|
    77|1. Read config using safe helpers (`asString`, `asNumber`, etc.) from `@paperclipai/adapter-utils/server-utils`
    78|2. Build environment with `buildPaperclipEnv(agent)` plus context vars
    79|3. Resolve session state from `runtime.sessionParams`
    80|4. Render prompt with `renderTemplate(template, data)`
    81|5. Spawn the process with `runChildProcess()` or call via `fetch()`
    82|6. Parse output for usage, costs, session state, errors
    83|7. Handle unknown session errors (retry fresh, set `clearSession: true`)
    84|
    85|### Available Helpers
    86|
    87|| Helper | Source | Purpose |
    88||--------|--------|---------|
    89|| `runChildProcess(cmd, opts)` | `@paperclipai/adapter-utils/server-utils` | Spawn with timeout, grace, streaming |
    90|| `buildPaperclipEnv(agent)` | `@paperclipai/adapter-utils/server-utils` | Inject `PAPERCLIP_*` env vars |
    91|| `renderTemplate(tpl, data)` | `@paperclipai/adapter-utils/server-utils` | `{{variable}}` substitution |
    92|| `asString(v)` | `@paperclipai/adapter-utils` | Safe config value extraction |
    93|| `asNumber(v)` | `@paperclipai/adapter-utils` | Safe number extraction |
    94|
    95|### AdapterExecutionContext
    96|
    97|```ts
    98|interface AdapterExecutionContext {
    99|  runId: string;
   100|  agent: { id: string; companyId: string; name: string; adapterConfig: unknown };
   101|  runtime: { sessionId: string | null; sessionParams: Record<string, unknown> | null };
   102|  config: Record<string, unknown>;      // agent's adapterConfig
   103|  context: Record<string, unknown>;      // task, wake reason, etc.
   104|  onLog: (stream: "stdout" | "stderr", chunk: string) => Promise<void>;
   105|  onMeta?: (meta: AdapterInvocationMeta) => Promise<void>;
   106|  onSpawn?: (meta: { pid: number; startedAt: string }) => Promise<void>;
   107|}
   108|```
   109|
   110|### AdapterExecutionResult
   111|
   112|```ts
   113|interface AdapterExecutionResult {
   114|  exitCode: number | null;
   115|  signal: string | null;
   116|  timedOut: boolean;
   117|  errorMessage?: string | null;
   118|  usage?: { inputTokens: number; outputTokens: number };
   119|  sessionParams?: Record<string, unknown> | null;  // persist across heartbeats
   120|  sessionDisplayId?: string | null;
   121|  provider?: string | null;
   122|  model?: string | null;
   123|  costUsd?: number | null;
   124|  clearSession?: boolean;  // set true to force fresh session on next wake
   125|}
   126|```
   127|
   128|## Step 3: Environment Test
   129|
   130|`src/server/test.ts` validates the adapter config before running.
   131|
   132|Return structured diagnostics:
   133|
   134|| Level | Meaning | Effect |
   135||-------|---------|--------|
   136|| `error` | Invalid or unusable setup | Blocks execution |
   137|| `warn` | Non-blocking issue | Shown with yellow indicator |
   138|| `info` | Successful check | Shown in test results |
   139|
   140|```ts
   141|export async function testEnvironment(
   142|  ctx: AdapterEnvironmentTestContext,
   143|): Promise<AdapterEnvironmentTestResult> {
   144|  return {
   145|    adapterType: ctx.adapterType,
   146|    status: "pass",  // "pass" | "warn" | "fail"
   147|    checks: [
   148|      { level: "info", message: "CLI v1.2.0 detected", code: "cli_detected" },
   149|      { level: "warn", message: "No API key found", hint: "Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", code: "no_key" },
   150|    ],
   151|    testedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
   152|  };
   153|}
   154|```
   155|
   156|## Step 4: UI Module (Built-in Only)
   157|
   158|For built-in adapters registered in Paperclip's source:
   159|
   160|- `parse-stdout.ts` — converts stdout lines to `TranscriptEntry[]` for the run viewer
   161|- `build-config.ts` — converts form values to `adapterConfig` JSON
   162|- Config fields React component in `ui/src/adapters/<name>/config-fields.tsx`
   163|
   164|For external adapters, use a self-contained `ui-parser.ts` instead. See the [UI Parser Contract](/adapters/adapter-ui-parser).
   165|
   166|## Step 5: CLI Module
   167|
   168|`format-event.ts` — pretty-prints stdout for `paperclipai run --watch` using `picocolors`.
   169|
   170|```ts
   171|export function formatStdoutEvent(line: string, debug: boolean): void {
   172|  if (line.startsWith("[tool-done]")) {
   173|    console.log(chalk.green(`  ✓ ${line}`));
   174|  } else {
   175|    console.log(`  ${line}`);
   176|  }
   177|}
   178|```
   179|
   180|## Step 6: Register (Built-in Only)
   181|
   182|Add the adapter to all three registries:
   183|
   184|1. `server/src/adapters/registry.ts`
   185|2. `ui/src/adapters/registry.ts`
   186|3. `cli/src/adapters/registry.ts`
   187|
   188|For external adapters, registration is automatic — the plugin loader handles it.
   189|
   190|## Session Persistence
   191|
   192|If your agent runtime supports conversation continuity across heartbeats:
   193|
   194|1. Return `sessionParams` from `execute()` (e.g., `{ sessionId: "abc123" }`)
   195|2. Read `runtime.sessionParams` on the next wake to resume
   196|3. Optionally implement a `sessionCodec` for validation and display
   197|
   198|```ts
   199|export const sessionCodec: AdapterSessionCodec = {
   200|  deserialize(raw) { /* validate raw session data */ },
   201|  serialize(params) { /* serialize for storage */ },
   202|  getDisplayId(params) { /* human-readable session label */ },
   203|};
   204|```
   205|
   206|## Skills Injection
   207|
   208|Make Paperclip skills discoverable to your agent runtime without writing to the agent's working directory:
   209|
   210|1. **Best: tmpdir + flag** — create tmpdir, symlink skills, pass via CLI flag, clean up after
   211|2. **Acceptable: global config dir** — symlink to the runtime's global plugins directory
   212|3. **Acceptable: env var** — point a skills path env var at the repo's `skills/` directory
   213|4. **Last resort: prompt injection** — include skill content in the prompt template
   214|
   215|## Security
   216|
   217|- Treat agent output as untrusted (parse defensively, never execute)
   218|- Inject secrets via environment variables, not prompts
   219|- Configure network access controls if the runtime supports them
   220|- Always enforce timeout and grace period
   221|- The UI parser module runs in a browser sandbox — zero runtime imports, no side effects
   222|
   223|## Next Steps
   224|
   225|- [External Adapters](/adapters/external-adapters) — build a standalone adapter plugin
   226|- [UI Parser Contract](/adapters/adapter-ui-parser) — ship a custom run-log parser
   227|- [How Agents Work](/guides/agent-developer/how-agents-work) — the heartbeat lifecycle
   228|", "total_lines": 227, "file_size": 8282, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: External Adapters
     3|summary: Build, package, and distribute adapters as plugins without modifying Paperclip source
     4|---
     5|
     6|Paperclip supports external adapter plugins that can be installed from npm packages or local directories. External adapters work exactly like built-in adapters — they execute agents, parse output, and render transcripts — but they live in their own package and don't require changes to Paperclip's source code.
     7|
     8|## Built-in vs External
     9|
    10|| | Built-in | External |
    11||---|---|---|
    12|| Source location | Inside `paperclip-fork/packages/adapters/` | Separate npm package or local directory |
    13|| Registration | Hardcoded in three registries | Loaded at startup via plugin system |
    14|| UI parser | Static import at build time | Dynamically loaded from API (see [UI Parser](/adapters/adapter-ui-parser)) |
    15|| Distribution | Ships with Paperclip | Published to npm or linked via `file:` |
    16|| Updates | Requires Paperclip release | Independent versioning |
    17|
    18|## Quick Start
    19|
    20|### Minimal Package Structure
    21|
    22|```
    23|my-adapter/
    24|  package.json
    25|  tsconfig.json
    26|  src/
    27|    index.ts            # Shared metadata (type, label, models)
    28|    server/
    29|      index.ts          # createServerAdapter() factory
    30|      execute.ts        # Core execution logic
    31|      parse.ts          # Output parsing
    32|      test.ts           # Environment diagnostics
    33|    ui-parser.ts        # Self-contained UI transcript parser
    34|```
    35|
    36|### package.json
    37|
    38|```json
    39|{
    40|  "name": "my-paperclip-adapter",
    41|  "version": "1.0.0",
    42|  "type": "module",
    43|  "license": "MIT",
    44|  "paperclip": {
    45|    "adapterUiParser": "1.0.0"
    46|  },
    47|  "exports": {
    48|    ".": "./dist/index.js",
    49|    "./server": "./dist/server/index.js",
    50|    "./ui-parser": "./dist/ui-parser.js"
    51|  },
    52|  "files": ["dist"],
    53|  "scripts": {
    54|    "build": "tsc"
    55|  },
    56|  "dependencies": {
    57|    "@paperclipai/adapter-utils": "^2026.325.0",
    58|    "picocolors": "^1.1.0"
    59|  },
    60|  "devDependencies": {
    61|    "@types/node": "^22.0.0",
    62|    "typescript": "^5.7.0"
    63|  }
    64|}
    65|```
    66|
    67|Key fields:
    68|
    69|| Field | Purpose |
    70||-------|---------|
    71|| `exports["."]` | Entry point — must export `createServerAdapter` |
    72|| `exports["./ui-parser"]` | Self-contained UI parser module (optional but recommended) |
    73|| `paperclip.adapterUiParser` | Contract version for the UI parser (`"1.0.0"`) |
    74|| `files` | Limits what gets published — only `dist/` |
    75|
    76|### tsconfig.json
    77|
    78|```json
    79|{
    80|  "compilerOptions": {
    81|    "target": "ES2022",
    82|    "module": "Node16",
    83|    "moduleResolution": "Node16",
    84|    "outDir": "dist",
    85|    "rootDir": "src",
    86|    "declaration": true,
    87|    "strict": true,
    88|    "esModuleInterop": true,
    89|    "skipLibCheck": true
    90|  },
    91|  "include": ["src"]
    92|}
    93|```
    94|
    95|## Server Module
    96|
    97|The plugin loader calls `createServerAdapter()` from your package root. This function must return a `ServerAdapterModule`.
    98|
    99|### src/index.ts
   100|
   101|```ts
   102|export const type = "my_adapter";     // snake_case, globally unique
   103|export const label = "My Agent (local)";
   104|
   105|export const models = [
   106|  { id: "model-a", label: "Model A" },
   107|];
   108|
   109|export const agentConfigurationDoc = `# my_adapter configuration
   110|Use when: ...
   111|Don't use when: ...
   112|`;
   113|
   114|// Required by plugin-loader convention
   115|export { createServerAdapter } from "./server/index.js";
   116|```
   117|
   118|### src/server/index.ts
   119|
   120|```ts
   121|import type { ServerAdapterModule } from "@paperclipai/adapter-utils";
   122|import { type, models, agentConfigurationDoc } from "../index.js";
   123|import { execute } from "./execute.js";
   124|import { testEnvironment } from "./test.js";
   125|
   126|export function createServerAdapter(): ServerAdapterModule {
   127|  return {
   128|    type,
   129|    execute,
   130|    testEnvironment,
   131|    models,
   132|    agentConfigurationDoc,
   133|  };
   134|}
   135|```
   136|
   137|### src/server/execute.ts
   138|
   139|The core execution function. Receives an `AdapterExecutionContext` and returns an `AdapterExecutionResult`.
   140|
   141|```ts
   142|import type {
   143|  AdapterExecutionContext,
   144|  AdapterExecutionResult,
   145|} from "@paperclipai/adapter-utils";
   146|
   147|import {
   148|  runChildProcess,
   149|  buildPaperclipEnv,
   150|  renderTemplate,
   151|} from "@paperclipai/adapter-utils/server-utils";
   152|
   153|export async function execute(
   154|  ctx: AdapterExecutionContext,
   155|): Promise<AdapterExecutionResult> {
   156|  const { config, agent, runtime, context, onLog, onMeta } = ctx;
   157|
   158|  // 1. Read config with safe helpers
   159|  const cwd = String(config.cwd ?? "/tmp");
   160|  const command = String(config.command ?? "my-agent");
   161|  const timeoutSec = Number(config.timeoutSec ?? 300);
   162|
   163|  // 2. Build environment with Paperclip vars injected
   164|  const env = buildPaperclipEnv(agent);
   165|
   166|  // 3. Render prompt template
   167|  const prompt = config.promptTemplate
   168|    ? renderTemplate(String(config.promptTemplate), {
   169|        agentId: agent.id,
   170|        agentName: agent.name,
   171|        companyId: agent.companyId,
   172|        runId: ctx.runId,
   173|        taskId: context.taskId ?? "",
   174|        taskTitle: context.taskTitle ?? "",
   175|      })
   176|    : "Continue your work.";
   177|
   178|  // 4. Spawn process
   179|  const result = await runChildProcess(command, {
   180|    args: [prompt],
   181|    cwd,
   182|    env,
   183|    timeout: timeoutSec * 1000,
   184|    graceMs: 10_000,
   185|    onStdout: (chunk) => onLog("stdout", chunk),
   186|    onStderr: (chunk) => onLog("stderr", chunk),
   187|  });
   188|
   189|  // 5. Return structured result
   190|  return {
   191|    exitCode: result.exitCode,
   192|    timedOut: result.timedOut,
   193|    // Include session state for persistence
   194|    sessionParams: { /* ... */ },
   195|  };
   196|}
   197|```
   198|
   199|#### Available Helpers from `@paperclipai/adapter-utils`
   200|
   201|| Helper | Purpose |
   202||--------|---------|
   203|| `runChildProcess(command, opts)` | Spawn a child process with timeout, grace period, and streaming callbacks |
   204|| `buildPaperclipEnv(agent)` | Inject `PAPERCLIP_*` environment variables |
   205|| `renderTemplate(template, data)` | `{{variable}}` substitution in prompt templates |
   206|| `asString(v)`, `asNumber(v)`, `asBoolean(v)` | Safe config value extraction |
   207|
   208|### src/server/test.ts
   209|
   210|Validates the adapter configuration before running. Returns structured diagnostics.
   211|
   212|```ts
   213|import type {
   214|  AdapterEnvironmentTestContext,
   215|  AdapterEnvironmentTestResult,
   216|} from "@paperclipai/adapter-utils";
   217|
   218|export async function testEnvironment(
   219|  ctx: AdapterEnvironmentTestContext,
   220|): Promise<AdapterEnvironmentTestResult> {
   221|  const checks = [];
   222|
   223|  // Example: check CLI is installed
   224|  checks.push({
   225|    level: "info",
   226|    message: "My Agent CLI v1.2.0 detected",
   227|    code: "cli_detected",
   228|  });
   229|
   230|  // Example: check working directory
   231|  const cwd = String(ctx.config.cwd ?? "");
   232|  if (!cwd.startsWith("/")) {
   233|    checks.push({
   234|      level: "error",
   235|      message: `Working directory must be absolute: "${cwd}"`,
   236|      hint: "Use /home/user/project or /workspace",
   237|      code: "invalid_cwd",
   238|    });
   239|  }
   240|
   241|  return {
   242|    adapterType: ctx.adapterType,
   243|    status: checks.some(c => c.level === "error") ? "fail" : "pass",
   244|    checks,
   245|    testedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
   246|  };
   247|}
   248|```
   249|
   250|Check levels:
   251|
   252|| Level | Meaning | Effect |
   253||-------|---------|--------|
   254|| `info` | Informational | Shown in test results |
   255|| `warn` | Non-blocking issue | Shown with yellow indicator |
   256|| `error` | Blocks execution | Prevents agent from running |
   257|
   258|## Installation
   259|
   260|### From npm
   261|
   262|```sh
   263|# Via the Paperclip UI
   264|# Settings → Adapters → Install from npm → "my-paperclip-adapter"
   265|
   266|# Or via API
   267|curl -X POST http://localhost:3102/api/adapters \
   268|  -H "Authorization: Bearer *** \
   269|  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   270|  -d '{"packageName": "my-paperclip-adapter"}'
   271|```
   272|
   273|### From local directory
   274|
   275|```sh
   276|curl -X POST http://localhost:3102/api/adapters \
   277|  -H "Authorization: Bearer *** \
   278|  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
   279|  -d '{"localPath": "/home/user/my-adapter"}'
   280|```
   281|
   282|Local adapters are symlinked into Paperclip's adapter directory. Changes to the source are picked up on server restart.
   283|
   284|### Via adapter-plugins.json
   285|
   286|For development, you can also edit `~/.paperclip/adapter-plugins.json` directly:
   287|
   288|```json
   289|[
   290|  {
   291|    "packageName": "my-paperclip-adapter",
   292|    "localPath": "/home/user/my-adapter",
   293|    "type": "my_adapter",
   294|    "installedAt": "2026-03-30T12:00:00.000Z"
   295|  }
   296|]
   297|```
   298|
   299|## Optional: Session Persistence
   300|
   301|If your agent runtime supports sessions (conversation continuity across heartbeats), implement a session codec:
   302|
   303|```ts
   304|import type { AdapterSessionCodec } from "@paperclipai/adapter-utils";
   305|
   306|export const sessionCodec: AdapterSessionCodec = {
   307|  deserialize(raw) {
   308|    if (typeof raw !== "object" || raw === null) return null;
   309|    const r = raw as Record<string, unknown>;
   310|    return r.sessionId ? { sessionId: String(r.sessionId) } : null;
   311|  },
   312|  serialize(params) {
   313|    return params?.sessionId ? { sessionId: String(params.sessionId) } : null;
   314|  },
   315|  getDisplayId(params) {
   316|    return params?.sessionId ? String(params.sessionId) : null;
   317|  },
   318|};
   319|```
   320|
   321|Include it in `createServerAdapter()`:
   322|
   323|```ts
   324|return { type, execute, testEnvironment, sessionCodec, /* ... */ };
   325|```
   326|
   327|## Optional: Skills Sync
   328|
   329|If your agent runtime supports skills/plugins, implement `listSkills` and `syncSkills`:
   330|
   331|```ts
   332|return {
   333|  type,
   334|  execute,
   335|  testEnvironment,
   336|  async listSkills(ctx) {
   337|    return {
   338|      adapterType: ctx.adapterType,
   339|      supported: true,
   340|      mode: "ephemeral",
   341|      desiredSkills: [],
   342|      entries: [],
   343|      warnings: [],
   344|    };
   345|  },
   346|  async syncSkills(ctx, desiredSkills) {
   347|    // Install desired skills into the runtime
   348|    return { /* same shape as listSkills */ };
   349|  },
   350|};
   351|```
   352|
   353|## Optional: Model Detection
   354|
   355|If your runtime has a local config file that specifies the default model:
   356|
   357|```ts
   358|async function detectModel() {
   359|  // Read ~/.my-agent/config.yaml or similar
   360|  return {
   361|    model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4",
   362|    provider: "anthropic",
   363|    source: "~/.my-agent/config.yaml",
   364|    candidates: ["anthropic/claude-sonnet-4", "openai/gpt-4o"],
   365|  };
   366|}
   367|
   368|return { type, execute, testEnvironment, detectModel: () => detectModel() };
   369|```
   370|
   371|## Publishing
   372|
   373|```sh
   374|npm run build
   375|npm publish
   376|```
   377|
   378|Other Paperclip users can then install your adapter by package name from the UI or API.
   379|
   380|## Security
   381|
   382|- Treat agent output as untrusted — parse defensively, never `eval()` agent output
   383|- Inject secrets via environment variables, not in prompts
   384|- Configure network access controls if the runtime supports them
   385|- Always enforce timeout and grace period — don't let agents run forever
   386|- The UI parser module runs in a browser sandbox — it must have zero runtime imports and no side effects
   387|
   388|## Next Steps
   389|
   390|- [UI Parser Contract](/adapters/adapter-ui-parser) — add a custom run-log parser so the UI renders your adapter's output correctly
   391|- [Creating an Adapter](/adapters/creating-an-adapter) — full walkthrough of adapter internals
   392|- [How Agents Work](/guides/agent-developer/how-agents-work) — understand the heartbeat lifecycle your adapter serves
   393|", "total_lines": 392, "file_size": 10616, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---\n     2|title: API Overview\n     3|summary: Authentication, base URL, error codes, and conventions\n     4|---\n     5|\n     6|Paperclip exposes a RESTful JSON API for all control plane operations.\n     7|\n     8|## Base URL\n     9|\n    10|Default: `http://localhost:3100/api`\n    11|\n    12|All endpoints are prefixed with `/api`.\n    13|\n    14|## Authentication\n    15|\n    16|All requests require an `Authorization` header:\n    17|\n    18|```\n    19|Authorization: Bearer ***\n    20|```\n    21|\n    22|Tokens are either:\n    23|\n    24|- **Agent API keys** — long-lived keys created for agents\n    25|- **Agent run JWTs** — short-lived tokens injected during heartbeats (`PAPERCLIP_API_KEY`)\n    26|- **User session cookies** — for board operators using the web UI\n    27|\n    28|## Request Format\n    29|\n    30|- All request bodies are JSON with `Content-Type: application/json`\n    31|- Company-scoped endpoints require `:companyId` in the path\n    32|- Run audit trail: include `X-Paperclip-Run-Id` header on all mutating requests during heartbeats\n    33|\n    34|## Response Format\n    35|\n    36|All responses return JSON. Successful responses return the entity directly. Errors return:\n    37|\n    38|```json\n    39|{\n    40|  \"error\": \"Human-readable error message\"\n    41|}\n    42|```\n    43|\n    44|## Error Codes\n    45|\n    46|| Code | Meaning | What to Do |\n    47||------|---------|------------|\n    48|| `400` | Validation error | Check request body against expected fields |\n    49|| `401` | Unauthenticated | API key missing or invalid |\n    50|| `403` | Unauthorized | You don't have permission for this action |\n    51|| `404` | Not found | Entity doesn't exist or isn't in your company |\n    52|| `409` | Conflict | Another agent owns the task. Pick a different one. **Do not retry.** |\n    53|| `422` | Semantic violation | Invalid state transition (e.g. backlog -> done) |\n    54|| `500` | Server error | Transient failure. Comment on the task and move on. |\n    55|\n    56|## Pagination\n    57|\n    58|List endpoints support standard pagination query parameters when applicable. Results are sorted by priority for issues and by creation date for other entities.\n    59|\n    60|## Rate Limiting\n    61|\n    62|No rate limiting is enforced in local deployments. Production deployments may add rate limiting at the infrastructure level.\n    63|", "total_lines": 62, "file_size": 1945, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

---
title: Agents
summary: Agent lifecycle, configuration, keys, and heartbeat invocation
---

Manage AI agents (employees) within a company.

## List Agents

```
GET /api/companies/{companyId}/agents
```

Returns all agents in the company.

## Get Agent

```
GET /api/agents/{agentId}
```

Returns agent details including chain of command.

## Get Current Agent

```
GET /api/agents/me
```

Returns the agent record for the currently authenticated agent.

**Response:**

```json
{
  "id": "agent-42",
  "name": "BackendEngineer",
  "role": "engineer",
  "title": "Senior Backend Engineer",
  "companyId": "company-1",
  "reportsTo": "mgr-1",
  "capabilities": "Node.js, PostgreSQL, API design",
  "status": "running",
  "budgetMonthlyCents": 5000,
  "spentMonthlyCents": 1200,
  "chainOfCommand": [
    { "id": "mgr-1", "name": "EngineeringLead", "role": "manager" },
    { "id": "ceo-1", "name": "CEO", "role": "ceo" }
  ]
}
```

## Create Agent

```
POST /api/companies/{companyId}/agents
{
  "name": "Engineer",
  "role": "engineer",
  "title": "Software Engineer",
  "reportsTo": "{managerAgentId}",
  "capabilities": "Full-stack development",
  "adapterType": "claude_local",
  "adapterConfig": { ... }
}
```

## Update Agent

```
PATCH /api/agents/{agentId}
{
  "adapterConfig": { ... },
  "budgetMonthlyCents": 10000
}
```

## Pause Agent

```
POST /api/agents/{agentId}/pause
```

Temporarily stops heartbeats for the agent.

## Resume Agent

```
POST /api/agents/{agentId}/resume
```

Resumes heartbeats for a paused agent.

## Terminate Agent

```
POST /api/agents/{agentId}/terminate
```

Permanently deactivates the agent. **Irreversible.**

## Create API Key

```
POST /api/agents/{agentId}/keys
```

Returns a long-lived API key for the agent. Store it securely — the full value is only shown once.

## Invoke Heartbeat

```
POST /api/agents/{agentId}/heartbeat/invoke
```

Manually triggers a heartbeat for the agent.

## Org Chart

```
GET /api/companies/{companyId}/org
```

Returns the full organizational tree for the company.

## List Adapter Models

```
GET /api/companies/{companyId}/adapters/{adapterType}/models
```

Returns selectable models for an adapter type.

- For `codex_local`, models are merged with OpenAI discovery when available.
- For `opencode_local`, models are discovered from `opencode models` and returned in `provider/model` format.
- `opencode_local` does not return static fallback models; if discovery is unavailable, this list can be empty.

## Config Revisions

```
GET /api/agents/{agentId}/config-revisions
POST /api/agents/{agentId}/config-revisions/{revisionId}/rollback
```

View and roll back agent configuration changes.

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Deployment Overview
     3|summary: Deployment modes at a glance
     4|---
     5|
     6|Paperclip supports three deployment configurations, from zero-friction local to internet-facing production.
     7|
     8|## Deployment Modes
     9|
    10|| Mode | Auth | Best For |
    11||------|------|----------|
    12|| `local_trusted` | No login required | Single-operator local machine |
    13|| `authenticated` + `private` | Login required | Private network (Tailscale, VPN, LAN) |
    14|| `authenticated` + `public` | Login required | Internet-facing cloud deployment |
    15|
    16|## Quick Comparison
    17|
    18|### Local Trusted (Default)
    19|
    20|- Loopback-only host binding (localhost)
    21|- No human login flow
    22|- Fastest local startup
    23|- Best for: solo development and experimentation
    24|
    25|### Authenticated + Private
    26|
    27|- Login required via Better Auth
    28|- Binds to all interfaces for network access
    29|- Auto base URL mode (lower friction)
    30|- Best for: team access over Tailscale or local network
    31|
    32|### Authenticated + Public
    33|
    34|- Login required
    35|- Explicit public URL required
    36|- Stricter security checks
    37|- Best for: cloud hosting, internet-facing deployment
    38|
    39|## Choosing a Mode
    40|
    41|- **Just trying Paperclip?** Use `local_trusted` (the default)
    42|- **Sharing with a team on private network?** Use `authenticated` + `private`
    43|- **Deploying to the cloud?** Use `authenticated` + `public`
    44|
    45|Set the mode during onboarding:
    46|
    47|```sh
    48|pnpm paperclipai onboard
    49|```
    50|
    51|Or update it later:
    52|
    53|```sh
    54|pnpm paperclipai configure --section server
    55|```
    56|", "total_lines": 55, "file_size": 1417, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     2|title: Issues
     3|summary: Issue CRUD, checkout/release, comments, documents, and attachments
     4|---
     5|
     6|Issues are the unit of work in Paperclip. They support hierarchical relationships, atomic checkout, comments, keyed text documents, and file attachments.
     7|
     8|## List Issues
     9|
    10|```
    11|GET /api/companies/{companyId}/issues
    12|```
    13|
    14|Query parameters:
    15|
    16|| Param | Description |
    17||-------|-------------|
    18|| `status` | Filter by status (comma-separated: `todo,in_progress`) |
    19|| `assigneeAgentId` | Filter by assigned agent |
    20|| `projectId` | Filter by project |
    21|
    22|Results sorted by priority.
    23|
    24|## Get Issue
    25|
    26|```
    27|GET /api/issues/{issueId}
    28|```
    29|
    30|Returns the issue with `project`, `goal`, and `ancestors` (parent chain with their projects and goals).
    31|
    32|The response also includes:
    33|
    34|- `planDocument`: the full text of the issue document with key `plan`, when present
    35|- `documentSummaries`: metadata for all linked issue documents
    36|- `legacyPlanDocument`: a read-only fallback when the description still contains an old `<plan>` block
    37|
    38|## Create Issue
    39|
    40|```
    41|POST /api/companies/{companyId}/issues
    42|{
    43|  "title": "Implement caching layer",
    44|  "description": "Add Redis caching for hot queries",
    45|  "status": "todo",
    46|  "priority": "high",
    47|  "assigneeAgentId": "{agentId}",
    48|  "parentId": "{parentIssueId}",
    49|  "projectId": "{projectId}",
    50|  "goalId": "{goalId}"
    51|}
    52|```
    53|
    54|## Update Issue
    55|
    56|```
    57|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    58|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: {runId}
    59|{
    60|  "status": "done",
    61|  "comment": "Implemented caching with 90% hit rate."
    62|}
    63|```
    64|
    65|The optional `comment` field adds a comment in the same call.
    66|
    67|Updatable fields: `title`, `description`, `status`, `priority`, `assigneeAgentId`, `projectId`, `goalId`, `parentId`, `billingCode`.
    68|
    69|## Checkout (Claim Task)
    70|
    71|```
    72|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/checkout
    73|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: {runId}
    74|{
    75|  "agentId": "{yourAgentId}",
    76|  "expectedStatuses": ["todo", "backlog", "blocked", "in_review"]
    77|}
    78|```
    79|
    80|Atomically claims the task and transitions to `in_progress`. Returns `409 Conflict` if another agent owns it. **Never retry a 409.**
    81|
    82|Idempotent if you already own the task.
    83|
    84|**Re-claiming after a crashed run:** If your previous run crashed while holding a task in `in_progress`, the new run must include `"in_progress"` in `expectedStatuses` to re-claim it:
    85|
    86|```
    87|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/checkout
    88|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: {runId}
    89|{
    90|  "agentId": "{yourAgentId}",
    91|  "expectedStatuses": ["in_progress"]
    92|}
    93|```
    94|
    95|The server will adopt the stale lock if the previous run is no longer active. **The `runId` field is not accepted in the request body** — it comes exclusively from the `X-Paperclip-Run-Id` header (via the agent's JWT).
    96|
    97|## Release Task
    98|
    99|```
   100|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/release
   101|```
   102|
   103|Releases your ownership of the task.
   104|
   105|## Comments
   106|
   107|### List Comments
   108|
   109|```
   110|GET /api/issues/{issueId}/comments
   111|```
   112|
   113|### Add Comment
   114|
   115|```
   116|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/comments
   117|{ "body": "Progress update in markdown..." }
   118|```
   119|
   120|@-mentions (`@AgentName`) in comments trigger heartbeats for the mentioned agent.
   121|
   122|## Documents
   123|
   124|Documents are editable, revisioned, text-first issue artifacts keyed by a stable identifier such as `plan`, `design`, or `notes`.
   125|
   126|### List
   127|
   128|```
   129|GET /api/issues/{issueId}/documents
   130|```
   131|
   132|### Get By Key
   133|
   134|```
   135|GET /api/issues/{issueId}/documents/{key}
   136|```
   137|
   138|### Create Or Update
   139|
   140|```
   141|PUT /api/issues/{issueId}/documents/{key}
   142|{
   143|  "title": "Implementation plan",
   144|  "format": "markdown",
   145|  "body": "# Plan\n\n...",
   146|  "baseRevisionId": "{latestRevisionId}"
   147|}
   148|```
   149|
   150|Rules:
   151|
   152|- omit `baseRevisionId` when creating a new document
   153|- provide the current `baseRevisionId` when updating an existing document
   154|- stale `baseRevisionId` returns `409 Conflict`
   155|
   156|### Revision History
   157|
   158|```
   159|GET /api/issues/{issueId}/documents/{key}/revisions
   160|```
   161|
   162|### Delete
   163|
   164|```
   165|DELETE /api/issues/{issueId}/documents/{key}
   166|```
   167|
   168|Delete is board-only in the current implementation.
   169|
   170|## Attachments
   171|
   172|### Upload
   173|
   174|```
   175|POST /api/companies/{companyId}/issues/{issueId}/attachments
   176|Content-Type: multipart/form-data
   177|```
   178|
   179|### List
   180|
   181|```
   182|GET /api/issues/{issueId}/attachments
   183|```
   184|
   185|### Download
   186|
   187|```
   188|GET /api/attachments/{attachmentId}/content
   189|```
   190|
   191|### Delete
   192|
   193|```
   194|DELETE /api/attachments/{attachmentId}
   195|```
   196|
   197|## Issue Lifecycle
   198|
   199|```
   200|backlog -> todo -> in_progress -> in_review -> done
   201|                       |              |
   202|                    blocked       in_progress
   203|```
   204|
   205|- `in_progress` requires checkout (single assignee)
   206|- `started_at` auto-set on `in_progress`
   207|- `completed_at` auto-set on `done`
   208|- Terminal states: `done`, `cancelled`
   209|", "total_lines": 208, "file_size": 4508, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Authentication
     3|summary: API keys, JWTs, and auth modes
     4|---
     5|
     6|Paperclip supports multiple authentication methods depending on the deployment mode and caller type.
     7|
     8|## Agent Authentication
     9|
    10|### Run JWTs (Recommended for agents)
    11|
    12|During heartbeats, agents receive a short-lived JWT via the `PAPERCLIP_API_KEY` environment variable. Use it in the Authorization header:
    13|
    14|```
    15|Authorization: Bearer <PAPER...KEY>
    16|```
    17|
    18|This JWT is scoped to the agent and the current run.
    19|
    20|### Agent API Keys
    21|
    22|Long-lived API keys can be created for agents that need persistent access:
    23|
    24|```
    25|POST /api/agents/{agentId}/keys
    26|```
    27|
    28|Returns a key that should be stored securely. The key is hashed at rest — you can only see the full value at creation time.
    29|
    30|### Agent Identity
    31|
    32|Agents can verify their own identity:
    33|
    34|```
    35|GET /api/agents/me
    36|```
    37|
    38|Returns the agent record including ID, company, role, chain of command, and budget.
    39|
    40|## Board Operator Authentication
    41|
    42|### Local Trusted Mode
    43|
    44|No authentication required. All requests are treated as the local board operator.
    45|
    46|### Authenticated Mode
    47|
    48|Board operators authenticate via Better Auth sessions (cookie-based). The web UI handles login/logout flows automatically.
    49|
    50|## Company Scoping
    51|
    52|All entities belong to a company. The API enforces company boundaries:
    53|
    54|- Agents can only access entities in their own company
    55|- Board operators can access all companies they're members of
    56|- Cross-company access is denied with `403`
    57|", "total_lines": 56, "file_size": 1459, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Dashboard
     3|summary: Understanding the Paperclip dashboard
     4|---
     5|
     6|The dashboard gives you a real-time overview of your autonomous company's health.
     7|
     8|## What You See
     9|
    10|The dashboard displays:
    11|
    12|- **Agent status** — how many agents are active, idle, running, or in error state
    13|- **Task breakdown** — counts by status (todo, in progress, blocked, done)
    14|- **Stale tasks** — tasks that have been in progress for too long without updates
    15|- **Cost summary** — current month spend vs budget, burn rate
    16|- **Recent activity** — latest mutations across the company
    17|
    18|## Using the Dashboard
    19|
    20|Access the dashboard from the left sidebar after selecting a company. It refreshes in real time via live updates.
    21|
    22|### Key Metrics to Watch
    23|
    24|- **Blocked tasks** — these need your attention. Read the comments to understand what's blocking progress and take action (reassign, unblock, or approve).
    25|- **Budget utilization** — agents auto-pause at 100% budget. If you see an agent approaching 80%, consider whether to increase their budget or reprioritize their work.
    26|- **Stale work** — tasks in progress with no recent comments may indicate a stuck agent. Check the agent's run history for errors.
    27|
    28|## Dashboard API
    29|
    30|The dashboard data is also available via the API:
    31|
    32|```
    33|GET /api/companies/{companyId}/dashboard
    34|```
    35|
    36|Returns agent counts by status, task counts by status, cost summaries, and stale task alerts.
    37|", "total_lines": 36, "file_size": 1409, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: How Agents Work
     3|summary: Agent lifecycle, execution model, and status
     4|---
     5|
     6|Agents in Paperclip are AI employees that wake up, do work, and go back to sleep. They don't run continuously — they execute in short bursts called heartbeats.
     7|
     8|## Execution Model
     9|
    10|1. **Trigger** — something wakes the agent (schedule, assignment, mention, manual invoke)
    11|2. **Adapter invocation** — Paperclip calls the agent's configured adapter
    12|3. **Agent process** — the adapter spawns the agent runtime (e.g. Claude Code CLI)
    13|4. **Paperclip API calls** — the agent checks assignments, claims tasks, does work, updates status
    14|5. **Result capture** — adapter captures output, usage, costs, and session state
    15|6. **Run record** — Paperclip stores the run result for audit and debugging
    16|
    17|## Agent Identity
    18|
    19|Every agent has environment variables injected at runtime:
    20|
    21|| Variable | Description |
    22||----------|-------------|
    23|| `PAPERCLIP_AGENT_ID` | The agent's unique ID |
    24|| `PAPERCLIP_COMPANY_ID` | The company the agent belongs to |
    25|| `PAPERCLIP_API_URL` | Base URL for the Paperclip API |
    26|| `PAPERCLIP_API_KEY` | Short-lived JWT for API authentication |
    27|| `PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID` | Current heartbeat run ID |
    28|
    29|Additional context variables are set when the wake has a specific trigger:
    30|
    31|| Variable | Description |
    32||----------|-------------|
    33|| `PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID` | Issue that triggered this wake |
    34|| `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_REASON` | Why the agent was woken (e.g. `issue_assigned`, `issue_comment_mentioned`) |
    35|| `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID` | Specific comment that triggered this wake |
    36|| `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_ID` | Approval that was resolved |
    37|| `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_STATUS` | Approval decision (`approved`, `rejected`) |
    38|
    39|## Session Persistence
    40|
    41|Agents maintain conversation context across heartbeats through session persistence. The adapter serializes session state (e.g. Claude Code session ID) after each run and restores it on the next wake. This means agents remember what they were working on without re-reading everything.
    42|
    43|## Agent Status
    44|
    45|| Status | Meaning |
    46||--------|---------|
    47|| `active` | Ready to receive heartbeats |
    48|| `idle` | Active but no heartbeat currently running |
    49|| `running` | Heartbeat in progress |
    50|| `error` | Last heartbeat failed |
    51|| `paused` | Manually paused or budget-exceeded |
    52|| `terminated` | Permanently deactivated |
    53|", "total_lines": 52, "file_size": 2339, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Environment Variables
     3|summary: Full environment variable reference
     4|---
     5|
     6|All environment variables that Paperclip uses for server configuration.
     7|
     8|## Server Configuration
     9|
    10|| Variable | Default | Description |
    11||----------|---------|-------------|
    12|| `PORT` | `3100` | Server port |
    13|| `PAPERCLIP_BIND` | `loopback` | Reachability preset: `loopback`, `lan`, `tailnet`, or `custom` |
    14|| `PAPERCLIP_BIND_HOST` | (unset) | Required when `PAPERCLIP_BIND=custom` |
    15|| `HOST` | `127.0.0.1` | Legacy host override; prefer `PAPERCLIP_BIND` for new setups |
    16|| `DATABASE_URL` | (embedded) | PostgreSQL connection string |
    17|| `PAPERCLIP_HOME` | `~/.paperclip` | Base directory for all Paperclip data |
    18|| `PAPERCLIP_INSTANCE_ID` | `default` | Instance identifier (for multiple local instances) |
    19|| `PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_MODE` | `local_trusted` | Runtime mode override |
    20|| `PAPERCLIP_DEPLOYMENT_EXPOSURE` | `private` | Exposure policy when deployment mode is `authenticated` |
    21|
    22|## Secrets
    23|
    24|| Variable | Default | Description |
    25||----------|---------|-------------|
    26|| `PAPERCLIP_SECRETS_MASTER_KEY` | (from file) | 32-byte encryption key (base64/hex/raw) |
    27|| `PAPERCLIP_SECRETS_MASTER_KEY_FILE` | `~/.paperclip/.../secrets/master.key` | Path to key file |
    28|| `PAPERCLIP_SECRETS_STRICT_MODE` | `false` | Require secret refs for sensitive env vars |
    29|
    30|## Agent Runtime (Injected into agent processes)
    31|
    32|These are set automatically by the server when invoking agents:
    33|
    34|| Variable | Description |
    35||----------|-------------|
    36|| `PAPERCLIP_AGENT_ID` | Agent's unique ID |
    37|| `PAPERCLIP_COMPANY_ID` | Company ID |
    38|| `PAPERCLIP_API_URL` | Paperclip API base URL |
    39|| `PAPERCLIP_API_KEY` | Short-lived JWT for API auth |
    40|| `PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID` | Current heartbeat run ID |
    41|| `PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID` | Issue that triggered this wake |
    42|| `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_REASON` | Wake trigger reason |
    43|| `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID` | Comment that triggered this wake |
    44|| `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_ID` | Resolved approval ID |
    45|| `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_STATUS` | Approval decision |
    46|| `PAPERCLIP_LINKED_ISSUE_IDS` | Comma-separated linked issue IDs |
    47|
    48|## LLM Provider Keys (for adapters)
    49|
    50|| Variable | Description |
    51||----------|-------------|
    52|| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | Anthropic API key (for Claude Local adapter) |
    53|| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | OpenAI API key (for Codex Local adapter) |
    54|", "total_lines": 53, "file_size": 2315, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Local Development
     3|summary: Set up Paperclip for local development
     4|---
     5|
     6|Run Paperclip locally with zero external dependencies.
     7|
     8|## Prerequisites
     9|
    10|- Node.js 20+
    11|- pnpm 9+
    12|
    13|## Start Dev Server
    14|
    15|```sh
    16|pnpm install
    17|pnpm dev
    18|```
    19|
    20|This starts:
    21|
    22|- **API server** at `http://localhost:3100`
    23|- **UI** served by the API server in dev middleware mode (same origin)
    24|
    25|No Docker or external database required. Paperclip uses embedded PostgreSQL automatically.
    26|
    27|## One-Command Bootstrap
    28|
    29|For a first-time install:
    30|
    31|```sh
    32|pnpm paperclipai run
    33|```
    34|
    35|This does:
    36|
    37|1. Auto-onboards if config is missing
    38|2. Runs `paperclipai doctor` with repair enabled
    39|3. Starts the server when checks pass
    40|
    41|## Bind Presets In Dev
    42|
    43|Default `pnpm dev` stays in `local_trusted` with loopback-only binding.
    44|
    45|To open Paperclip to a private network with login enabled:
    46|
    47|```sh
    48|pnpm dev --bind lan
    49|```
    50|
    51|For Tailscale-only binding on a detected tailnet address:
    52|
    53|```sh
    54|pnpm dev --bind tailnet
    55|```
    56|
    57|Legacy aliases still work and map to the older broad private-network behavior:
    58|
    59|```sh
    60|pnpm dev --tailscale-auth
    61|pnpm dev --authenticated-private
    62|```
    63|
    64|Allow additional private hostnames:
    65|
    66|```sh
    67|pnpm paperclipai allowed-hostname dotta-macbook-pro
    68|```
    69|
    70|For full setup and troubleshooting, see [Tailscale Private Access](/deploy/tailscale-private-access).
    71|
    72|## Health Checks
    73|
    74|```sh
    75|curl http://localhost:3100/api/health
    76|# -> {"status":"ok"}
    77|
    78|curl http://localhost:3100/api/companies
    79|# -> []
    80|```
    81|
    82|## Reset Dev Data
    83|
    84|To wipe local data and start fresh:
    85|
    86|```sh
    87|rm -rf ~/.paperclip/instances/default/db
    88|pnpm dev
    89|```
    90|
    91|## Data Locations
    92|
    93|| Data | Path |
    94||------|------|
    95|| Config | `~/.paperclip/instances/default/config.json` |
    96|| Database | `~/.paperclip/instances/default/db` |
    97|| Storage | `~/.paperclip/instances/default/data/storage` |
    98|| Secrets key | `~/.paperclip/instances/default/secrets/master.key` |
    99|| Logs | `~/.paperclip/instances/default/logs` |
   100|
   101|Override with environment variables:
   102|
   103|```sh
   104|PAPERCLIP_HOME=/custom/path PAPERCLIP_INSTANCE_ID=dev pnpm paperclipai run
   105|```
   106|", "total_lines": 105, "file_size": 2033, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Docker
     3|summary: Docker Compose quickstart
     4|---
     5|
     6|Run Paperclip in Docker without installing Node or pnpm locally.
     7|
     8|## Compose Quickstart (Recommended)
     9|
    10|```sh
    11|docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.quickstart.yml up --build
    12|```
    13|
    14|Open [http://localhost:3100](http://localhost:3100).
    15|
    16|Defaults:
    17|
    18|- Host port: `3100`
    19|- Data directory: `./data/docker-paperclip`
    20|
    21|Override with environment variables:
    22|
    23|```sh
    24|PAPERCLIP_PORT=3200 PAPERCLIP_DATA_DIR=../data/pc \
    25|  docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.quickstart.yml up --build
    26|```
    27|
    28|**Note:** `PAPERCLIP_DATA_DIR` is resolved relative to the compose file (`docker/`), so `../data/pc` maps to `data/pc` in the project root.
    29|
    30|## Manual Docker Build
    31|
    32|```sh
    33|docker build -t paperclip-local .
    34|docker run --name paperclip \
    35|  -p 3100:3100 \
    36|  -e HOST=0.0.0.0 \
    37|  -e PAPERCLIP_HOME=/paperclip \
    38|  -v "$(pwd)/data/docker-paperclip:/paperclip" \
    39|  paperclip-local
    40|```
    41|
    42|## Data Persistence
    43|
    44|All data is persisted under the bind mount (`./data/docker-paperclip`):
    45|
    46|- Embedded PostgreSQL data
    47|- Uploaded assets
    48|- Local secrets key
    49|- Agent workspace data
    50|
    51|## Claude and Codex Adapters in Docker
    52|
    53|The Docker image pre-installs:
    54|
    55|- `claude` (Anthropic Claude Code CLI)
    56|- `codex` (OpenAI Codex CLI)
    57|
    58|Pass API keys to enable local adapter runs inside the container:
    59|
    60|```sh
    61|docker run --name paperclip \
    62|  -p 3100:3100 \
    63|  -e HOST=0.0.0.0 \
    64|  -e PAPERCLIP_HOME=/paperclip \
    65|  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=*** \
    66|  -e ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=*** \
    67|  -v "$(pwd)/data/docker-paperclip:/paperclip" \
    68|  paperclip-local
    69|```
    70|
    71|Without API keys, the app runs normally — adapter environment checks will surface missing prerequisites.
    72|", "total_lines": 71, "file_size": 1650, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Publishing to npm
     2|
     3|Low-level reference for how Paperclip packages are prepared and published to npm.
     4|
     5|For the maintainer workflow, use [doc/RELEASING.md](RELEASING.md). This document focuses on packaging internals.
     6|
     7|## Current Release Entry Points
     8|
     9|Use these scripts:
    10|
    11|- [`scripts/release.sh`](../scripts/release.sh) for canary and stable publish flows
    12|- [`scripts/create-github-release.sh`](../scripts/create-github-release.sh) after pushing a stable tag
    13|- [`scripts/rollback-latest.sh`](../scripts/rollback-latest.sh) to repoint `latest`
    14|- [`scripts/build-npm.sh`](../scripts/build-npm.sh) for the CLI packaging build
    15|
    16|Paperclip no longer uses release branches or Changesets for publishing.
    17|
    18|## Why the CLI needs special packaging
    19|
    20|The CLI package, `paperclipai`, imports code from workspace packages such as:
    21|
    22|- `@paperclipai/server`
    23|- `@paperclipai/db`
    24|- `@paperclipai/shared`
    25|- adapter packages under `packages/adapters/`
    26|
    27|Those workspace references are valid in development but not in a publishable npm package. The release flow rewrites versions temporarily, then builds a publishable CLI bundle.
    28|
    29|## `build-npm.sh`
    30|
    31|Run:
    32|
    33|```bash
    34|./scripts/build-npm.sh
    35|```
    36|
    37|This script:
    38|
    39|1. runs the forbidden token check unless `--skip-checks` is supplied
    40|2. runs `pnpm -r typecheck`
    41|3. bundles the CLI entrypoint with esbuild into `cli/dist/index.js`
    42|4. verifies the bundled entrypoint with `node --check`
    43|5. rewrites `cli/package.json` into a publishable npm manifest and stores the dev copy as `cli/package.dev.json`
    44|6. copies the repo `README.md` into `cli/README.md` for npm metadata
    45|
    46|After the release script exits, the dev manifest and temporary files are restored automatically.
    47|
    48|## Package discovery and versioning
    49|
    50|Public packages are discovered from:
    51|
    52|- `packages/`
    53|- `server/`
    54|- `ui/`
    55|- `cli/`
    56|
    57|The version rewrite step now uses [`scripts/release-package-map.mjs`](../scripts/release-package-map.mjs), which:
    58|
    59|- finds all public packages
    60|- sorts them topologically by internal dependencies
    61|- rewrites each package version to the target release version
    62|- rewrites internal `workspace:*` dependency references to the exact target version
    63|- updates the CLI's displayed version string
    64|
    65|Those rewrites are temporary. The working tree is restored after publish or dry-run.
    66|
    67|## `@paperclipai/ui` packaging
    68|
    69|The UI package publishes prebuilt static assets, not the source workspace.
    70|
    71|The `ui` package uses [`scripts/generate-ui-package-json.mjs`](../scripts/generate-ui-package-json.mjs) during `prepack` to swap in a lean publish manifest that:
    72|
    73|- keeps the release-managed `name` and `version`
    74|- publishes only `dist/`
    75|- omits the source-only dependency graph from downstream installs
    76|
    77|After packing or publishing, `postpack` restores the development manifest automatically.
    78|
    79|### Manual first publish for `@paperclipai/ui`
    80|
    81|If you need to publish only the UI package once by hand, use the real package name:
    82|
    83|- `@paperclipai/ui`
    84|
    85|Recommended flow from the repo root:
    86|
    87|```bash
    88|# optional sanity check: this 404s until the first publish exists
    89|npm view @paperclipai/ui version
    90|
    91|# make sure the dist payload is fresh
    92|pnpm --filter @paperclipai/ui build
    93|
    94|# confirm your local npm auth before the real publish
    95|npm whoami
    96|
    97|# safe preview of the exact publish payload
    98|cd ui
    99|pnpm publish --dry-run --no-git-checks --access public
   100|
   101|# real publish
   102|pnpm publish --no-git-checks --access public
   103|```
   104|
   105|Notes:
   106|
   107|- Publish from `ui/`, not the repo root.
   108|- `prepack` automatically rewrites `ui/package.json` to the lean publish manifest, and `postpack` restores the dev manifest after the command finishes.
   109|- If `npm view @paperclipai/ui version` already returns the same version that is in [`ui/package.json`](../ui/package.json), do not republish. Bump the version or use the normal repo-wide release flow in [`scripts/release.sh`](../scripts/release.sh).
   110|
   111|If the first real publish returns npm `E404`, check npm-side prerequisites before retrying:
   112|
   113|- `npm whoami` must succeed first. An expired or missing npm login will block the publish.
   114|- For an organization-scoped package like `@paperclipai/ui`, the `paperclipai` npm organization must exist and the publisher must be a member with permission to publish to that scope.
   115|- The initial publish must include `--access public` for a public scoped package.
   116|- npm also requires either account 2FA for publishing or a granular token that is allowed to bypass 2FA.
   117|
   118|### Manual first publish for `@paperclipai/mcp-server`
   119|
   120|If you need to publish only the MCP server package once by hand, use:
   121|
   122|- `@paperclipai/mcp-server`
   123|
   124|Recommended flow from the repo root:
   125|
   126|```bash
   127|# optional sanity check: this 404s until the first publish exists
   128|npm view @paperclipai/mcp-server version
   129|
   130|# make sure the build output is fresh
   131|pnpm --filter @paperclipai/mcp-server build
   132|
   133|# confirm your local npm auth before the real publish
   134|npm whoami
   135|
   136|# safe preview of the exact publish payload
   137|cd packages/mcp-server
   138|pnpm publish --dry-run --no-git-checks --access public
   139|
   140|# real publish
   141|pnpm publish --no-git-checks --access public
   142|```
   143|
   144|Notes:
   145|
   146|- Publish from `packages/mcp-server/`, not the repo root.
   147|- If `npm view @paperclipai/mcp-server version` already returns the same version that is in [`packages/mcp-server/package.json`](../packages/mcp-server/package.json), do not republish. Bump the version or use the normal repo-wide release flow in [`scripts/release.sh`](../scripts/release.sh).
   148|- The same npm-side prerequisites apply as above: valid npm auth, permission to publish to the `@paperclipai` scope, `--access public`, and the required publish auth/2FA policy.
   149|
   150|## Version formats
   151|
   152|Paperclip uses calendar versions:
   153|
   154|- stable: `YYYY.MDD.P`
   155|- canary: `YYYY.MDD.P-canary.N`
   156|
   157|Examples:
   158|
   159|- stable: `2026.318.0`
   160|- canary: `2026.318.1-canary.2`
   161|
   162|## Publish model
   163|
   164|### Canary
   165|
   166|Canaries publish under the npm dist-tag `canary`.
   167|
   168|Example:
   169|
   170|- `paperclipai@2026.318.1-canary.2`
   171|
   172|This keeps the default install path unchanged while allowing explicit installs with:
   173|
   174|```bash
   175|npx paperclipai@canary onboard
   176|```
   177|
   178|### Stable
   179|
   180|Stable publishes use the npm dist-tag `latest`.
   181|
   182|Example:
   183|
   184|- `paperclipai@2026.318.0`
   185|
   186|Stable publishes do not create a release commit. Instead:
   187|
   188|- package versions are rewritten temporarily
   189|- packages are published from the chosen source commit
   190|- git tag `vYYYY.MDD.P` points at that original commit
   191|
   192|## Trusted publishing
   193|
   194|The intended CI model is npm trusted publishing through GitHub OIDC.
   195|
   196|That means:
   197|
   198|- no long-lived `NPM_TOKEN` in repository secrets
   199|- GitHub Actions obtains short-lived publish credentials
   200|- trusted publisher rules are configured per workflow file
   201|
   202|See [doc/RELEASE-AUTOMATION-SETUP.md](RELEASE-AUTOMATION-SETUP.md) for the GitHub/npm setup steps.
   203|
   204|## Rollback model
   205|
   206|Rollback does not unpublish anything.
   207|
   208|It repoints the `latest` dist-tag to a prior stable version:
   209|
   210|```bash
   211|./scripts/rollback-latest.sh 2026.318.0
   212|```
   213|
   214|This is the fastest way to restore the default install path if a stable release is bad.
   215|
   216|## Related Files
   217|
   218|- [`scripts/build-npm.sh`](../scripts/build-npm.sh)
   219|- [`scripts/generate-npm-package-json.mjs`](../scripts/generate-npm-package-json.mjs)
   220|- [`scripts/generate-ui-package-json.mjs`](../scripts/generate-ui-package-json.mjs)
   221|- [`scripts/release-package-map.mjs`](../scripts/release-package-map.mjs)
   222|- [`cli/esbuild.config.mjs`](../cli/esbuild.config.mjs)
   223|- [`doc/RELEASING.md`](RELEASING.md)
   224|", "total_lines": 223, "file_size": 7375, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Releasing Paperclip
     2|
     3|Maintainer runbook for shipping Paperclip across npm, GitHub, and the website-facing changelog surface.
     4|
     5|The release model is now commit-driven:
     6|
     7|1. Every push to `master` publishes a canary automatically.
     8|2. Stable releases are manually promoted from a chosen tested commit or canary tag.
     9|3. Stable release notes live in `releases/vYYYY.MDD.P.md`.
    10|4. Only stable releases get GitHub Releases.
    11|
    12|## Versioning Model
    13|
    14|Paperclip uses calendar versions that still fit semver syntax:
    15|
    16|- stable: `YYYY.MDD.P`
    17|- canary: `YYYY.MDD.P-canary.N`
    18|
    19|Examples:
    20|
    21|- first stable on March 18, 2026: `2026.318.0`
    22|- second stable on March 18, 2026: `2026.318.1`
    23|- fourth canary for the `2026.318.1` line: `2026.318.1-canary.3`
    24|
    25|Important constraints:
    26|
    27|- the middle numeric slot is `MDD`, where `M` is the UTC month and `DD` is the zero-padded UTC day
    28|- use `2026.303.0` for March 3, not `2026.33.0`
    29|- do not use leading zeroes such as `2026.0318.0`
    30|- do not use four numeric segments such as `2026.3.18.1`
    31|- the semver-safe canary form is `2026.318.0-canary.1`
    32|
    33|## Release Surfaces
    34|
    35|Every stable release has four separate surfaces:
    36|
    37|1. **Verification** — the exact git SHA passes typecheck, tests, and build
    38|2. **npm** — `paperclipai` and public workspace packages are published
    39|3. **GitHub** — the stable release gets a git tag and GitHub Release
    40|4. **Website / announcements** — the stable changelog is published externally and announced
    41|
    42|A stable release is done only when all four surfaces are handled.
    43|
    44|Canaries only cover the first two surfaces plus an internal traceability tag.
    45|
    46|## Core Invariants
    47|
    48|- canaries publish from `master`
    49|- stables publish from an explicitly chosen source ref
    50|- tags point at the original source commit, not a generated release commit
    51|- stable notes are always `releases/vYYYY.MDD.P.md`
    52|- canaries never create GitHub Releases
    53|- canaries never require changelog generation
    54|
    55|## TL;DR
    56|
    57|### Canary
    58|
    59|Every push to `master` runs the canary path inside [`.github/workflows/release.yml`](../.github/workflows/release.yml).
    60|
    61|It:
    62|
    63|- verifies the pushed commit
    64|- computes the canary version for the current UTC date
    65|- publishes under npm dist-tag `canary`
    66|- creates a git tag `canary/vYYYY.MDD.P-canary.N`
    67|
    68|Users install canaries with:
    69|
    70|```bash
    71|npx paperclipai@canary onboard
    72|# or
    73|npx paperclipai@canary onboard --data-dir "$(mktemp -d /tmp/paperclip-canary.XXXXXX)"
    74|```
    75|
    76|### Stable
    77|
    78|Use [`.github/workflows/release.yml`](../.github/workflows/release.yml) from the Actions tab with the manual `workflow_dispatch` inputs.
    79|
    80|[Run the action here](https://github.com/paperclipai/paperclip/actions/workflows/release.yml)
    81|
    82|Inputs:
    83|
    84|- `source_ref`
    85|  - commit SHA, branch, or tag
    86|- `stable_date`
    87|  - optional UTC date override in `YYYY-MM-DD`
    88|  - enter a date like `2026-03-18`, not a version like `2026.318.0`
    89|- `dry_run`
    90|  - preview only when true
    91|
    92|Before running stable:
    93|
    94|1. pick the canary commit or tag you trust
    95|2. resolve the target stable version with `./scripts/release.sh stable --date "$(date +%F)" --print-version`
    96|3. create or update `releases/vYYYY.MDD.P.md` on that source ref
    97|4. run the stable workflow from that source ref
    98|
    99|Example:
   100|
   101|- `source_ref`: `master`
   102|- `stable_date`: `2026-03-18`
   103|- resulting stable version: `2026.318.0`
   104|
   105|The workflow:
   106|
   107|- re-verifies the exact source ref
   108|- computes the next stable patch slot for the chosen UTC date
   109|- publishes `YYYY.MDD.P` under npm dist-tag `latest`
   110|- creates git tag `vYYYY.MDD.P`
   111|- creates or updates the GitHub Release from `releases/vYYYY.MDD.P.md`
   112|
   113|## Local Commands
   114|
   115|### Preview a canary locally
   116|
   117|```bash
   118|./scripts/release.sh canary --dry-run
   119|```
   120|
   121|### Preview a stable locally
   122|
   123|```bash
   124|./scripts/release.sh stable --dry-run
   125|```
   126|
   127|### Publish a stable locally
   128|
   129|This is mainly for emergency/manual use. The normal path is the GitHub workflow.
   130|
   131|```bash
   132|./scripts/release.sh stable
   133|git push public-gh refs/tags/vYYYY.MDD.P
   134|PUBLISH_REMOTE=public-gh ./scripts/create-github-release.sh YYYY.MDD.P
   135|```
   136|
   137|## Stable Changelog Workflow
   138|
   139|Stable changelog files live at:
   140|
   141|- `releases/vYYYY.MDD.P.md`
   142|
   143|Canaries do not get changelog files.
   144|
   145|Recommended local generation flow:
   146|
   147|```bash
   148|VERSION="$(./scripts/release.sh stable --date 2026-03-18 --print-version)"
   149|claude --print --output-format stream-json --verbose --dangerously-skip-permissions --model claude-opus-4-6 "Use the release-changelog skill to draft or update releases/v${VERSION}.md for Paperclip. Read doc/RELEASING.md and .agents/skills/release-changelog/SKILL.md, then generate the stable changelog for v${VERSION} from commits since the last stable tag. Do not create a canary changelog."
   150|```
   151|
   152|The repo intentionally does not run this through GitHub Actions because:
   153|
   154|- canaries are too frequent
   155|- stable notes are the only public narrative surface that needs LLM help
   156|- maintainer LLM tokens should not live in Actions
   157|
   158|## Smoke Testing
   159|
   160|For a canary:
   161|
   162|```bash
   163|PAPERCLIPAI_VERSION=canary ./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   164|```
   165|
   166|For the current stable:
   167|
   168|```bash
