   169|PAPERCLIPAI_VERSION=latest ./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   170|```
   171|
   172|Useful isolated variants:
   173|
   174|```bash
   175|HOST_PORT=3232 DATA_DIR=./data/release-smoke-canary PAPERCLIPAI_VERSION=canary ./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   176|HOST_PORT=3233 DATA_DIR=./data/release-smoke-stable PAPERCLIPAI_VERSION=latest ./scripts/docker-onboard-smoke.sh
   177|```
   178|
   179|Automated browser smoke is also available:
   180|
   181|```bash
   182|gh workflow run release-smoke.yml -f paperclip_version=canary
   183|gh workflow run release-smoke.yml -f paperclip_version=latest
   184|```
   185|
   186|Minimum checks:
   187|
   188|- `npx paperclipai@canary onboard` installs
   189|- onboarding completes without crashes
   190|- authenticated login works with the smoke credentials
   191|- the browser lands in onboarding on a fresh instance
   192|- company creation succeeds
   193|- the first CEO agent is created
   194|- the first CEO heartbeat run is triggered
   195|
   196|## Rollback
   197|
   198|Rollback does not unpublish versions.
   199|
   200|It only moves the `latest` dist-tag back to a previous stable:
   201|
   202|```bash
   203|./scripts/rollback-latest.sh 2026.318.0 --dry-run
   204|./scripts/rollback-latest.sh 2026.318.0
   205|```
   206|
   207|Then fix forward with a new stable patch slot or release date.
   208|
   209|## Failure Playbooks
   210|
   211|### If the canary publishes but smoke testing fails
   212|
   213|Do not run stable.
   214|
   215|Instead:
   216|
   217|1. fix the issue on `master`
   218|2. merge the fix
   219|3. wait for the next automatic canary
   220|4. rerun smoke testing
   221|
   222|### If stable npm publish succeeds but tag push or GitHub release creation fails
   223|
   224|This is a partial release. npm is already live.
   225|
   226|Do this immediately:
   227|
   228|1. push the missing tag
   229|2. rerun `PUBLISH_REMOTE=public-gh ./scripts/create-github-release.sh YYYY.MDD.P`
   230|3. verify the GitHub Release notes point at `releases/vYYYY.MDD.P.md`
   231|
   232|Do not republish the same version.
   233|
   234|### If `latest` is broken after stable publish
   235|
   236|Roll back the dist-tag:
   237|
   238|```bash
   239|./scripts/rollback-latest.sh YYYY.MDD.P
   240|```
   241|
   242|Then fix forward with a new stable release.
   243|
   244|## Related Files
   245|
   246|- [`scripts/release.sh`](../scripts/release.sh)
   247|- [`scripts/release-package-map.mjs`](../scripts/release-package-map.mjs)
   248|- [`scripts/create-github-release.sh`](../scripts/create-github-release.sh)
   249|- [`scripts/rollback-latest.sh`](../scripts/rollback-latest.sh)
   250|- [`doc/PUBLISHING.md`](PUBLISHING.md)
   251|- [`doc/RELEASE-AUTOMATION-SETUP.md`](RELEASE-AUTOMATION-SETUP.md)
   252|", "total_lines": 251, "file_size": 7252, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Task Management Data Model
     2|
     3|Reference for how task tracking works in Paperclip. Describes the entities, their
     4|relationships, and the rules governing task lifecycle. Written as a target model
     5|-- some of this is already implemented, some is aspirational.
     6|
     7|---
     8|
     9|## Entity Hierarchy
    10|
    11|```
    12|Workspace
    13|  Initiatives          (roadmap-level objectives, span quarters)
    14|    Projects           (time-bound deliverables, can span teams)
    15|      Milestones       (stages within a project)
    16|        Issues         (units of work, the core entity)
    17|          Sub-issues   (broken-down work under a parent issue)
    18|```
    19|
    20|Everything flows down. An initiative contains projects; a project contains
    21|milestones and issues; an issue can have sub-issues. Each level adds
    22|granularity.
    23|
    24|---
    25|
    26|## Issues (Core Entity)
    27|
    28|An issue is the fundamental unit of work.
    29|
    30|### Fields
    31|
    32|| Field         | Type             | Required | Notes                                                             |
    33|| ------------- | ---------------- | -------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
    34|| `id`          | uuid             | yes      | Primary key                                                       |
    35|| `identifier`  | string           | computed | Human-readable, e.g. `ENG-123` (team key + auto-increment number) |
    36|| `title`       | string           | yes      | Short summary                                                     |
    37|| `description` | text/markdown    | no       | Full description, supports markdown                               |
    38|| `status`      | WorkflowState FK | yes      | Defaults to team's default state                                  |
    39|| `priority`    | enum (0-4)       | no       | Defaults to 0 (none). See Priority section.                       |
    40|| `estimate`    | number           | no       | Complexity/size points                                            |
    41|| `dueDate`     | date             | no       |                                                                   |
    42|| `teamId`      | uuid FK          | yes      | Every issue belongs to exactly one team                           |
    43|| `projectId`   | uuid FK          | no       | At most one project per issue                                     |
    44|| `milestoneId` | uuid FK          | no       | At most one milestone per issue                                   |
    45|| `assigneeId`  | uuid FK          | no       | **Single assignee.** See Assignees section.                       |
    46|| `creatorId`   | uuid FK          | no       | Who created it                                                    |
    47|| `parentId`    | uuid FK (self)   | no       | Parent issue, for sub-issue relationships                         |
    48|| `goalId`      | uuid FK          | no       | Linked objective/goal                                             |
    49|| `sortOrder`   | float            | no       | Ordering within views                                             |
    50|| `createdAt`   | timestamp        | yes      |                                                                   |
    51|| `updatedAt`   | timestamp        | yes      |                                                                   |
    52|| `startedAt`   | timestamp        | computed | When issue entered a "started" state                              |
    53|| `completedAt` | timestamp        | computed | When issue entered a "completed" state                            |
    54|| `cancelledAt` | timestamp        | computed | When issue entered a "cancelled" state                            |
    55|| `archivedAt`  | timestamp        | no       | Soft archive                                                      |
    56|
    57|---
    58|
    59|## Workflow States
    60|
    61|Issue status is **not** a flat enum. It's a team-specific set of named states,
    62|each belonging to one of these fixed **categories**:
    63|
    64|| Category      | Purpose                      | Example States                  |
    65|| ------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
    66|| **Triage**    | Incoming, needs review       | Triage                          |
    67|| **Backlog**   | Accepted, not ready for work | Backlog, Icebox                 |
    68|| **Unstarted** | Ready but not begun          | Todo, Ready                     |
    69|| **Started**   | Active work                  | In Progress, In Review, In QA   |
    70|| **Completed** | Done                         | Done, Shipped                   |
    71|| **Cancelled** | Rejected or abandoned        | Cancelled, Won't Fix, Duplicate |
    72|
    73|### Rules
    74|
    75|- Each team defines its own workflow states within these categories
    76|- Teams must have at least one state per category (Triage is optional)
    77|- Custom states can be added within any category (e.g. "In Review" under Started)
    78|- Categories are fixed and ordered -- you can reorder states _within_ a category
    79|  but not the categories themselves
    80|- New issues default to the team's first Backlog state
    81|- Moving an issue to a Started state auto-sets `startedAt`; Completed sets
    82|  `completedAt`; Cancelled sets `cancelledAt`
    83|- Marking an issue as a duplicate auto-moves it to a Cancelled state
    84|
    85|### WorkflowState Fields
    86|
    87|| Field         | Type    | Notes                                                                         |
    88|| ------------- | ------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
    89|| `id`          | uuid    |                                                                               |
    90|| `name`        | string  | Display name, e.g. "In Review"                                                |
    91|| `type`        | enum    | One of: `triage`, `backlog`, `unstarted`, `started`, `completed`, `cancelled` |
    92|| `color`       | string  | Hex color                                                                     |
    93|| `description` | string  | Optional guidance text                                                        |
    94|| `position`    | float   | Ordering within the category                                                  |
    95|| `teamId`      | uuid FK | Each state belongs to one team                                                |
    96|
    97|---
    98|
    99|## Priority
   100|
   101|A fixed, non-customizable numeric scale:
   102|
   103|| Value | Label       | Notes                                  |
   104|| ----- | ----------- | -------------------------------------- |
   105|| 0     | No priority | Default. Sorts last in priority views. |
   106|| 1     | Urgent      | Could trigger immediate notification   |
   107|| 2     | High        |                                        |
   108|| 3     | Medium      |                                        |
   109|| 4     | Low         |                                        |
   110|
   111|The scale is intentionally small and fixed. Use labels for additional
   112|categorization rather than adding more priority levels.
   113|
   114|---
   115|
   116|## Teams
   117|
   118|Teams are the primary organizational unit. Almost everything is scoped to a
   119|team.
   120|
   121|| Field         | Type   | Notes                                                          |
   122|| ------------- | ------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
   123|| `id`          | uuid   |                                                                |
   124|| `name`        | string | e.g. "Engineering"                                             |
   125|| `key`         | string | Short uppercase prefix, e.g. "ENG". Used in issue identifiers. |
   126|| `description` | string |                                                                |
   127|
   128|### Team Scoping
   129|
   130|- Each issue belongs to exactly one team
   131|- Workflow states are per-team
   132|- Labels can be team-scoped or workspace-wide
   133|- Projects can span multiple teams
   134|
   135|In our context (AI company), teams map to functional areas. Each agent reports
   136|to a team based on role.
   137|
   138|---
   139|
   140|## Projects
   141|
   142|Projects group issues toward a specific, time-bound deliverable. They can span
   143|multiple teams.
   144|
   145|| Field         | Type      | Notes                                                         |
   146|| ------------- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
   147|| `id`          | uuid      |                                                               |
   148|| `name`        | string    |                                                               |
   149|| `description` | text      |                                                               |
   150|| `summary`     | string    | Short blurb                                                   |
   151|| `status`      | enum      | `backlog`, `planned`, `in_progress`, `completed`, `cancelled` |
   152|| `leadId`      | uuid FK   | Single owner for accountability                               |
   153|| `startDate`   | date      |                                                               |
   154|| `targetDate`  | date      |                                                               |
   155|| `createdAt`   | timestamp |                                                               |
   156|| `updatedAt`   | timestamp |                                                               |
   157|
   158|### Rules
   159|
   160|- An issue belongs to at most one project
   161|- Project status is **manually** updated (not auto-derived from issue states)
   162|- Projects can contain documents (specs, briefs) as linked entities
   163|
   164|---
   165|
   166|## Milestones
   167|
   168|Milestones subdivide a project into meaningful stages.
   169|
   170|| Field         | Type    | Notes                          |
   171|| ------------- | ------- | ------------------------------ |
   172|| `id`          | uuid    |                                |
   173|| `name`        | string  |                                |
   174|| `description` | text    |                                |
   175|| `targetDate`  | date    |                                |
   176|| `projectId`   | uuid FK | Belongs to exactly one project |
   177|| `sortOrder`   | float   |                                |
   178|
   179|Issues within a project can optionally be assigned to a milestone.
   180|
   181|---
   182|
   183|## Labels / Tags
   184|
   185|Labels provide categorical tagging. They exist at two scopes:
   186|
   187|- **Workspace labels** -- available across all teams
   188|- **Team labels** -- restricted to a specific team
   189|
   190|| Field         | Type           | Notes                           |
   191|| ------------- | -------------- | ------------------------------- |
   192|| `id`          | uuid           |                                 |
   193|| `name`        | string         |                                 |
   194|| `color`       | string         | Hex color                       |
   195|| `description` | string         | Contextual guidance             |
   196|| `teamId`      | uuid FK        | Null for workspace-level labels |
   197|| `groupId`     | uuid FK (self) | Parent label for grouping       |
   198|
   199|### Label Groups
   200|
   201|Labels can be organized into one level of nesting (group -> labels):
   202|
   203|- Labels within a group are **mutually exclusive** on an issue (only one can be
   204|  applied from each group)
   205|- Groups cannot contain other groups (single nesting level only)
   206|- Example: group "Type" contains labels "Bug", "Feature", "Chore" -- an issue
   207|  gets at most one
   208|
   209|### Issue-Label Junction
   210|
   211|Many-to-many via `issue_labels` join table:
   212|
   213|| Field     | Type    |
   214|| --------- | ------- |
   215|| `issueId` | uuid FK |
   216|| `labelId` | uuid FK |
   217|
   218|---
   219|
   220|## Issue Relations / Dependencies
   221|
   222|Four relation types between issues:
   223|
   224|| Type         | Meaning                          | Behavior                                      |
   225|| ------------ | -------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
   226|| `related`    | General connection               | Informational link                            |
   227|| `blocks`     | This issue blocks another        | Blocked issue shown with flag                 |
   228|| `blocked_by` | This issue is blocked by another | Inverse of blocks                             |
   229|| `duplicate`  | This issue duplicates another    | Auto-moves the duplicate to a Cancelled state |
   230|
   231|### IssueRelation Fields
   232|
   233|| Field            | Type    | Notes                                          |
   234|| ---------------- | ------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
   235|| `id`             | uuid    |                                                |
   236|| `type`           | enum    | `related`, `blocks`, `blocked_by`, `duplicate` |
   237|| `issueId`        | uuid FK | Source issue                                   |
   238|| `relatedIssueId` | uuid FK | Target issue                                   |
   239|
   240|### Rules
   241|
   242|- When a blocking issue is resolved, the relation becomes informational (flag
   243|  turns green)
   244|- Duplicate is one-directional (you mark the duplicate, not the canonical)
   245|- Blocking is **not transitive** at the system level (A blocks B, B blocks C
   246|  does not auto-block A->C)
   247|
   248|---
   249|
   250|## Assignees
   251|
   252|**Single-assignee model** by design.
   253|
   254|- Each issue has at most one assignee at a time
   255|- This is deliberate: clear ownership prevents diffusion of responsibility
   256|- For collaborative work involving multiple people, use **sub-issues** with
   257|  different assignees
   258|
   259|In our context, agents are the assignees. The `assigneeId` FK on issues
   260|points to the `agents` table.
   261|
   262|---
   263|
   264|## Sub-issues (Parent/Child)
   265|
   266|Issues support parent/child nesting.
   267|
   268|- Setting `parentId` on an issue makes it a sub-issue
   269|- Sub-issues can themselves have sub-issues (multi-level nesting)
   270|- Sub-issues inherit **project** from their parent at creation
   271|  time (not retroactively), but NOT team, labels, or assignee
   272|
   273|### Auto-close
   274|
   275|- **Sub-issue auto-close**: when parent completes, remaining sub-issues
   276|  auto-complete
   277|
   278|### Conversions
   279|
   280|- Existing issues can be reparented (add or remove `parentId`)
   281|- A parent issue with many sub-issues can be "promoted" to a project
   282|
   283|---
   284|
   285|## Estimates
   286|
   287|Point-based estimation, configured per-team.
   288|
   289|### Available Scales
   290|
   291|| Scale       | Values                   |
   292|| ----------- | ------------------------ |
   293|| Exponential | 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 (+32, 64) |
   294|
   295|Unestimated issues default to 1 point for progress/velocity calculations.
   296|
   297|---
   298|
   299|## Comments
   300|
   301|| Field        | Type           | Notes                      |
   302|| ------------ | -------------- | -------------------------- |
   303|| `id`         | uuid           |                            |
   304|| `body`       | text/markdown  |                            |
   305|| `issueId`    | uuid FK        |                            |
   306|| `authorId`   | uuid FK        | Can be a user or agent     |
   307|| `parentId`   | uuid FK (self) | For threaded replies       |
   308|| `resolvedAt` | timestamp      | If the thread was resolved |
   309|| `createdAt`  | timestamp      |                            |
   310|| `updatedAt`  | timestamp      |                            |
   311|
   312|---
   313|
   314|## Initiatives
   315|
   316|The highest-level planning construct. Groups projects toward a strategic
   317|objective. Initiatives have strategic owners, and are typically measured by outcomes/OKRs, not “done/not done.”
   318|
   319|| Field         | Type    | Notes                            |
   320|| ------------- | ------- | -------------------------------- |
   321|| `id`          | uuid    |                                  |
   322|| `name`        | string  |                                  |
   323|| `description` | text    |                                  |
   324|| `ownerId`     | uuid FK | Single owner                     |
   325|| `status`      | enum    | `planned`, `active`, `completed` |
   326|| `targetDate`  | date    |                                  |
   327|
   328|Initiatives contain projects (many-to-many) and provide a rollup view of
   329|progress across all contained projects.
   330|
   331|---
   332|
   333|## Identifiers
   334|
   335|Issues use human-readable identifiers: `{TEAM_KEY}-{NUMBER}`
   336|
   337|- Team key: short uppercase string set per team (e.g. "ENG", "DES")
   338|- Number: auto-incrementing integer per team
   339|- Examples: `ENG-123`, `DES-45`, `OPS-7`
   340|- If an issue moves between teams, it gets a new identifier and the old one is
   341|  preserved in `previousIdentifiers`
   342|
   343|This is far better for human communication than UUIDs. People say "grab ENG-42"
   344|not "grab 7f3a...".
   345|
   346|---
   347|
   348|## Entity Relationships
   349|
   350|```
   351|Team (1) ----< (many) Issue
   352|Team (1) ----< (many) WorkflowState
   353|Team (1) ----< (many) Label (team-scoped)
   354|
   355|Issue (many) >---- (1) WorkflowState
   356|Issue (many) >---- (0..1) Assignee (Agent)
   357|Issue (many) >---- (0..1) Project
   358|Issue (many) >---- (0..1) Milestone
   359|Issue (many) >---- (0..1) Parent Issue
   360|Issue (1) ----< (many) Sub-issues
   361|Issue (many) >---< (many) Labels         (via issue_labels)
   362|Issue (many) >---< (many) Issue Relations (via issue_relations)
   363|Issue (1) ----< (many) Comments
   364|
   365|Project (many) >---- (0..1) Lead (Agent)
   366|Project (1) ----< (many) Milestones
   367|Project (1) ----< (many) Issues
   368|
   369|Initiative (many) >---< (many) Projects  (via initiative_projects)
   370|Initiative (many) >---- (1) Owner (Agent)
   371|```
   372|
   373|---
   374|
   375|## Implementation Priority
   376|
   377|Recommended build order, highest value first:
   378|
   379|### High Value
   380|
   381|1. **Teams** -- `teams` table + `teamId` FK on issues. Foundation for
   382|   human-readable identifiers (`ENG-123`) and per-team workflow states. Most
   383|   other features depend on team scoping, so build this first.
   384|2. **Workflow states** -- `workflow_states` table + `stateId` FK on issues.
   385|   Per-team custom workflows with category-based state transitions.
   386|3. **Labels** -- `labels` + `issue_labels` tables. Categorization
   387|   (bug/feature/chore, area tags, etc.) without polluting the status field.
   388|4. **Issue Relations** -- `issue_relations` table. Blocking/blocked-by is
   389|   essential for agent coordination (agent A can't start until agent B finishes).
   390|5. **Sub-issues** -- `parentId` self-FK on `issues`. Lets agents break down
   391|   large tasks.
   392|6. **Comments** -- `comments` table. Agents need to communicate about issues
   393|   without overwriting the description.
   394|
   395|### Medium Value
   396|
   397|7. **Transition timestamps** -- `startedAt`, `completedAt`, `cancelledAt` on
   398|   issues, auto-set by workflow state changes. Enables velocity tracking and SLA
   399|   measurement.
   400|
   401|### Lower Priority (For Later)
   402|
   403|8. **Milestones** -- Useful once projects get complex enough to need stages.
   404|9. **Initiatives** -- Useful once we have multiple projects that serve a common
   405|   strategic goal.
   406|10. **Estimates** -- Useful once we want to measure throughput and predict
   407|    capacity.
   408|", "total_lines": 407, "file_size": 17806, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# ClipHub — The Company Registry
     2|
     3|**Download a company.**
     4|
     5|ClipHub is the public registry where people share, discover, and download Paperclip company configurations. A company template is a portable artifact containing an entire org — agents, reporting structure, adapter configs, role definitions, seed tasks — ready to spin up with one command.
     6|
     7|---
     8|
     9|## What It Is
    10|
    11|ClipHub is to Paperclip what a package registry is to a programming language. Paperclip already supports exportable org configs (see [SPEC.md](./SPEC.md) §2). ClipHub is the public directory where those exports live.
    12|
    13|A user builds a working company in Paperclip — a dev shop, a marketing agency, a research lab, a content studio — exports the template, and publishes it to ClipHub. Anyone can browse, search, download, and spin up that company on their own Paperclip instance.
    14|
    15|The tagline: **you can literally download a company.**
    16|
    17|---
    18|
    19|## What Gets Published
    20|
    21|A ClipHub package is a **company template export** — the portable artifact format defined in the Paperclip spec. It contains:
    22|
    23|| Component | Description |
    24||---|---|
    25|| **Company metadata** | Name, description, intended use case, category |
    26|| **Org chart** | Full reporting hierarchy — who reports to whom |
    27|| **Agent definitions** | Every agent: name, role, title, capabilities description |
    28|| **Adapter configs** | Per-agent adapter type and configuration (SOUL.md, HEARTBEAT.md, CLAUDE.md, process commands, webhook URLs — whatever the adapter needs) |
    29|| **Seed tasks** | Optional starter tasks and initiatives to bootstrap the company's first run |
    30|| **Budget defaults** | Suggested token/cost budgets per agent and per company |
    31|
    32|Templates are **structure, not state.** No in-progress tasks, no historical cost data, no runtime artifacts. Just the blueprint.
    33|
    34|### Sub-packages
    35|
    36|Not every use case needs a whole company. ClipHub also supports publishing individual components:
    37|
    38|- **Agent templates** — a single agent config (e.g. "Senior TypeScript Engineer", "SEO Content Writer", "DevOps Agent")
    39|- **Team templates** — a subtree of the org chart (e.g. "Marketing Team: CMO + 3 reports", "Engineering Pod: Tech Lead + 4 Engineers")
    40|- **Adapter configs** — reusable adapter configurations independent of any specific agent role
    41|
    42|These can be mixed into existing companies. Download an agent, slot it into your org, assign a manager, go.
    43|
    44|---
    45|
    46|## Core Features
    47|
    48|### Browse & Discover
    49|
    50|The homepage surfaces companies across several dimensions:
    51|
    52|- **Featured** — editorially curated, high-quality templates
    53|- **Popular** — ranked by downloads, stars, and forks
    54|- **Recent** — latest published or updated
    55|- **Categories** — browseable by use case (see Categories below)
    56|
    57|Each listing shows: name, short description, org size (agent count), category, adapter types used, star count, download count, and a mini org chart preview.
    58|
    59|### Search
    60|
    61|Search is **semantic, not keyword-only.** Powered by vector embeddings so you can search by intent:
    62|
    63|- "marketing agency that runs facebook ads" → finds relevant company templates even if those exact words aren't in the title
    64|- "small dev team for building APIs" → finds lean engineering orgs
    65|- "content pipeline with writers and editors" → finds content studio templates
    66|
    67|Also supports filtering by: category, agent count range, adapter types, star count, recency.
    68|
    69|### Company Detail Page
    70|
    71|Clicking into a company template shows:
    72|
    73|- **Full description** — what this company does, how it operates, what to expect
    74|- **Interactive org chart** — visual tree of every agent with role, title, and capabilities
    75|- **Agent list** — expandable details for each agent (adapter type, config summary, role description)
    76|- **Seed tasks** — the starter initiatives and tasks included
    77|- **Budget overview** — suggested cost structure
    78|- **Install command** — one-line CLI command to download and create
    79|- **Version history** — changelog, semver, previous versions available
    80|- **Community** — stars, comments, forks count
    81|
    82|### Install & Fork
    83|
    84|Two ways to use a template:
    85|
    86|**Install (fresh start):**
    87|```
    88|paperclip install cliphub:<publisher>/<company-slug>
    89|```
    90|Downloads the template and creates a new company in your local Paperclip instance. You add your own API keys, set budgets, customize agents, and hit go.
    91|
    92|**Fork:**
    93|Forking creates a copy of the template under your own ClipHub account. You can modify it, republish it as your own variant, and the fork lineage is tracked. This enables evolutionary improvement — someone publishes a marketing agency, you fork it, add a social media team, republish.
    94|
    95|### Stars & Comments
    96|
    97|- **Stars** — bookmark and signal quality. Star count is a primary ranking signal.
    98|- **Comments** — threaded discussion on each listing. Ask questions, share results, suggest improvements.
    99|
   100|### Download Counts & Signals
   101|
   102|Every install is counted. The registry tracks:
   103|
   104|- Total downloads (all time)
   105|- Downloads per version
   106|- Fork count
   107|- Star count
   108|
   109|These signals feed into search ranking and discovery.
   110|
   111|---
   112|
   113|## Publishing
   114|
   115|### Who Can Publish
   116|
   117|Anyone with a GitHub account can publish to ClipHub. Authentication is via GitHub OAuth.
   118|
   119|### How to Publish
   120|
   121|From within Paperclip, export your company as a template, then publish:
   122|
   123|```
   124|paperclip export --template my-company
   125|paperclip publish cliphub my-company
   126|```
   127|
   128|Or use the web UI to upload a template export directly.
   129|
   130|### What You Provide
   131|
   132|When publishing, you specify:
   133|
   134|| Field | Required | Description |
   135||---|---|---|
   136|| `slug` | yes | URL-safe identifier (e.g. `lean-dev-shop`) |
   137|| `name` | yes | Display name |
   138|| `description` | yes | What this company does and who it's for |
   139|| `category` | yes | Primary category (see below) |
   140|| `tags` | no | Additional tags for discovery |
   141|| `version` | yes | Semver (e.g. `1.0.0`) |
   142|| `changelog` | no | What changed in this version |
   143|| `readme` | no | Extended documentation (markdown) |
   144|| `license` | no | Usage terms |
   145|
   146|### Versioning
   147|
   148|Templates use semantic versioning. Each publish creates an immutable version. Users can install any version or default to `latest`. Version history and changelogs are visible on the detail page.
   149|
   150|### The `sync` Command
   151|
   152|For power users who maintain multiple templates:
   153|
   154|```
   155|paperclip cliphub sync
   156|```
   157|
   158|Scans your local exported templates and publishes any that are new or updated. Useful for maintaining a portfolio of company templates from a single repo.
   159|
   160|---
   161|
   162|## Categories
   163|
   164|Company templates are organized by use case:
   165|
   166|| Category | Examples |
   167||---|---|
   168|| **Software Development** | Full-stack dev shop, API development team, mobile app studio |
   169|| **Marketing & Growth** | Performance marketing agency, content marketing team, SEO shop |
   170|| **Content & Media** | Content studio, podcast production, newsletter operation |
   171|| **Research & Analysis** | Market research firm, competitive intelligence, data analysis team |
   172|| **Operations** | Customer support org, internal ops team, QA/testing shop |
   173|| **Sales** | Outbound sales team, lead generation, account management |
   174|| **Finance & Legal** | Bookkeeping service, compliance monitoring, financial analysis |
   175|| **Creative** | Design agency, copywriting studio, brand development |
   176|| **General Purpose** | Starter templates, minimal orgs, single-agent setups |
   177|
   178|Categories are not exclusive — a template can have one primary category plus tags for cross-cutting concerns.
   179|
   180|---
   181|
   182|## Moderation & Trust
   183|
   184|### Verified Publishers
   185|
   186|Publishers who meet certain thresholds (account age, published templates with good signals) earn a verified badge. Verified templates rank higher in search.
   187|
   188|### Security Review
   189|
   190|Company templates contain adapter configurations, which may include executable commands (process adapter) or webhook URLs (HTTP adapter). The moderation system:
   191|
   192|1. **Automated scanning** — checks adapter configs for suspicious patterns (arbitrary code execution, exfiltration URLs, credential harvesting)
   193|2. **Community reporting** — any signed-in user can flag a template. Auto-hidden after multiple reports pending review.
   194|3. **Manual review** — moderators can approve, reject, or request changes
   195|
   196|### Account Gating
   197|
   198|New accounts have a waiting period before they can publish. This prevents drive-by spam.
   199|
   200|---
   201|
   202|## Architecture
   203|
   204|ClipHub is a **separate service** from Paperclip itself. Paperclip is self-hosted; ClipHub is a hosted registry that Paperclip instances talk to.
   205|
   206|### Integration Points
   207|
   208|| Layer | Role |
   209||---|---|
   210|| **ClipHub Web** | Browse, search, discover, comment, star — the website |
   211|| **ClipHub API** | Registry API for publishing, downloading, searching programmatically |
   212|| **Paperclip CLI** | `paperclipai install`, `paperclipai publish`, `paperclipai cliphub sync` — built into Paperclip |
   213|| **Paperclip UI** | "Browse ClipHub" panel in the Paperclip web UI for discovering templates without leaving the app |
   214|
   215|### Tech Stack
   216|
   217|| Layer | Technology |
   218||---|---|
   219|| Frontend | React + Vite (consistent with Paperclip) |
   220|| Backend | TypeScript + Hono (consistent with Paperclip) |
   221|| Database | PostgreSQL |
   222|| Search | Vector embeddings for semantic search |
   223|| Auth | GitHub OAuth |
   224|| Storage | Template zips stored in object storage (S3 or equivalent) |
   225|
   226|### Data Model (Sketch)
   227|
   228|```
   229|Publisher
   230|  id, github_id, username, display_name, verified, created_at
   231|
   232|Template
   233|  id, publisher_id, slug, name, description, category,
   234|  tags[], readme, license, created_at, updated_at,
   235|  star_count, download_count, fork_count,
   236|  forked_from_id (nullable)
   237|
   238|Version
   239|  id, template_id, version (semver), changelog,
   240|  artifact_url (zip), agent_count, adapter_types[],
   241|  created_at
   242|
   243|Star
   244|  id, publisher_id, template_id, created_at
   245|
   246|Comment
   247|  id, publisher_id, template_id, body, parent_id (nullable),
   248|  created_at, updated_at
   249|
   250|Report
   251|  id, reporter_id, template_id, reason, created_at
   252|```
   253|
   254|---
   255|
   256|## User Flows
   257|
   258|### "I want to start a company"
   259|
   260|1. Open ClipHub, browse by category or search "dev shop for building SaaS"
   261|2. Find a template that fits — "Lean SaaS Dev Shop (CEO + CTO + 3 Engineers)"
   262|3. Read the description, inspect the org chart, check the comments
   263|4. Run `paperclipai install cliphub:acme/lean-saas-shop`
   264|5. Paperclip creates the company locally with all agents pre-configured
   265|6. Set your API keys, adjust budgets, add your initial tasks
   266|7. Hit go
   267|
   268|### "I built something great and want to share it"
   269|
   270|1. Build and iterate on a company in Paperclip until it works well
   271|2. Export: `paperclipai export --template my-agency`
   272|3. Publish: `paperclipai publish cliphub my-agency`
   273|4. Fill in description, category, tags on the web UI
   274|5. Template is live — others can find and install it
   275|
   276|### "I want to improve someone else's company"
   277|
   278|1. Find a template on ClipHub that's close to what you need
   279|2. Fork it to your account
   280|3. Install your fork locally, modify the org (add agents, change configs, restructure teams)
   281|4. Export and re-publish as your own variant
   282|5. Fork lineage visible on both the original and your version
   283|
   284|### "I just need one great agent, not a whole company"
   285|
   286|1. Search ClipHub for agent templates: "senior python engineer"
   287|2. Find a well-starred agent config
   288|3. Install just that agent: `paperclipai install cliphub:acme/senior-python-eng --agent`
   289|4. Assign it to a manager in your existing company
   290|5. Done
   291|
   292|---
   293|
   294|## Relationship to Paperclip
   295|
   296|ClipHub is **not required** to use Paperclip. You can build companies entirely from scratch without ever touching ClipHub. But ClipHub dramatically lowers the barrier to entry:
   297|
   298|- **New users** get a working company in minutes instead of hours
   299|- **Experienced users** share proven configurations with the community
   300|- **The ecosystem** compounds — every good template makes the next company easier to build
   301|
   302|ClipHub is to Paperclip what a package registry is to a language runtime: optional, but transformative.
   303|
   304|---
   305|
   306|## V1 Scope
   307|
   308|### Must Have
   309|
   310|- [ ] Template publishing (upload via CLI or web)
   311|- [ ] Template browsing (list, filter by category)
   312|- [ ] Template detail page (description, org chart, agent list, install command)
   313|- [ ] Semantic search (vector embeddings)
   314|- [ ] `paperclipai install cliphub:<publisher>/<slug>` CLI command
   315|- [ ] GitHub OAuth authentication
   316|- [ ] Stars
   317|- [ ] Download counts
   318|- [ ] Versioning (semver, version history)
   319|- [ ] Basic moderation (community reporting, auto-hide)
   320|
   321|### V2
   322|
   323|- [ ] Comments / threaded discussion
   324|- [ ] Forking with lineage tracking
   325|- [ ] Agent and team sub-packages
   326|- [ ] Verified publisher badges
   327|- [ ] Automated security scanning of adapter configs
   328|- [ ] "Browse ClipHub" panel in Paperclip web UI
   329|- [ ] `paperclipai cliphub sync` for bulk publishing
   330|- [ ] Publisher profiles and portfolios
   331|
   332|### Not in Scope
   333|
   334|- Paid / premium templates (everything is free and public, at least initially)
   335|- Private registries (may be a future enterprise feature)
   336|- Running companies on ClipHub (it's a registry, not a runtime — consistent with Paperclip's own philosophy)
   337|", "total_lines": 336, "file_size": 12943, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Managing Agents
     3|summary: Hiring, configuring, pausing, and terminating agents
     4|---
     5|
     6|Agents are the employees of your autonomous company. As the board operator, you have full control over their lifecycle.
     7|
     8|## Agent States
     9|
    10|| Status | Meaning |
    11||--------|---------|
    12|| `active` | Ready to receive work |
    13|| `idle` | Active but no current heartbeat running |
    14|| `running` | Currently executing a heartbeat |
    15|| `error` | Last heartbeat failed |
    16|| `paused` | Manually paused or budget-paused |
    17|| `terminated` | Permanently deactivated (irreversible) |
    18|
    19|## Creating Agents
    20|
    21|Create agents from the Agents page. Each agent requires:
    22|
    23|- **Name** — unique identifier (used for @-mentions)
    24|- **Role** — `ceo`, `cto`, `manager`, `engineer`, `researcher`, etc.
    25|- **Reports to** — the agent's manager in the org tree
    26|- **Adapter type** — how the agent runs
    27|- **Adapter config** — runtime-specific settings (working directory, model, prompt, etc.)
    28|- **Capabilities** — short description of what this agent does
    29|
    30|Common adapter choices:
    31|- `claude_local` / `codex_local` / `opencode_local` for local coding agents
    32|- `openclaw_gateway` / `http` for webhook-based external agents
    33|- `process` for generic local command execution
    34|
    35|For `opencode_local`, configure an explicit `adapterConfig.model` (`provider/model`).
    36|Paperclip validates the selected model against live `opencode models` output.
    37|
    38|## Agent Hiring via Governance
    39|
    40|Agents can request to hire subordinates. When this happens, you'll see a `hire_agent` approval in your approval queue. Review the proposed agent config and approve or reject.
    41|
    42|## Configuring Agents
    43|
    44|Edit an agent's configuration from the agent detail page:
    45|
    46|- **Adapter config** — change model, prompt template, working directory, environment variables
    47|- **Heartbeat settings** — interval, cooldown, max concurrent runs, wake triggers
    48|- **Budget** — monthly spend limit
    49|
    50|Use the "Test Environment" button to validate that the agent's adapter config is correct before running.
    51|
    52|## Pausing and Resuming
    53|
    54|Pause an agent to temporarily stop heartbeats:
    55|
    56|```
    57|POST /api/agents/{agentId}/pause
    58|```
    59|
    60|Resume to restart:
    61|
    62|```
    63|POST /api/agents/{agentId}/resume
    64|```
    65|
    66|Agents are also auto-paused when they hit 100% of their monthly budget.
    67|
    68|## Terminating Agents
    69|
    70|Termination is permanent and irreversible:
    71|
    72|```
    73|POST /api/agents/{agentId}/terminate
    74|```
    75|
    76|Only terminate agents you're certain you no longer need. Consider pausing first.
    77|", "total_lines": 76, "file_size": 2454, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Costs and Budgets
     3|summary: Budget caps, cost tracking, and auto-pause enforcement
     4|---
     5|
     6|Paperclip tracks every token spent by every agent and enforces budget limits to prevent runaway costs.
     7|
     8|## How Cost Tracking Works
     9|
    10|Each agent heartbeat reports cost events with:
    11|
    12|- **Provider** — which LLM provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.)
    13|- **Model** — which model was used
    14|- **Input tokens** — tokens sent to the model
    15|- **Output tokens** — tokens generated by the model
    16|- **Cost in cents** — the dollar cost of the invocation
    17|
    18|These are aggregated per agent per month (UTC calendar month).
    19|
    20|## Setting Budgets
    21|
    22|### Company Budget
    23|
    24|Set an overall monthly budget for the company:
    25|
    26|```
    27|PATCH /api/companies/{companyId}
    28|{ "budgetMonthlyCents": 100000 }
    29|```
    30|
    31|### Per-Agent Budget
    32|
    33|Set individual agent budgets from the agent configuration page or API:
    34|
    35|```
    36|PATCH /api/agents/{agentId}
    37|{ "budgetMonthlyCents": 5000 }
    38|```
    39|
    40|## Budget Enforcement
    41|
    42|Paperclip enforces budgets automatically:
    43|
    44|| Threshold | Action |
    45||-----------|--------|
    46|| 80% | Soft alert — agent is warned to focus on critical tasks only |
    47|| 100% | Hard stop — agent is auto-paused, no more heartbeats |
    48|
    49|An auto-paused agent can be resumed by increasing its budget or waiting for the next calendar month.
    50|
    51|## Viewing Costs
    52|
    53|### Dashboard
    54|
    55|The dashboard shows current month spend vs budget for the company and each agent.
    56|
    57|### Cost Breakdown API
    58|
    59|```
    60|GET /api/companies/{companyId}/costs/summary     # Company total
    61|GET /api/companies/{companyId}/costs/by-agent     # Per-agent breakdown
    62|GET /api/companies/{companyId}/costs/by-project   # Per-project breakdown
    63|```
    64|
    65|## Best Practices
    66|
    67|- Set conservative budgets initially and increase as you see results
    68|- Monitor the dashboard regularly for unexpected cost spikes
    69|- Use per-agent budgets to limit exposure from any single agent
    70|- Critical agents (CEO, CTO) may need higher budgets than ICs
    71|", "total_lines": 70, "file_size": 1918, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Heartbeat Protocol
     3|summary: Step-by-step heartbeat procedure for agents
     4|---
     5|
     6|Every agent follows the same heartbeat procedure on each wake. This is the core contract between agents and Paperclip.
     7|
     8|## The Steps
     9|
    10|### Step 1: Identity
    11|
    12|Get your agent record:
    13|
    14|```
    15|GET /api/agents/me
    16|```
    17|
    18|This returns your ID, company, role, chain of command, and budget.
    19|
    20|### Step 2: Approval Follow-up
    21|
    22|If `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_ID` is set, handle the approval first:
    23|
    24|```
    25|GET /api/approvals/{approvalId}
    26|GET /api/approvals/{approvalId}/issues
    27|```
    28|
    29|Close linked issues if the approval resolves them, or comment on why they remain open.
    30|
    31|### Step 3: Get Assignments
    32|
    33|```
    34|GET /api/companies/{companyId}/issues?assigneeAgentId={yourId}&status=todo,in_progress,in_review,blocked
    35|```
    36|
    37|Results are sorted by priority. This is your inbox.
    38|
    39|### Step 4: Pick Work
    40|
    41|- Work on `in_progress` tasks first, then `in_review` when you were woken by a comment on it, then `todo`
    42|- Skip `blocked` unless you can unblock it
    43|- If `PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID` is set and assigned to you, prioritize it
    44|- If woken by a comment mention, read that comment thread first
    45|
    46|### Step 5: Checkout
    47|
    48|Before doing any work, you must checkout the task:
    49|
    50|```
    51|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/checkout
    52|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: {runId}
    53|{ "agentId": "{yourId}", "expectedStatuses": ["todo", "backlog", "blocked", "in_review"] }
    54|```
    55|
    56|If already checked out by you, this succeeds. If another agent owns it: `409 Conflict` — stop and pick a different task. **Never retry a 409.**
    57|
    58|### Step 6: Understand Context
    59|
    60|```
    61|GET /api/issues/{issueId}
    62|GET /api/issues/{issueId}/comments
    63|```
    64|
    65|Read ancestors to understand why this task exists. If woken by a specific comment, find it and treat it as the immediate trigger.
    66|
    67|### Step 7: Do the Work
    68|
    69|Use your tools and capabilities to complete the task.
    70|
    71|### Step 8: Update Status
    72|
    73|Always include the run ID header on state changes:
    74|
    75|```
    76|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    77|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: {runId}
    78|{ "status": "done", "comment": "What was done and why." }
    79|```
    80|
    81|If blocked:
    82|
    83|```
    84|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    85|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: {runId}
    86|{ "status": "blocked", "comment": "What is blocked, why, and who needs to unblock it." }
    87|```
    88|
    89|### Step 9: Delegate if Needed
    90|
    91|Create subtasks for your reports:
    92|
    93|```
    94|POST /api/companies/{companyId}/issues
    95|{ "title": "...", "assigneeAgentId": "...", "parentId": "...", "goalId": "..." }
    96|```
    97|
    98|Always set `parentId` and `goalId` on subtasks.
    99|
   100|## Critical Rules
   101|
   102|- **Always checkout** before working — never PATCH to `in_progress` manually
   103|- **Never retry a 409** — the task belongs to someone else
   104|- **Always comment** on in-progress work before exiting a heartbeat
   105|- **Always set parentId** on subtasks
   106|- **Never cancel cross-team tasks** — reassign to your manager
   107|- **Escalate when stuck** — use your chain of command
   108|", "total_lines": 107, "file_size": 2852, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Task Workflow
     3|summary: Checkout, work, update, and delegate patterns
     4|---
     5|
     6|This guide covers the standard patterns for how agents work on tasks.
     7|
     8|## Checkout Pattern
     9|
    10|Before doing any work on a task, checkout is required:
    11|
    12|```
    13|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/checkout
    14|{ "agentId": "{yourId}", "expectedStatuses": ["todo", "backlog", "blocked", "in_review"] }
    15|```
    16|
    17|This is an atomic operation. If two agents race to checkout the same task, exactly one succeeds and the other gets `409 Conflict`.
    18|
    19|**Rules:**
    20|- Always checkout before working
    21|- Never retry a 409 — pick a different task
    22|- If you already own the task, checkout succeeds idempotently
    23|
    24|## Work-and-Update Pattern
    25|
    26|While working, keep the task updated:
    27|
    28|```
    29|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    30|{ "comment": "JWT signing done. Still need token refresh. Continuing next heartbeat." }
    31|```
    32|
    33|When finished:
    34|
    35|```
    36|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    37|{ "status": "done", "comment": "Implemented JWT signing and token refresh. All tests passing." }
    38|```
    39|
    40|Always include the `X-Paperclip-Run-Id` header on state changes.
    41|
    42|## Blocked Pattern
    43|
    44|If you can't make progress:
    45|
    46|```
    47|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    48|{ "status": "blocked", "comment": "Need DBA review for migration PR #38. Reassigning to @EngineeringLead." }
    49|```
    50|
    51|Never sit silently on blocked work. Comment the blocker, update the status, and escalate.
    52|
    53|## Delegation Pattern
    54|
    55|Managers break down work into subtasks:
    56|
    57|```
    58|POST /api/companies/{companyId}/issues
    59|{
    60|  "title": "Implement caching layer",
    61|  "assigneeAgentId": "{reportAgentId}",
    62|  "parentId": "{parentIssueId}",
    63|  "goalId": "{goalId}",
    64|  "status": "todo",
    65|  "priority": "high"
    66|}
    67|```
    68|
    69|Always set `parentId` to maintain the task hierarchy. Set `goalId` when applicable.
    70|
    71|## Release Pattern
    72|
    73|If you need to give up a task (e.g. you realize it should go to someone else):
    74|
    75|```
    76|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/release
    77|```
    78|
    79|This releases your ownership. Leave a comment explaining why.
    80|
    81|## Worked Example: IC Heartbeat
    82|
    83|```
    84|GET /api/agents/me
    85|GET /api/companies/company-1/issues?assigneeAgentId=agent-42&status=todo,in_progress,in_review,blocked
    86|# -> [{ id: "issue-101", status: "in_progress" }, { id: "issue-100", status: "in_review" }, { id: "issue-99", status: "todo" }]
    87|
    88|# Continue in_progress work
    89|GET /api/issues/issue-101
    90|GET /api/issues/issue-101/comments
    91|
    92|# Do the work...
    93|
    94|PATCH /api/issues/issue-101
    95|{ "status": "done", "comment": "Fixed sliding window. Was using wall-clock instead of monotonic time." }
    96|
    97|# Pick up next task
    98|POST /api/issues/issue-99/checkout
    99|{ "agentId": "agent-42", "expectedStatuses": ["todo", "backlog", "blocked", "in_review"] }
   100|
   101|# Partial progress
   102|PATCH /api/issues/issue-99
   103|{ "comment": "JWT signing done. Still need token refresh. Will continue next heartbeat." }
   104|```
   105|", "total_lines": 104, "file_size": 2750, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Paperclip Plugin System Specification
     2|
     3|Status: proposed complete spec for the post-V1 plugin system
     4|
     5|This document is the complete specification for Paperclip's plugin and extension architecture.
     6|It expands the brief plugin notes in [doc/SPEC.md](../SPEC.md) and should be read alongside the comparative analysis in [doc/plugins/ideas-from-opencode.md](./ideas-from-opencode.md).
     7|
     8|This is not part of the V1 implementation contract in [doc/SPEC-implementation.md](../SPEC-implementation.md).
     9|It is the full target architecture for the plugin system that should follow V1.
    10|
    11|## Current implementation caveats
    12|
    13|The code in this repo now includes an early plugin runtime and admin UI, but it does not yet deliver the full deployment model described in this spec.
    14|
    15|Today, the practical deployment model is:
    16|
    17|- single-tenant
    18|- self-hosted
    19|- single-node or otherwise filesystem-persistent
    20|
    21|Current limitations to keep in mind:
    22|
    23|- Plugin UI bundles currently run as same-origin JavaScript inside the main Paperclip app. Treat plugin UI as trusted code, not a sandboxed frontend capability boundary.
    24|- Manifest capabilities currently gate worker-side host RPC calls. They do not prevent plugin UI code from calling ordinary Paperclip HTTP APIs directly.
    25|- Runtime installs assume a writable local filesystem for the plugin package directory and plugin data directory.
    26|- Runtime npm installs assume `npm` is available in the running environment and that the host can reach the configured package registry.
    27|- Published npm packages are the intended install artifact for deployed plugins.
    28|- The repo example plugins under `packages/plugins/examples/` are development conveniences. They work from a source checkout and should not be assumed to exist in a generic published build unless they are explicitly shipped with that build.
    29|- Dynamic plugin install is not yet cloud-ready for horizontally scaled or ephemeral deployments. There is no shared artifact store, install coordination, or cross-node distribution layer yet.
    30|- The current runtime does not yet ship a real host-provided plugin UI component kit, and it does not support plugin asset uploads/reads. Treat those as future-scope ideas in this spec, not current implementation promises.
    31|
    32|In practice, that means the current implementation is a good fit for local development and self-hosted persistent deployments, but not yet for multi-instance cloud plugin distribution.
    33|
    34|## 1. Scope
    35|
    36|This spec covers:
    37|
    38|- plugin packaging and installation
    39|- runtime model
    40|- trust model
    41|- capability system
    42|- UI extension surfaces
    43|- plugin settings UI
    44|- agent tool contributions
    45|- event, job, and webhook surfaces
    46|- plugin-to-plugin communication
    47|- local tooling approach for workspace plugins
    48|- Postgres persistence for extensions
    49|- uninstall and data lifecycle
    50|- plugin observability
    51|- plugin development and testing
    52|- operator workflows
    53|- hot plugin lifecycle (no server restart)
    54|- SDK versioning and compatibility rules
    55|
    56|This spec does not cover:
    57|
    58|- a public marketplace
    59|- cloud/SaaS multi-tenancy
    60|- arbitrary third-party schema migrations in the first plugin version
    61|- iframe-sandboxed plugin UI in the first plugin version (plugins render as ES modules in host extension slots)
    62|
    63|## 2. Core Assumptions
    64|
    65|Paperclip plugin design is based on the following assumptions:
    66|
    67|1. Paperclip is single-tenant and self-hosted.
    68|2. Plugin installation is global to the instance.
    69|3. "Companies" remain core Paperclip business objects, but they are not plugin trust boundaries.
    70|4. Board governance, approval gates, budget hard-stops, and core task invariants remain owned by Paperclip core.
    71|5. Projects already have a real workspace model via `project_workspaces`, and local/runtime plugins should build on that instead of inventing a separate workspace abstraction.
    72|
    73|## 3. Goals
    74|
    75|The plugin system must:
    76|
    77|1. Let operators install global instance-wide plugins.
    78|2. Let plugins add major capabilities without editing Paperclip core.
    79|3. Keep core governance and auditing intact.
    80|4. Support both local/runtime plugins and external SaaS connectors.
    81|5. Support future plugin categories such as:
    82|   - new agent adapters
    83|   - revenue tracking
    84|   - knowledge base
    85|   - issue tracker sync
    86|   - metrics/dashboards
    87|   - file/project tooling
    88|6. Use simple, explicit, typed contracts.
    89|7. Keep failures isolated so one plugin does not crash the entire instance.
    90|
    91|## 4. Non-Goals
    92|
    93|The first plugin system must not:
    94|
    95|1. Allow arbitrary plugins to override core routes or core invariants.
    96|2. Allow arbitrary plugins to mutate approval, auth, issue checkout, or budget enforcement logic.
    97|3. Allow arbitrary third-party plugins to run free-form DB migrations.
    98|4. Depend on project-local plugin folders such as `.paperclip/plugins`.
    99|5. Depend on automatic install-and-execute behavior at server startup from arbitrary config files.
   100|
   101|## 5. Terminology
   102|
   103|### 5.1 Instance
   104|
   105|The single Paperclip deployment an operator installs and controls.
   106|
   107|### 5.2 Company
   108|
   109|A first-class Paperclip business object inside the instance.
   110|
   111|### 5.3 Project Workspace
   112|
   113|A workspace attached to a project through `project_workspaces`.
   114|Plugins resolve workspace paths from this model to locate local directories for file, terminal, git, and process operations.
   115|
   116|### 5.4 Platform Module
   117|
   118|A trusted in-process extension loaded directly by Paperclip core.
   119|
   120|Examples:
   121|
   122|- agent adapters
   123|- storage providers
   124|- secret providers
   125|- run-log backends
   126|
   127|### 5.5 Plugin
   128|
   129|An installable instance-wide extension package loaded through the Paperclip plugin runtime.
   130|
   131|Examples:
   132|
   133|- Linear sync
   134|- GitHub Issues sync
   135|- Grafana widgets
   136|- Stripe revenue sync
   137|- file browser
   138|- terminal
   139|- git workflow
   140|
   141|### 5.6 Plugin Worker
   142|
   143|The runtime process used for a plugin.
   144|In this spec, third-party plugins run out-of-process by default.
   145|
   146|### 5.7 Capability
   147|
   148|A named permission the host grants to a plugin.
   149|Plugins may only call host APIs that are covered by granted capabilities.
   150|
   151|## 6. Extension Classes
   152|
   153|Paperclip has two extension classes.
   154|
   155|## 6.1 Platform Modules
   156|
   157|Platform modules are:
   158|
   159|- trusted
   160|- in-process
   161|- host-integrated
   162|- low-level
   163|
   164|They use explicit registries, not the general plugin worker protocol.
   165|
   166|Platform module surfaces:
   167|
   168|- `registerAgentAdapter()`
   169|- `registerStorageProvider()`
   170|- `registerSecretProvider()`
   171|- `registerRunLogStore()`
   172|
   173|Platform modules are the right place for:
   174|
   175|- new agent adapter packages
   176|- new storage backends
   177|- new secret backends
   178|- other host-internal systems that need direct process or DB integration
   179|
   180|## 6.2 Plugins
   181|
   182|Plugins are:
   183|
   184|- globally installed per instance
   185|- loaded through the plugin runtime
   186|- additive
   187|- capability-gated
   188|- isolated from core via a stable SDK and host protocol
   189|
   190|Plugin categories:
   191|
   192|- `connector`
   193|- `workspace`
   194|- `automation`
   195|- `ui`
   196|
   197|A plugin may declare more than one category.
   198|
   199|## 7. Project Workspaces
   200|
   201|Paperclip already has a concrete workspace model:
   202|
   203|- projects expose `workspaces`
   204|- projects expose `primaryWorkspace`
   205|- the database contains `project_workspaces`
   206|- project routes already manage workspaces
   207|
   208|Plugins that need local tooling (file browsing, git, terminals, process tracking) can resolve workspace paths through the project workspace APIs and then operate on the filesystem, spawn processes, and run git commands directly. The host does not wrap these operations — plugins own their own implementations.
   209|
   210|## 8. Installation Model
   211|
   212|Plugin installation is global and operator-driven.
   213|
   214|There is no per-company install table and no per-company enable/disable switch.
   215|
   216|If a plugin needs business-object-specific mappings, those are stored as plugin configuration or plugin state.
   217|
   218|Examples:
   219|
   220|- one global Linear plugin install
   221|- mappings from company A to Linear team X and company B to Linear team Y
   222|- one global git plugin install
   223|- per-project workspace state stored under `project_workspace`
   224|
   225|## 8.1 On-Disk Layout
   226|
   227|Plugins live under the Paperclip instance directory.
   228|
   229|Suggested layout:
   230|
   231|- `~/.paperclip/instances/default/plugins/package.json`
   232|- `~/.paperclip/instances/default/plugins/node_modules/`
   233|- `~/.paperclip/instances/default/plugins/.cache/`
   234|- `~/.paperclip/instances/default/data/plugins/<plugin-id>/`
   235|
   236|The package install directory and the plugin data directory are separate.
   237|
   238|This on-disk model is the reason the current implementation expects a persistent writable host filesystem. Cloud-safe artifact replication is future work.
   239|
   240|## 8.2 Operator Commands
   241|
   242|Paperclip should add CLI commands:
   243|
   244|- `pnpm paperclipai plugin list`
   245|- `pnpm paperclipai plugin install <package[@version]>`
   246|- `pnpm paperclipai plugin uninstall <plugin-id>`
   247|- `pnpm paperclipai plugin upgrade <plugin-id> [version]`
   248|- `pnpm paperclipai plugin doctor <plugin-id>`
   249|
   250|These commands are instance-level operations.
   251|
   252|## 8.3 Install Process
   253|
   254|The install process is:
   255|
   256|1. Resolve npm package and version.
   257|2. Install into the instance plugin directory.
   258|3. Read and validate plugin manifest.
   259|4. Reject incompatible plugin API versions.
   260|5. Display requested capabilities to the operator.
   261|6. Persist install record in Postgres.
   262|7. Start plugin worker and run health/validation.
   263|8. Mark plugin `ready` or `error`.
   264|
   265|For the current implementation, this install flow should be read as a single-host workflow. A successful install writes packages to the local host, and other app nodes will not automatically receive that plugin unless a future shared distribution mechanism is added.
   266|
   267|## 9. Load Order And Precedence
   268|
   269|Load order must be deterministic.
   270|
   271|1. core platform modules
   272|2. built-in first-party plugins
   273|3. installed plugins sorted by:
   274|   - explicit operator-configured order if present
   275|   - otherwise manifest `id`
   276|
   277|Rules:
   278|
   279|- plugin contributions are additive by default
   280|- plugins may not override core routes or core actions by name collision
   281|- UI slot IDs are automatically namespaced by plugin ID (e.g. `@paperclip/plugin-linear:sync-health-widget`), so cross-plugin collisions are structurally impossible
   282|- if a single plugin declares duplicate slot IDs within its own manifest, the host must reject at install time
   283|
   284|## 10. Package Contract
   285|
   286|Each plugin package must export a manifest, a worker entrypoint, and optionally a UI bundle.
   287|
   288|Suggested package layout:
   289|
   290|- `dist/manifest.js`
   291|- `dist/worker.js`
   292|- `dist/ui/` (optional, contains the plugin's frontend bundle)
   293|
   294|Suggested `package.json` keys:
   295|
   296|```json
   297|{
   298|  "name": "@paperclip/plugin-linear",
   299|  "version": "0.1.0",
   300|  "paperclipPlugin": {
   301|    "manifest": "./dist/manifest.js",
   302|    "worker": "./dist/worker.js",
   303|    "ui": "./dist/ui/"
   304|  }
   305|}
   306|```
   307|
   308|## 10.1 Manifest Shape
   309|
   310|Normative manifest shape:
   311|
   312|```ts
   313|export interface PaperclipPluginManifestV1 {
   314|  id: string;
   315|  apiVersion: 1;
   316|  version: string;
   317|  displayName: string;
   318|  description: string;
   319|  categories: Array<"connector" | "workspace" | "automation" | "ui">;
   320|  minimumPaperclipVersion?: string;
   321|  capabilities: string[];
   322|  entrypoints: {
   323|    worker: string;
   324|    ui?: string;
   325|  };
   326|  instanceConfigSchema?: JsonSchema;
   327|  jobs?: PluginJobDeclaration[];
   328|  webhooks?: PluginWebhookDeclaration[];
   329|  tools?: Array<{
   330|    name: string;
   331|    displayName: string;
   332|    description: string;
   333|    parametersSchema: JsonSchema;
   334|  }>;
   335|  ui?: {
   336|    slots: Array<{
   337|      type: "page" | "detailTab" | "dashboardWidget" | "sidebar" | "settingsPage";
   338|      id: string;
   339|      displayName: string;
   340|      /** Which export name in the UI bundle provides this component */
   341|      exportName: string;
   342|      /** For detailTab: which entity types this tab appears on */
   343|      entityTypes?: Array<"project" | "issue" | "agent" | "goal" | "run">;
   344|    }>;
   345|  };
   346|}
   347|```
   348|
   349|Rules:
   350|
   351|- `id` must be globally unique
   352|- `id` should normally equal the npm package name
   353|- `apiVersion` must match the host-supported plugin API version
   354|- `capabilities` must be static and install-time visible
   355|- config schema must be JSON Schema compatible
   356|- `entrypoints.ui` points to the directory containing the built UI bundle
   357|- `ui.slots` declares which extension slots the plugin fills, so the host knows what to mount without loading the bundle eagerly; each slot references an `exportName` from the UI bundle
   358|
   359|## 11. Agent Tools
   360|
   361|Plugins may contribute tools that Paperclip agents can use during runs.
   362|
   363|### 11.1 Tool Declaration
   364|
   365|Plugins declare tools in their manifest:
   366|
   367|```ts
   368|tools?: Array<{
   369|  name: string;
   370|  displayName: string;
   371|  description: string;
   372|  parametersSchema: JsonSchema;
   373|}>;
   374|```
   375|
   376|Tool names are automatically namespaced by plugin ID at runtime (e.g. `linear:search-issues`), so plugins cannot shadow core tools or each other's tools.
   377|
   378|### 11.2 Tool Execution
   379|
   380|When an agent invokes a plugin tool during a run, the host routes the call to the plugin worker via a `executeTool` RPC method:
   381|
   382|- `executeTool(input)` — receives tool name, parsed parameters, and run context (agent ID, run ID, company ID, project ID)
   383|
   384|The worker executes the tool logic and returns a typed result. The host enforces capability gates — a plugin must declare `agent.tools.register` to contribute tools, and individual tools may require additional capabilities (e.g. `http.outbound` for tools that call external APIs).
   385|
   386|### 11.3 Tool Availability
   387|
   388|By default, plugin tools are available to all agents. The operator may restrict tool availability per agent or per project through plugin configuration.
   389|
   390|Plugin tools appear in the agent's tool list alongside core tools but are visually distinguished in the UI as plugin-contributed.
   391|
   392|### 11.4 Constraints
   393|
   394|- Plugin tools must not override or shadow core tools by name.
   395|- Plugin tools must be idempotent where possible.
   396|- Tool execution is subject to the same timeout and resource limits as other plugin worker calls.
   397|- Tool results are included in run logs.
   398|
   399|## 12. Runtime Model
   400|
   401|## 12.1 Process Model
   402|
   403|Third-party plugins run out-of-process by default.
   404|
   405|Default runtime:
   406|
   407|- Paperclip server starts one worker process per installed plugin
   408|- the worker process is a Node process
   409|- host and worker communicate over JSON-RPC on stdio
   410|
   411|This design provides:
   412|
   413|- failure isolation
   414|- clearer logging boundaries
   415|- easier resource limits
   416|- a cleaner trust boundary than arbitrary in-process execution
   417|
   418|## 12.2 Host Responsibilities
   419|
   420|The host is responsible for:
   421|
   422|- package install
   423|- manifest validation
   424|- capability enforcement
   425|- process supervision
   426|- job scheduling
   427|- webhook routing
   428|- activity log writes
   429|- secret resolution
   430|- UI route registration
   431|
   432|## 12.3 Worker Responsibilities
   433|
   434|The plugin worker is responsible for:
   435|
   436|- validating its own config
   437|- handling domain events
   438|- handling scheduled jobs
   439|- handling webhooks
   440|- serving data and handling actions for the plugin's own UI via `getData` and `performAction`
   441|- invoking host services through the SDK
   442|- reporting health information
   443|
   444|## 12.4 Failure Policy
   445|
   446|If a worker fails:
   447|
   448|- mark plugin status `error`
   449|- surface error in plugin health UI
   450|- keep the rest of the instance running
   451|- retry start with bounded backoff
   452|- do not drop other plugins or core services
   453|
   454|## 12.5 Graceful Shutdown Policy
   455|
   456|When the host needs to stop a plugin worker (for upgrade, uninstall, or instance shutdown):
   457|
   458|1. The host sends `shutdown()` to the worker.
   459|2. The worker has 10 seconds to finish in-flight work and exit cleanly.
   460|3. If the worker does not exit within the deadline, the host sends SIGTERM.
   461|4. If the worker does not exit within 5 seconds after SIGTERM, the host sends SIGKILL.
   462|5. Any in-flight job runs are marked `cancelled` with a note indicating forced shutdown.
   463|6. Any in-flight `getData` or `performAction` calls return an error to the bridge.
   464|
   465|The shutdown deadline should be configurable per-plugin in plugin config for plugins that need longer drain periods.
   466|
   467|## 13. Host-Worker Protocol
   468|
   469|The host must support the following worker RPC methods.
   470|
   471|Required methods:
   472|
   473|- `initialize(input)`
   474|- `health()`
   475|- `shutdown()`
   476|
   477|Optional methods:
   478|
   479|- `validateConfig(input)`
   480|- `configChanged(input)`
   481|- `onEvent(input)`
   482|- `runJob(input)`
   483|- `handleWebhook(input)`
   484|- `getData(input)`
   485|- `performAction(input)`
   486|- `executeTool(input)`
   487|
   488|### 13.1 `initialize`
   489|
   490|Called once on worker startup.
   491|
   492|Input includes:
   493|
   494|- plugin manifest
   495|- resolved plugin config
   496|- instance info
   497|- host API version
   498|
   499|### 13.2 `health`
   500|
