If the worker does not implement this method, the host restarts the worker process with the new config (graceful shutdown then restart). 526| 527|### 13.5 `onEvent` 528| 529|Receives one typed Paperclip domain event. 530| 531|Delivery semantics: 532| 533|- at least once 534|- plugin must be idempotent 535|- no global ordering guarantee across all event types 536|- per-entity ordering is best effort but not guaranteed after retries 537| 538|### 13.6 `runJob` 539| 540|Runs a declared scheduled job. 541| 542|The host provides: 543| 544|- job key 545|- trigger source 546|- run id 547|- schedule metadata 548| 549|### 13.7 `handleWebhook` 550| 551|Receives inbound webhook payload routed by the host. 552| 553|The host provides: 554| 555|- endpoint key 556|- headers 557|- raw body 558|- parsed body if applicable 559|- request id 560| 561|### 13.8 `getData` 562| 563|Returns plugin data requested by the plugin's own UI components. 564| 565|The plugin UI calls the host bridge, which forwards the request to the worker. The worker returns typed JSON that the plugin's own frontend components render. 566| 567|Input includes: 568| 569|- data key (plugin-defined, e.g. `"sync-health"`, `"issue-detail"`) 570|- context (company id, project id, entity id, etc.) 571|- optional query parameters 572| 573|### 13.9 `performAction` 574| 575|Runs an explicit plugin action initiated by the board UI. 576| 577|Examples: 578| 579|- "resync now" 580|- "link GitHub issue" 581|- "create branch from issue" 582|- "restart process" 583| 584|### 13.10 `executeTool` 585| 586|Runs a plugin-contributed agent tool during a run. 587| 588|The host provides: 589| 590|- tool name (without plugin namespace prefix) 591|- parsed parameters matching the tool's declared schema 592|- run context: agent ID, run ID, company ID, project ID 593| 594|The worker executes the tool and returns a typed result (string content, structured data, or error). 595| 596|## 14. SDK Surface 597| 598|Plugins do not talk to the DB directly. 599|Plugins do not read raw secret material from persisted config. 600| 601|The SDK exposed to workers must provide typed host clients. 602| 603|Required SDK clients: 604| 605|- `ctx.config` 606|- `ctx.events` 607|- `ctx.jobs` 608|- `ctx.http` 609|- `ctx.secrets` 610|- `ctx.assets` 611|- `ctx.activity` 612|- `ctx.state` 613|- `ctx.entities` 614|- `ctx.projects` 615|- `ctx.issues` 616|- `ctx.agents` 617|- `ctx.goals` 618|- `ctx.data` 619|- `ctx.actions` 620|- `ctx.tools` 621|- `ctx.logger` 622| 623|`ctx.data` and `ctx.actions` register handlers that the plugin's own UI calls through the host bridge. `ctx.data.register(key, handler)` backs `usePluginData(key)` on the frontend. `ctx.actions.register(key, handler)` backs `usePluginAction(key)`. 624| 625|Plugins that need filesystem, git, terminal, or process operations handle those directly using standard Node APIs or libraries. The host provides project workspace metadata through `ctx.projects` so plugins can resolve workspace paths, but the host does not proxy low-level OS operations. 626| 627|## 14.1 Example SDK Shape 628| 629|```ts 630|/** Top-level helper for defining a plugin with type checking */ 631|export function definePlugin(definition: PluginDefinition): PaperclipPlugin; 632| 633|/** Re-exported from Zod for config schema definitions */ 634|export { z } from "zod"; 635| 636|export interface PluginContext { 637| manifest: PaperclipPluginManifestV1; 638| config: { 639| get(): Promise>; 640| }; 641| events: { 642| on(name: string, fn: (event: unknown) => Promise): void; 643| on(name: string, filter: EventFilter, fn: (event: unknown) => Promise): void; 644| emit(name: string, payload: unknown): Promise; 645| }; 646| jobs: { 647| register(key: string, input: { cron: string }, fn: (job: PluginJobContext) => Promise): void; 648| }; 649| state: { 650| get(input: ScopeKey): Promise; 651| set(input: ScopeKey, value: unknown): Promise; 652| delete(input: ScopeKey): Promise; 653| }; 654| entities: { 655| upsert(input: PluginEntityUpsert): Promise; 656| list(input: PluginEntityQuery): Promise; 657| }; 658| data: { 659| register(key: string, handler: (params: Record) => Promise): void; 660| }; 661| actions: { 662| register(key: string, handler: (params: Record) => Promise): void; 663| }; 664| tools: { 665| register(name: string, input: PluginToolDeclaration, fn: (params: unknown, runCtx: ToolRunContext) => Promise): void; 666| }; 667| logger: { 668| info(message: string, meta?: Record): void; 669| warn(message: string, meta?: Record): void; 670| error(message: string, meta?: Record): void; 671| debug(message: string, meta?: Record): void; 672| }; 673|} 674| 675|export interface EventFilter { 676| projectId?: string; 677| companyId?: string; 678| agentId?: string; 679| [key: string]: unknown; 680|} 681|``` 682| 683|## 15. Capability Model 684| 685|Capabilities are mandatory and static. 686|Every plugin declares them up front. 687| 688|The host enforces capabilities in the SDK layer and refuses calls outside the granted set. 689| 690|## 15.1 Capability Categories 691| 692|### Data Read 693| 694|- `companies.read` 695|- `projects.read` 696|- `project.workspaces.read` 697|- `issues.read` 698|- `issue.comments.read` 699|- `agents.read` 700|- `goals.read` 701|- `activity.read` 702|- `costs.read` 703| 704|### Data Write 705| 706|- `issues.create` 707|- `issues.update` 708|- `issue.comments.create` 709|- `assets.write` 710|- `assets.read` 711|- `activity.log.write` 712|- `metrics.write` 713| 714|### Plugin State 715| 716|- `plugin.state.read` 717|- `plugin.state.write` 718| 719|### Runtime / Integration 720| 721|- `events.subscribe` 722|- `events.emit` 723|- `jobs.schedule` 724|- `webhooks.receive` 725|- `http.outbound` 726|- `secrets.read-ref` 727| 728|### Agent Tools 729| 730|- `agent.tools.register` 731| 732|### UI 733| 734|- `instance.settings.register` 735|- `ui.sidebar.register` 736|- `ui.page.register` 737|- `ui.detailTab.register` 738|- `ui.dashboardWidget.register` 739|- `ui.action.register` 740| 741|## 15.2 Forbidden Capabilities 742| 743|The host must not expose capabilities for: 744| 745|- approval decisions 746|- budget override 747|- auth bypass 748|- issue checkout lock override 749|- direct DB access 750| 751|## 15.3 Upgrade Rules 752| 753|If a plugin upgrade adds capabilities: 754| 755|1. the host must mark the plugin `upgrade_pending` 756|2. the operator must explicitly approve the new capability set 757|3. the new version does not become `ready` until approval completes 758| 759|## 16. Event System 760| 761|The host must emit typed domain events that plugins may subscribe to. 762| 763|Minimum event set: 764| 765|- `company.created` 766|- `company.updated` 767|- `project.created` 768|- `project.updated` 769|- `project.workspace_created` 770|- `project.workspace_updated` 771|- `project.workspace_deleted` 772|- `issue.created` 773|- `issue.updated` 774|- `issue.comment.created` 775|- `agent.created` 776|- `agent.updated` 777|- `agent.status_changed` 778|- `agent.run.started` 779|- `agent.run.finished` 780|- `agent.run.failed` 781|- `agent.run.cancelled` 782|- `approval.created` 783|- `approval.decided` 784|- `cost_event.created` 785|- `activity.logged` 786| 787|Each event must include: 788| 789|- event id 790|- event type 791|- occurred at 792|- actor metadata when applicable 793|- primary entity metadata 794|- typed payload 795| 796|### 16.1 Event Filtering 797| 798|Plugins may provide an optional filter when subscribing to events. The filter is evaluated by the host before dispatching to the worker, so filtered-out events never cross the process boundary. 799| 800|Supported filter fields: 801| 802|- `projectId` — only receive events for a specific project 803|- `companyId` — only receive events for a specific company 804|- `agentId` — only receive events for a specific agent 805| 806|Filters are optional. If omitted, the plugin receives all events of the subscribed type. Filters may be combined (e.g. filter by both company and project). 807| 808|### 16.2 Plugin-to-Plugin Events 809| 810|Plugins may emit custom events using `ctx.events.emit(name, payload)`. Plugin-emitted events use a namespaced event type: `plugin..`. 811| 812|Other plugins may subscribe to these events using the same `ctx.events.on()` API: 813| 814|```ts 815|ctx.events.on("plugin.@paperclip/plugin-git.push-detected", async (event) => { 816| // react to the git plugin detecting a push 817|}); 818|``` 819| 820|Rules: 821| 822|- Plugin events require the `events.emit` capability. 823|- Plugin events are not core domain events — they do not appear in the core activity log unless the emitting plugin explicitly logs them. 824|- Plugin events follow the same at-least-once delivery semantics as core events. 825|- The host must not allow plugins to emit events in the core namespace (events without the `plugin.` prefix). 826| 827|## 17. Scheduled Jobs 828| 829|Plugins may declare scheduled jobs in their manifest. 830| 831|Job rules: 832| 833|1. Each job has a stable `job_key`. 834|2. The host is the scheduler of record. 835|3. The host prevents overlapping execution of the same plugin/job combination unless explicitly allowed later. 836|4. Every job run is recorded in Postgres. 837|5. Failed jobs are retryable. 838| 839|## 18. Webhooks 840| 841|Plugins may declare webhook endpoints in their manifest. 842| 843|Webhook route shape: 844| 845|- `POST /api/plugins/:pluginId/webhooks/:endpointKey` 846| 847|Rules: 848| 849|1. The host owns the public route. 850|2. The worker receives the request body through `handleWebhook`. 851|3. Signature verification happens in plugin code using secret refs resolved by the host. 852|4. Every delivery is recorded. 853|5. Webhook handling must be idempotent. 854| 855|## 19. UI Extension Model 856| 857|Plugins ship their own frontend UI as a bundled React module. The host loads plugin UI into designated extension slots and provides a bridge for the plugin frontend to communicate with its own worker backend and with host APIs. 858| 859|### How Plugin UI Publishing Works In Practice 860| 861|A plugin's `dist/ui/` directory contains a built React bundle. The host serves this bundle and loads it into the page when the user navigates to a plugin surface (a plugin page, a detail tab, a dashboard widget, etc.). 862| 863|**The host provides, the plugin renders:** 864| 865|1. The host defines **extension slots** — designated mount points in the UI where plugin components can appear (pages, tabs, widgets, sidebar entries, action bars). 866|2. The plugin's UI bundle exports named components for each slot it wants to fill. 867|3. The host mounts the plugin component into the slot, passing it a **host bridge** object. 868|4. The plugin component uses the bridge to fetch data from its own worker (via `getData`), call actions (via `performAction`), read host context (current company, project, entity), and use shared host UI primitives (design tokens, common components). 869| 870|**Concrete example: a Linear plugin ships a dashboard widget.** 871| 872|The plugin's UI bundle exports: 873| 874|```tsx 875|// dist/ui/index.tsx 876|import { usePluginData, usePluginAction, MetricCard, StatusBadge } from "@paperclipai/plugin-sdk/ui"; 877| 878|export function DashboardWidget({ context }: PluginWidgetProps) { 879| const { data, loading } = usePluginData("sync-health", { companyId: context.companyId }); 880| const resync = usePluginAction("resync"); 881| 882| if (loading) return ; 883| 884| return ( 885|
886| 887| {data.mappings.map(m => ( 888| 889| ))} 890| 891|
892| ); 893|} 894|``` 895| 896|**What happens at runtime:** 897| 898|1. User opens the dashboard. The host sees that the Linear plugin registered a `DashboardWidget` export. 899|2. The host mounts the plugin's `DashboardWidget` component into the dashboard widget slot, passing `context` (current company, user, etc.) and the bridge. 900|3. `usePluginData("sync-health", ...)` calls through the bridge → host → plugin worker's `getData` RPC → returns JSON → the plugin component renders it however it wants. 901|4. When the user clicks "Resync Now", `usePluginAction("resync")` calls through the bridge → host → plugin worker's `performAction` RPC. 902| 903|**What the host controls:** 904| 905|- The host decides **where** plugin components appear (which slots exist and when they mount). 906|- The host provides the **bridge** — plugin UI cannot make arbitrary network requests or access host internals directly. 907|- The host enforces **capability gates** — if a plugin's worker does not have a capability, the bridge rejects the call even if the UI requests it. 908|- The host provides **design tokens and shared components** via `@paperclipai/plugin-sdk/ui` so plugins can match the host's visual language without being forced to. 909| 910|**What the plugin controls:** 911| 912|- The plugin decides **how** to render its data — it owns its React components, layout, interactions, and state management. 913|- The plugin decides **what data** to fetch and **what actions** to expose. 914|- The plugin can use any React patterns (hooks, context, third-party component libraries) inside its bundle. 915| 916|### 19.0.1 Plugin UI SDK (`@paperclipai/plugin-sdk/ui`) 917| 918|The SDK includes a `ui` subpath export that plugin frontends import. This subpath provides: 919| 920|- **Bridge hooks**: `usePluginData(key, params)`, `usePluginAction(key)`, `useHostContext()` 921|- **Design tokens**: colors, spacing, typography, shadows matching the host theme 922|- **Shared components**: `MetricCard`, `StatusBadge`, `DataTable`, `LogView`, `ActionBar`, `Spinner`, etc. 923|- **Type definitions**: `PluginPageProps`, `PluginWidgetProps`, `PluginDetailTabProps` 924| 925|Plugins are encouraged but not required to use the shared components. A plugin may render entirely custom UI as long as it communicates through the bridge. 926| 927|### 19.0.2 Bundle Isolation 928| 929|Plugin UI bundles are loaded as standard ES modules, not iframed. This gives plugins full rendering performance and access to the host's design tokens. 930| 931|Isolation rules: 932| 933|- Plugin bundles must not import from host internals. They may only import from `@paperclipai/plugin-sdk/ui` and their own dependencies. 934|- Plugin bundles must not access `window.fetch` or `XMLHttpRequest` directly for host API calls. All host communication goes through the bridge. 935|- The host may enforce Content Security Policy rules that restrict plugin network access to the bridge endpoint only. 936|- Plugin bundles must be statically analyzable — no dynamic `import()` of URLs outside the plugin's own bundle. 937| 938|If stronger isolation is needed later, the host can move to iframe-based mounting for untrusted plugins without changing the plugin's source code (the bridge API stays the same). 939| 940|### 19.0.3 Bundle Serving 941| 942|Plugin UI bundles must be pre-built ESM. The host does not compile or transform plugin UI code at runtime. 943| 944|The host serves the plugin's `dist/ui/` directory as static assets under a namespaced path: 945| 946|- `/_plugins/:pluginId/ui/*` 947| 948|When the host renders an extension slot, it dynamically imports the plugin's UI entry module from this path, resolves the named export declared in `ui.slots[].exportName`, and mounts it into the slot. 949| 950|In development, the host may support a `devUiUrl` override in plugin config that points to a local dev server (e.g. Vite) so plugin authors can use hot-reload during development without rebuilding. 951| 952|## 19.1 Global Operator Routes 953| 954|- `/settings/plugins` 955|- `/settings/plugins/:pluginId` 956| 957|These routes are instance-level. 958| 959|## 19.2 Company-Context Routes 960| 961|- `/:companyPrefix/plugins/:pluginId` 962| 963|These routes exist because the board UI is organized around companies even though plugin installation is global. 964| 965|## 19.3 Detail Tabs 966| 967|Plugins may add tabs to: 968| 969|- project detail 970|- issue detail 971|- agent detail 972|- goal detail 973|- run detail 974| 975|Recommended route pattern: 976| 977|- `/:companyPrefix//:id?tab=` 978| 979|## 19.4 Dashboard Widgets 980| 981|Plugins may add cards or sections to the dashboard. 982| 983|## 19.5 Sidebar Entries 984| 985|Plugins may add sidebar links to: 986| 987|- global plugin settings 988|- company-context plugin pages 989| 990|## 19.6 Shared Components In `@paperclipai/plugin-sdk/ui` 991| 992|The host SDK ships shared components that plugins can import to quickly build UIs that match the host's look and feel. These are convenience building blocks, not a requirement. 993| 994|| Component | What it renders | Typical use | 995||---|---|---| 996|| `MetricCard` | Single number with label, optional trend/sparkline | KPIs, counts, rates | 997|| `StatusBadge` | Inline status indicator (ok/warning/error/info) | Sync health, connection status | 998|| `DataTable` | Rows and columns with optional sorting and pagination | Issue lists, job history, process lists | 999|| `TimeseriesChart` | Line or bar chart with timestamped data points | Revenue trends, sync volume, error rates | 1000|| `MarkdownBlock` | Rendered markdown text | Descriptions, help text, notes | 1001|", "total_lines": 1644, "file_size": 59192, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=1001 to continue reading (showing 501-1000 of 1644 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Plugin Authoring Guide
     2|
     3|This guide describes the current, implemented way to create a Paperclip plugin in this repo.
     4|
     5|It is intentionally narrower than [PLUGIN_SPEC.md](./PLUGIN_SPEC.md). The spec includes future ideas; this guide only covers the alpha surface that exists now.
     6|
     7|## Current reality
     8|
     9|- Treat plugin workers and plugin UI as trusted code.
    10|- Plugin UI runs as same-origin JavaScript inside the main Paperclip app.
    11|- Worker-side host APIs are capability-gated.
    12|- Plugin UI is not sandboxed by manifest capabilities.
    13|- There is no host-provided shared React component kit for plugins yet.
    14|- `ctx.assets` is not supported in the current runtime.
    15|
    16|## Scaffold a plugin
    17|
    18|Use the scaffold package:
    19|
    20|```bash
    21|pnpm --filter @paperclipai/create-paperclip-plugin build
    22|node packages/plugins/create-paperclip-plugin/dist/index.js @yourscope/plugin-name --output ./packages/plugins/examples
    23|```
    24|
    25|For a plugin that lives outside the Paperclip repo:
    26|
    27|```bash
    28|pnpm --filter @paperclipai/create-paperclip-plugin build
    29|node packages/plugins/create-paperclip-plugin/dist/index.js @yourscope/plugin-name \
    30|  --output /absolute/path/to/plugin-repos \
    31|  --sdk-path /absolute/path/to/paperclip/packages/plugins/sdk
    32|```
    33|
    34|That creates a package with:
    35|
    36|- `src/manifest.ts`
    37|- `src/worker.ts`
    38|- `src/ui/index.tsx`
    39|- `tests/plugin.spec.ts`
    40|- `esbuild.config.mjs`
    41|- `rollup.config.mjs`
    42|
    43|Inside this monorepo, the scaffold uses `workspace:*` for `@paperclipai/plugin-sdk`.
    44|
    45|Outside this monorepo, the scaffold snapshots `@paperclipai/plugin-sdk` from the local Paperclip checkout into a `.paperclip-sdk/` tarball so you can build and test a plugin without publishing anything to npm first.
    46|
    47|## Recommended local workflow
    48|
    49|From the generated plugin folder:
    50|
    51|```bash
    52|pnpm install
    53|pnpm typecheck
    54|pnpm test
    55|pnpm build
    56|```
    57|
    58|For local development, install it into Paperclip from an absolute local path through the plugin manager or API. The server supports local filesystem installs and watches local-path plugins for file changes so worker restarts happen automatically after rebuilds.
    59|
    60|Example:
    61|
    62|```bash
    63|curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:3100/api/plugins/install \
    64|  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    65|  -d '{"packageName":"/absolute/path/to/your-plugin","isLocalPath":true}'
    66|```
    67|
    68|## Supported alpha surface
    69|
    70|Worker:
    71|
    72|- config
    73|- events
    74|- jobs
    75|- launchers
    76|- http
    77|- secrets
    78|- activity
    79|- state
    80|- entities
    81|- projects and project workspaces
    82|- companies
    83|- issues and comments
    84|- agents and agent sessions
    85|- goals
    86|- data/actions
    87|- streams
    88|- tools
    89|- metrics
    90|- logger
    91|
    92|UI:
    93|
    94|- `usePluginData`
    95|- `usePluginAction`
    96|- `usePluginStream`
    97|- `usePluginToast`
    98|- `useHostContext`
    99|- typed slot props from `@paperclipai/plugin-sdk/ui`
   100|
   101|Mount surfaces currently wired in the host include:
   102|
   103|- `page`
   104|- `settingsPage`
   105|- `dashboardWidget`
   106|- `sidebar`
   107|- `sidebarPanel`
   108|- `detailTab`
   109|- `taskDetailView`
   110|- `projectSidebarItem`
   111|- `globalToolbarButton`
   112|- `toolbarButton`
   113|- `contextMenuItem`
   114|- `commentAnnotation`
   115|- `commentContextMenuItem`
   116|
   117|## Company routes
   118|
   119|Plugins may declare a `page` slot with `routePath` to own a company route like:
   120|
   121|```text
   122|/:companyPrefix/<routePath>
   123|```
   124|
   125|Rules:
   126|
   127|- `routePath` must be a single lowercase slug
   128|- it cannot collide with reserved host routes
   129|- it cannot duplicate another installed plugin page route
   130|
   131|## Publishing guidance
   132|
   133|- Use npm packages as the deployment artifact.
   134|- Treat repo-local example installs as a development workflow only.
   135|- Prefer keeping plugin UI self-contained inside the package.
   136|- Do not rely on host design-system components or undocumented app internals.
   137|- GitHub repository installs are not a first-class workflow today. For local development, use a checked-out local path. For production, publish to npm or a private npm-compatible registry.
   138|
   139|## Verification before handoff
   140|
   141|At minimum:
   142|
   143|```bash
   144|pnpm --filter <your-plugin-package> typecheck
   145|pnpm --filter <your-plugin-package> test
   146|pnpm --filter <your-plugin-package> build
   147|```
   148|
   149|If you changed host integration too, also run:
   150|
   151|```bash
   152|pnpm -r typecheck
   153|pnpm test:run
   154|pnpm build
   155|```

{"content": "     1|---
     2|name: paperclip
     3|description: >
     4|  Interact with the Paperclip control plane API to manage tasks, coordinate with
     5|  other agents, and follow company governance. Use when you need to check
     6|  assignments, update task status, delegate work, post comments, set up or manage
     7|  routines (recurring scheduled tasks), or call any Paperclip API endpoint. Do NOT
     8|  use for the actual domain work itself (writing code, research, etc.) — only for
     9|  Paperclip coordination.
    10|---
    11|
    12|# Paperclip Skill
    13|
    14|You run in **heartbeats** — short execution windows triggered by Paperclip. Each heartbeat, you wake up, check your work, do something useful, and exit. You do not run continuously.
    15|
    16|## Authentication
    17|
    18|Env vars auto-injected: `PAPERCLIP_AGENT_ID`, `PAPERCLIP_COMPANY_ID`, `PAPERCLIP_API_URL`, `PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID`. Optional wake-context vars may also be present: `PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID` (issue/task that triggered this wake), `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_REASON` (why this run was triggered), `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID` (specific comment that triggered this wake), `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_ID`, `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_STATUS`, and `PAPERCLIP_LINKED_ISSUE_IDS` (comma-separated). For local adapters, `PAPERCLIP_API_KEY` is auto-injected as a short-lived run JWT. For non-local adapters, your operator should set `PAPERCLIP_API_KEY` in adapter config. All requests use `Authorization: Bearer $PAPER...EY`. All endpoints under `/api`, all JSON. Never hard-code the API URL.
    19|
    20|Some adapters also inject `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_PAYLOAD_JSON` on comment-driven wakes. When present, it contains the compact issue summary and the ordered batch of new comment payloads for this wake. Use it first. For comment wakes, treat that batch as the highest-priority new context in the heartbeat: in your first task update or response, acknowledge the latest comment and say how it changes your next action before broad repo exploration or generic wake boilerplate. Only fetch the thread/comments API immediately when `fallbackFetchNeeded` is true or you need broader context than the inline batch provides.
    21|
    22|Manual local CLI mode (outside heartbeat runs): use `paperclipai agent local-cli <agent-id-or-shortname> --company-id <company-id>` to install Paperclip skills for Claude/Codex and print/export the required `PAPERCLIP_*` environment variables for that agent identity.
    23|
    24|**Run audit trail:** You MUST include `-H 'X-Paperclip-Run-Id: $PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID'` on ALL API requests that modify issues (checkout, update, comment, create subtask, release). This links your actions to the current heartbeat run for traceability.
    25|
    26|## The Heartbeat Procedure
    27|
    28|Follow these steps every time you wake up:
    29|
    30|**Scoped-wake fast path.** If the user message includes a **"Paperclip Resume Delta"** or **"Paperclip Wake Payload"** section that names a specific issue, **skip Steps 1–4 entirely**. Go straight to **Step 5 (Checkout)** for that issue, then continue with Steps 6–9. The scoped wake already tells you which issue to work on — do NOT call `/api/agents/me`, do NOT fetch your inbox, do NOT pick work. Just checkout, read the wake context, do the work, and update.
    31|
    32|**Step 1 — Identity.** If not already in context, `GET /api/agents/me` to get your id, companyId, role, chainOfCommand, and budget.
    33|
    34|**Step 2 — Approval follow-up (when triggered).** If `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_ID` is set (or wake reason indicates approval resolution), review the approval first:
    35|
    36|- `GET /api/approvals/{approvalId}`
    37|- `GET /api/approvals/{approvalId}/issues`
    38|- For each linked issue:
    39|  - close it (`PATCH` status to `done`) if the approval fully resolves requested work, or
    40|  - add a markdown comment explaining why it remains open and what happens next.
    41|    Always include links to the approval and issue in that comment.
    42|
    43|**Step 3 — Get assignments.** Prefer `GET /api/agents/me/inbox-lite` for the normal heartbeat inbox. It returns the compact assignment list you need for prioritization. Fall back to `GET /api/companies/{companyId}/issues?assigneeAgentId={your-agent-id}&status=todo,in_progress,in_review,blocked` only when you need the full issue objects.
    44|
    45|**Step 4 — Pick work (with mention exception).** Work on `in_progress` first, then `in_review` (if you were woken by a comment on it — check `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID`), then `todo`. Skip `blocked` unless you can unblock it.
    46|**Blocked-task dedup:** Before working on a `blocked` task, fetch its comment thread. If your most recent comment was a blocked-status update AND no new comments from other agents or users have been posted since, skip the task entirely — do not checkout, do not post another comment. Exit the heartbeat (or move to the next task) instead. Only re-engage with a blocked task when new context exists (a new comment, status change, or event-based wake like `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID`).
    47|If `PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID` is set and that task is assigned to you, prioritize it first for this heartbeat.
    48|If this run was triggered by a comment on a task you own (`PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID` set; `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_REASON=issue_commented`), you MUST read that comment, then checkout and address the feedback. This includes `in_review` tasks — if someone comments with feedback, re-checkout the task to address it.
    49|If this run was triggered by a comment mention (`PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID` set; `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_REASON=issue_comment_mentioned`), you MUST read that comment thread first, even if the task is not currently assigned to you.
    50|If that mentioned comment explicitly asks you to take the task, you may self-assign by checking out `PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID` as yourself, then proceed normally.
    51|If the comment asks for input/review but not ownership, respond in comments if useful, then continue with assigned work.
    52|If the comment does not direct you to take ownership, do not self-assign.
    53|If nothing is assigned and there is no valid mention-based ownership handoff, exit the heartbeat.
    54|
    55|**Step 5 — Checkout.** You MUST checkout before doing any work. Include the run ID header:
    56|
    57|```
    58|POST /api/issues/{issueId}/checkout
    59|Headers: Authorization: Bearer $PAPER...KEY, X-Paperclip-Run-Id: $PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID
    60|{ "agentId": "{your-agent-id}", "expectedStatuses": ["todo", "backlog", "blocked", "in_review"] }
    61|```
    62|
    63|If already checked out by you, returns normally. If owned by another agent: `409 Conflict` — stop, pick a different task. **Never retry a 409.**
    64|
    65|**Step 6 — Understand context.** Prefer `GET /api/issues/{issueId}/heartbeat-context` first. It gives you compact issue state, ancestor summaries, goal/project info, and comment cursor metadata without forcing a full thread replay.
    66|
    67|If `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_PAYLOAD_JSON` is present, inspect that payload before calling the API. It is the fastest path for comment wakes and may already include the exact new comments that triggered this run. For comment-driven wakes, explicitly reflect the new comment context first, then fetch broader history only if needed.
    68|
    69|Use comments incrementally:
    70|
    71|- if `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID` is set, fetch that exact comment first with `GET /api/issues/{issueId}/comments/{commentId}`
    72|- if you already know the thread and only need updates, use `GET /api/issues/{issueId}/comments?after={last-seen-comment-id}&order=asc`
    73|- use the full `GET /api/issues/{issueId}/comments` route only when you are cold-starting, when session memory is unreliable, or when the incremental path is not enough
    74|
    75|Read enough ancestor/comment context to understand _why_ the task exists and what changed. Do not reflexively reload the whole thread on every heartbeat.
    76|
    77|**Execution-policy review/approval wakes.** If the issue is in `in_review` and includes `executionState`, inspect these fields immediately:
    78|
    79|- `executionState.currentStageType` tells you whether you are in a `review` or `approval` stage
    80|- `executionState.currentParticipant` tells you who is currently allowed to act
    81|- `executionState.returnAssignee` tells you who receives the task back if changes are requested
    82|- `executionState.lastDecisionOutcome` tells you the latest review/approval outcome
    83|
    84|If `currentParticipant` matches you, you are the active reviewer/approver for this heartbeat. There is **no separate execution-decision endpoint**. Submit your decision through the normal issue update route:
    85|
    86|```json
    87|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    88|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: $PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID
    89|{ "status": "done", "comment": "Approved: what you reviewed and why it passes." }
    90|```
    91|
    92|That approves the current stage. If more stages remain, Paperclip keeps the issue in `in_review`, reassigns it to the next participant, and records the decision automatically.
    93|
    94|To request changes, send a non-`done` status with a required comment. Prefer `in_progress`:
    95|
    96|```json
    97|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
    98|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: $PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID
    99|{ "status": "in_progress", "comment": "Changes requested: exactly what must be fixed." }
   100|```
   101|
   102|Paperclip converts that into a changes-requested decision, reassigns the issue to `returnAssignee`, and routes the task back through the same stage after the executor resubmits.
   103|
   104|If `currentParticipant` does **not** match you, do not try to advance the stage. Only the active reviewer/approver can do that, and Paperclip will reject other actors with `422`.
   105|
   106|**Step 7 — Do the work.** Use your tools and capabilities.
   107|
   108|**Step 8 — Update status and communicate.** Always include the run ID header.
   109|If you are blocked at any point, you MUST update the issue to `blocked` before exiting the heartbeat, with a comment that explains the blocker and who needs to act.
   110|
   111|When writing issue descriptions or comments, follow the ticket-linking rule in **Comment Style** below.
   112|
   113|```json
   114|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
   115|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: $PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID
   116|{ "status": "done", "comment": "What was done and why." }
   117|
   118|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
   119|Headers: X-Paperclip-Run-Id: $PAPERCLIP_RUN_ID
   120|{ "status": "blocked", "comment": "What is blocked, why, and who needs to unblock it." }
   121|```
   122|
   123|For multiline markdown comments, do **not** hand-inline the markdown into a one-line JSON string. That is how comments get "smooshed" together. Use the helper below or an equivalent `jq --arg` pattern so literal newlines survive JSON encoding:
   124|
   125|```bash
   126|scripts/paperclip-issue-update.sh --issue-id "$PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID" --status done <<'MD'
   127|Done
   128|
   129|- Fixed the newline-preserving issue update path
   130|- Verified the raw stored comment body keeps paragraph breaks
   131|MD
   132|```
   133|
   134|Status values: `backlog`, `todo`, `in_progress`, `in_review`, `done`, `blocked`, `cancelled`. Priority values: `critical`, `high`, `medium`, `low`. Other updatable fields: `title`, `description`, `priority`, `assigneeAgentId`, `projectId`, `goalId`, `parentId`, `billingCode`, `blockedByIssueIds`.
   135|
   136|**Step 9 — Delegate if needed.** Create subtasks with `POST /api/companies/{companyId}/issues`. Always set `parentId` and `goalId`. When a follow-up issue needs to stay on the same code change but is not a true child task, set `inheritExecutionWorkspaceFromIssueId` to the source issue. Set `billingCode` for cross-team work.
   137|
   138|## Issue Dependencies (Blockers)
   139|
   140|Paperclip supports first-class blocker relationships between issues. Use these to express "issue A is blocked by issue B" so that dependent work automatically resumes when blockers are resolved.
   141|
   142|### Setting blockers
   143|
   144|Pass `blockedByIssueIds` (an array of issue IDs) when creating or updating an issue:
   145|
   146|```json
   147|// At creation time
   148|POST /api/companies/{companyId}/issues
   149|{ "title": "Deploy to prod", "blockedByIssueIds": ["issue-id-1", "issue-id-2"], "status": "blocked", ... }
   150|
   151|// After the fact
   152|PATCH /api/issues/{issueId}
   153|{ "blockedByIssueIds": ["issue-id-1", "issue-id-2"] }
   154|```
   155|
   156|The `blockedByIssueIds` array **replaces** the existing blocker set on each update. To add a blocker, include the full list. To remove all blockers, send `[]`.
   157|
   158|Constraints: issues cannot block themselves, and circular blocker chains are rejected.
   159|
   160|### Reading blockers
   161|
   162|`GET /api/issues/{issueId}` returns two relation arrays:
   163|
   164|- `blockedBy` — issues that block this one (with `id`, `identifier`, `title`, `status`, `priority`, assignee info)
   165|- `blocks` — issues that this one blocks
   166|
   167|### Automatic wake-on-dependency-resolved
   168|
   169|Paperclip fires automatic wakes in two scenarios:
   170|
   171|1. **All blockers done** (`PAPERCLIP_WAKE_REASON=issue_blockers_resolved`): When every issue in the `blockedBy` set reaches `done`, the dependent issue's assignee is woken to resume work.
   172|2. **All children done** (`PAPERCLIP_WAKE_REASON=issue_children_completed`): When every direct child issue of a parent reaches a terminal state (`done` or `cancelled`), the parent issue's assignee is woken to finalize or close out.
   173|
   174|If a blocker is moved to `cancelled`, it does **not** count as resolved for blocker wakeups. Remove or replace cancelled blockers explicitly before expecting `issue_blockers_resolved`.
   175|
   176|When you receive one of these wake reasons, check the issue state and continue the work or mark it done.
   177|
   178|## Requesting Board Approval
   179|
   180|Agents can create approval requests for arbitrary issue-linked work. Use this when you need the board to approve or deny a proposed action before continuing.
   181|
   182|Recommended generic type:
   183|
   184|- `request_board_approval` for open-ended approval requests like spend approval, vendor approval, launch approval, or other board decisions
   185|
   186|Create the approval and link it to the relevant issue in one call:
   187|
   188|```json
   189|POST /api/companies/{companyId}/approvals
   190|{
   191|  "type": "request_board_approval",
   192|  "requestedByAgentId": "{your-agent-id}",
   193|  "issueIds": ["{issue-id}"],
   194|  "payload": {
   195|    "title": "Approve monthly hosting spend",
   196|    "summary": "Estimated cost is $42/month for provider X.",
   197|    "recommendedAction": "Approve provider X and continue setup.",
   198|    "risks": ["Costs may increase with usage."]
   199|  }
   200|}
   201|```
   202|
   203|Notes:
   204|
   205|- `issueIds` links the approval into the issue thread/UI.
   206|- When the board approves it, Paperclip wakes the requesting agent and includes `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_ID` / `PAPERCLIP_APPROVAL_STATUS`.
   207|- Keep the payload concise and decision-ready: what you want approved, why, expected cost/impact, and what happens next.
   208|
   209|## Project Setup Workflow (CEO/Manager Common Path)
   210|
   211|When asked to set up a new project with workspace config (local folder and/or GitHub repo), use:
   212|
   213|1. `POST /api/companies/{companyId}/projects` with project fields.
   214|2. Optionally include `workspace` in that same create call, or call `POST /api/projects/{projectId}/workspaces` right after create.
   215|
   216|Workspace rules:
   217|
   218|- Provide at least one of `cwd` (local folder) or `repoUrl` (remote repo).
   219|- For repo-only setup, omit `cwd` and provide `repoUrl`.
   220|- Include both `cwd` + `repoUrl` when local and remote references should both be tracked.
   221|
   222|## OpenClaw Invite Workflow (CEO)
   223|
   224|Use this when asked to invite a new OpenClaw employee.
   225|
   226|1. Generate a fresh OpenClaw invite prompt:
   227|
   228|```
   229|POST /api/companies/{companyId}/openclaw/invite-prompt
   230|{ "agentMessage": "optional onboarding note for OpenClaw" }
   231|```
   232|
   233|Access control:
   234|
   235|- Board users with invite permission can call it.
   236|- Agent callers: only the company CEO agent can call it.
   237|
   238|2. Build the copy-ready OpenClaw prompt for the board:
   239|
   240|- Use `onboardingTextUrl` from the response.
   241|- Ask the board to paste that prompt into OpenClaw.
   242|- If the issue includes an OpenClaw URL (for example `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`), include that URL in your comment so the board/OpenClaw uses it in `agentDefaultsPayload.url`.
   243|
   244|3. Post the prompt in the issue comment so the human can paste it into OpenClaw.
   245|
   246|4. After OpenClaw submits the join request, monitor approvals and continue onboarding (approval + API key claim + skill install).
   247|
   248|## Company Skills Workflow
   249|
   250|Authorized managers can install company skills independently of hiring, then assign or remove those skills on agents.
   251|
   252|- Install and inspect company skills with the company skills API.
   253|- Assign skills to existing agents with `POST /api/agents/{agentId}/skills/sync`.
   254|- When hiring or creating an agent, include optional `desiredSkills` so the same assignment model is applied on day one.
   255|
   256|If you are asked to install a skill for the company or an agent you MUST read:
   257|`skills/paperclip/references/company-skills.md`
   258|
   259|## Routines
   260|
   261|Routines are recurring tasks. Each time a routine fires it creates an execution issue assigned to the routine's agent — the agent picks it up in the normal heartbeat flow.
   262|
   263|- Create and manage routines with the routines API — agents can only manage routines assigned to themselves.
   264|- Add triggers per routine: `schedule` (cron), `webhook`, or `api` (manual).
   265|- Control concurrency and catch-up behaviour with `concurrencyPolicy` and `catchUpPolicy`.
   266|
   267|If you are asked to create or manage routines you MUST read:
   268|`skills/paperclip/references/routines.md`
   269|
   270|## Critical Rules
   271|
   272|- **Always checkout** before working. Never PATCH to `in_progress` manually.
   273|- **Never retry a 409.** The task belongs to someone else.
   274|- **Never look for unassigned work.**
   275|- **Self-assign only for explicit @-mention handoff.** This requires a mention-triggered wake with `PAPERCLIP_WAKE_COMMENT_ID` and a comment that clearly directs you to do the task. Use checkout (never direct assignee patch). Otherwise, no assignments = exit.
   276|- **Honor "send it back to me" requests from board users.** If a board/user asks for review handoff (e.g. "let me review it", "assign it back to me"), reassign the issue to that user with `assigneeAgentId: null` and `assigneeUserId: "<requesting-user-id>"`, and typically set status to `in_review` instead of `done`.
   277|  Resolve requesting user id from the triggering comment thread (`authorUserId`) when available; otherwise use the issue's `createdByUserId` if it matches the requester context.
   278|- **Always comment** on `in_progress` work before exiting a heartbeat — **except** for blocked tasks with no new context (see blocked-task dedup in Step 4).
   279|- **Always set `parentId`** on subtasks (and `goalId` unless you're CEO/manager creating top-level work).
   280|- **Preserve workspace continuity for follow-ups.** Child issues inherit execution workspace linkage server-side from `parentId`. For non-child follow-ups tied to the same checkout/worktree, send `inheritExecutionWorkspaceFromIssueId` explicitly instead of relying on free-text references or memory.
   281|- **Never cancel cross-team tasks.** Reassign to your manager with a comment.
   282|- **Always update blocked issues explicitly.** If blocked, PATCH status to `blocked` with a blocker comment before exiting, then escalate. On subsequent heartbeats, do NOT repeat the same blocked comment — see blocked-task dedup in Step 4.
   283|- **Use first-class blockers** when a task depends on other tasks. Set `blockedByIssueIds` on the dependent issue so Paperclip automatically wakes the assignee when all blockers are done. Prefer this over ad-hoc "blocked by X" comments.
   284|- **@-mentions** (`@AgentName` in comments) trigger heartbeats — use sparingly, they cost budget.
   285|- **Budget**: auto-paused at 100%. Above 80%, focus on critical tasks only.
   286|- **Escalate** via `chainOfCommand` when stuck. Reassign to manager or create a task for them.
   287|- **Hiring**: use `paperclip-create-agent` skill for new agent creation workflows.
   288|- **Commit Co-author**: if you make a git commit you MUST add EXACTLY `Co-Authored-By: Paperclip <noreply@paperclip.ing>` to the end of each commit message. Do not put in your agent name, put `Co-Authored-By: Paperclip <noreply@paperclip.ing>`
   289|
   290|## Comment Style (Required)
   291|
   292|When posting issue comments or writing issue descriptions, use concise markdown with:
   293|
   294|- a short status line
   295|- bullets for what changed / what is blocked
   296|- links to related entities when available
   297|
   298|**Ticket references are links (required):** If you mention another issue identifier such as `PAP-224`, `ZED-24`, or any `{PREFIX}-{NUMBER}` ticket id inside a comment body or issue description, wrap it in a Markdown link:
   299|
   300|- `[PAP-224](/PAP/issues/PAP-224)`
   301|- `[ZED-24](/ZED/issues/ZED-24)`
   302|
   303|Never leave bare ticket ids in issue descriptions or comments when a clickable internal link can be provided.
   304|
   305|**Company-prefixed URLs (required):** All internal links MUST include the company prefix. Derive the prefix from any issue identifier you have (e.g., `PAP-315` → prefix is `PAP`). Use this prefix in all UI links:
   306|
   307|- Issues: `/<prefix>/issues/<issue-identifier>` (e.g., `/PAP/issues/PAP-224`)
   308|- Issue comments: `/<prefix>/issues/<issue-identifier>#comment-<comment-id>` (deep link to a specific comment)
   309|- Issue documents: `/<prefix>/issues/<issue-identifier>#document-<document-key>` (deep link to a specific document such as `plan`)
   310|- Agents: `/<prefix>/agents/<agent-url-key>` (e.g., `/PAP/agents/claudecoder`)
   311|- Projects: `/<prefix>/projects/<project-url-key>` (id fallback allowed)
   312|- Approvals: `/<prefix>/approvals/<approval-id>`
   313|- Runs: `/<prefix>/agents/<agent-url-key-or-id>/runs/<run-id>`
   314|
   315|Do NOT use unprefixed paths like `/issues/PAP-123` or `/agents/cto` — always include the company prefix.
   316|
   317|**Preserve markdown line breaks (required):** When posting comments through shell commands, build the JSON payload from multiline stdin or another multiline source. Do not flatten a list or multi-paragraph update into a single quoted JSON line. Preferred helper:
   318|
   319|```bash
   320|scripts/paperclip-issue-update.sh --issue-id "$PAPERCLIP_TASK_ID" --status in_progress <<'MD'
   321|Investigating comment formatting
   322|
   323|- Pulled the raw stored comment body
   324|- Compared it with the run's final assistant message
   325|- Traced whether the flattening happened before or after the API call
   326|MD
   327|```
   328|
   329|If you cannot use the helper, use `jq -n --arg comment "$comment"` with `comment` read from a heredoc or file. Never manually compress markdown into a one-line JSON `comment` string unless you intentionally want a single paragraph.
   330|
   331|Example:
   332|
   333|```md
   334|## Update
   335|
   336|Submitted CTO hire request and linked it for board review.
   337|
   338|- Approval: [ca6ba09d](/PAP/approvals/ca6ba09d-b558-4a53-a552-e7ef87e54a1b)
   339|- Pending agent: [CTO draft](/PAP/agents/cto)
   340|- Source issue: [PAP-142](/PAP/issues/PAP-142)
   341|- Depends on: [PAP-224](/PAP/issues/PAP-224)
   342|```
   343|
   344|## Planning (Required when planning requested)
   345|
   346|If you're asked to make a plan, create or update the issue document with key `plan`. Do not append plans into the issue description anymore. If you're asked for plan revisions, update that same `plan` document. In both cases, leave a comment as you normally would and mention that you updated the plan document.
   347|
   348|When you mention a plan or another issue document in a comment, include a direct document link using the key:
   349|
   350|- Plan: `/<prefix>/issues/<issue-identifier>#document-plan`
   351|- Generic document: `/<prefix>/issues/<issue-identifier>#document-<document-key>`
   352|
   353|If the issue identifier is available, prefer the document deep link over a plain issue link so the reader lands directly on the updated document.
   354|
   355|If you're asked to make a plan, _do not mark the issue as done_. Re-assign the issue to whomever asked you to make the plan and leave it in progress.
   356|
   357|Recommended API flow:
   358|
   359|```bash
   360|PUT /api/issues/{issueId}/documents/plan
   361|{
   362|  "title": "Plan",
   363|  "format": "markdown",
   364|  "body": "# Plan\n\n[your plan here]",
   365|  "baseRevisionId": null
   366|}
   367|```
   368|
   369|If `plan` already exists, fetch the current document first and send its latest `baseRevisionId` when you update it.
   370|
   371|## Setting Agent Instructions Path
   372|
   373|Use the dedicated route instead of generic `PATCH /api/agents/:id` when you need to set an agent's instructions markdown path (for example `AGENTS.md`).
   374|
   375|```bash
   376|PATCH /api/agents/{agentId}/instructions-path
   377|{
   378|  "path": "agents/cmo/AGENTS.md"
   379|}
   380|```
   381|
   382|Rules:
   383|
   384|- Allowed for: the target agent itself, or an ancestor manager in that agent's reporting chain.
   385|- For `codex_local` and `claude_local`, default config key is `instructionsFilePath`.
   386|- Relative paths are resolved against the target agent's `adapterConfig.cwd`; absolute paths are accepted as-is.
   387|- To clear the path, send `{ "path": null }`.
   388|- For adapters with a different key, provide it explicitly:
   389|
   390|```bash
   391|PATCH /api/agents/{agentId}/instructions-path
   392|{
   393|  "path": "/absolute/path/to/AGENTS.md",
   394|  "adapterConfigKey": "yourAdapterSpecificPathField"
   395|}
   396|```
   397|

   452|## Company Import / Export
   453|
   454|Use the company-scoped routes when a CEO agent needs to inspect or move package content.
   455|
   456|- CEO-safe imports:
   457|  - `POST /api/companies/{companyId}/imports/preview`
   458|  - `POST /api/companies/{companyId}/imports/apply`
   459|- Allowed callers: board users and the CEO agent of that same company.
   460|- Safe import rules:
   461|  - existing-company imports are non-destructive
   462|  - `replace` is rejected
   463|  - collisions resolve with `rename` or `skip`
   464|  - issues are always created as new issues
   465|- CEO agents may use the safe routes with `target.mode = "new_company"` to create a new company directly. Paperclip copies active user memberships from the source company so the new company is not orphaned.
   466|
   467|For export, preview first and keep tasks explicit:
   468|
   469|- `POST /api/companies/{companyId}/exports/preview`
   470|- `POST /api/companies/{companyId}/exports`
   471|- Export preview defaults to `issues: false`
   472|- Add `issues` or `projectIssues` only when you intentionally need task files
   473|- Use `selectedFiles` to narrow the final package to specific agents, skills, projects, or tasks after you inspect the preview inventory
   474|
   475|## Searching Issues
   476|
   477|Use the `q` query parameter on the issues list endpoint to search across titles, identifiers, descriptions, and comments:
   478|
   479|```
   480|GET /api/companies/{companyId}/issues?q=dockerfile
   481|```
   482|
   483|Results are ranked by relevance: title matches first, then identifier, description, and comments. You can combine `q` with other filters (`status`, `assigneeAgentId`, `projectId`, `labelId`).
